source.title: Capacity consolidation policy and prioritization for dedicated enterprise requests
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Enterprise customers submit dedicated capacity requests that are highly variable — many small, overlapping requests and some burst-only asks — leading to fragmented reservations, inefficient GPU allocations, and missed SLA targets. This ticket defines a consolidation policy and prioritization guidance to reduce fragmentation, improve utilization, and align reserved throughput and GPU class decisions with clear enterprise SLAs and scaling expectations.

Scope: intake time prioritization rubric, request bundling rules, GPU fitment recommendations (A/B/C bands), temporal smoothing (monthly windowing and predictable burst windows), and fallbacks when capacity or GPU class is constrained. This is product-facing guidance for intake gating and decisioning; implementation will require downstream ENG follow-up for automation and console UI changes.
acceptance_criteria: A concise policy document exists that intake reviewers can apply to aggregate compatible requests into a single reservation
A prioritized mapping from workload characteristics to GPU band recommendations (NVIDIA A100-80GB, A100-40GB, H100 equivalents), including cost/throughput heuristics
Clear SLA target buckets defined (P99 latency, throughput units, availability) and how they map to priority tiers
Temporal smoothing rules and example calendar windows that reduce burst fragmentation by >= 30% in simulation (back-of-envelope expected outcome), and a plan to measure in production
Fallback rules documented for model downgrades, cross-region routing, and burst throttling when reserved capacity thresholds are exceeded
decisions: Prioritize consolidation of requests from the same customer across products and teams when overlapping time windows and GPU band preference match
Introduce three GPU fit bands (Performance, Balanced, Cost-Optimized) with explicit throughput-per-GPU estimates for common model families
Adopt temporal smoothing: requests are bucketed into calendar months with optional burst windows (weekly heavy-use windows) to reduce per-month reservation fragmentation

If preferred GPU band not available, auto-evaluate compatible fallback bands and recommend: (a) scheduled migration, (b) temporary bilateral burst on shared pool, or (c) deploy compatible quantized model variant under an agreed degradation SLO
tradeoffs_and_risks: Consolidation improves utilization but can increase blast radius for failures; mitigation includes per-customer isolation policies and staged rollouts
Stronger smoothing reduces flexibility for customers who truly need ad-hoc spikes; offer explicit burst add-ons priced differently
GPU band heuristics are estimates and may need continuous refinement as model families and quantization options change
Automating decisions will require careful UI/UX and clear communications to avoid surprise billings or SLA changes
notes_and_activity: 2026-02-25: Initial intake — observed 14 enterprise requests in last quarter with 38% overlap in calendar windows (Ava)
2026-03-02: PM/Eng sync — proposed three GPU bands and draft throughput heuristics (Diego)
2026-03-05: Finance review — recommended separate pricing for burst windows to preserve predictability (Lina Patel)
2026-03-08: Legal asked for contract language templates for fallbacks and degraded-SLO scenarios (Marcus Cole)
2026-03-10: Updated acceptance criteria with measurable fragmentation reduction target (Diego)

Links: Confluence runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DED/Capacity+Consolidation+Runbook, Drive folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1Example, Draft PR for GPU band heuristic: https://github.com/redwood-inference/dedicated/pull/789
next_steps: Finalize consolidation policy and circulate to sales and solutions engineering for feedback by 2026-03-17
Ship intake form changes to capture preferred burst windows and model family by 2026-03-24 (ENG follow-up ticket required)
Create measurement dashboard for reservation fragmentation and utilization (Console feature request) — roll to ENG as ENG-5102
Draft contract addendum language for burst pricing and fallback SLOs with Legal — target 2026-03-31

source.title: Cycle Pulse & Echo: Capacity Canvas and Slate Shepherding
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: In high-velocity cycles we repeatedly hit two friction points: (1) teams over-commit early and then scramble to de-scope mid-cycle, and (2) platform ops can't reliably forecast burst capacity needs for Dedicated on short notice. Scope: establish a repeatable lightweight process and artifacts (Capacity Canvas + Slate Shepherd) that combine quantitative capacity budgeting with qualitative tradeoff surfaces so PMs and Eng leads can make deterministic cycle choices. Goals: reduce mid-cycle scope churn by 30%, shorten decision turnaround for staffing reallocations to <48h, and provide a 2-week predictive window for Dedicated capacity requests. Approach: introduce "Pulse" checkpoints (pre-cycle, week2 pulse, mid-cycle pulse) with clear signals and an "Echo" reserve policy (micro-credits pool + shadow roster). Deliverables: (1) Capacity Canvas template (workload-weighted token-hours, critical-path roles, cross-team friction points), (2) Slate Shepherding rubric (decomposition rules, soft/hard deltas, canary gating), (3) Runbook for triggering micro-credit allocations and emergency de-scope flows, (4) Dashboard cards and alert thresholds in Console to show capacity burn and reserve projections. Tradeoffs: simpler heuristics reduce cognitive load but may underfit highly variable infra bursts; we include a small modelling component to flag low-confidence forecasts. Stakeholders: PMs, Eng leads, SRE, People Ops for short-term staffing, Solutions for Dedicated customer communications. Success metrics: mid-cycle scope changes reduced; average time-to-allocate staff from pool; Dedicated capacity requests predicted 2 weeks ahead with >75% accuracy. This ticket covers articulation of templates, decision criteria, small pilot with two initiatives, and tooling hooks for alerts. Links to artifacts and PRs included below.
acceptance_criteria: Capacity Canvas template authored and reviewed by Eng+PM+SRE
Slate Shepherd rubric with examples for decompose/trim decisions and accepted by two initiative leads
Runbook for micro-credit allocation with owner and SLA (<48h) for staff allocation
Dashboard cards in Console prototype showing reserve burn and forecasted risk
Pilot run completed on Initiative Alpha and Initiative Beta with post-mortem and metrics
comments: 2026-02-21 - Ava Chen: Kickoff doc linked. Proposed week2 pulse to capture early velocity signals rather than waiting for mid-cycle churn. See initial Canvas draft: confluence.redwood.ai/CycleOps/Canvas-Drafts-371829

2026-02-25 - Liam O'Sullivan: Spoke with SRE (Marisol) - Dedicated capacity model can provide 10-day lookahead if we surface queue lengths and scheduled deployments. Proposal: Echo reserve set to 15% of committed GPU-hours for major initiatives and 5% for smaller slices.

2026-03-01 - Marisol Patel (SRE): Shared a quick script (GH PR: github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pulls/4256) to export queue and scheduled deploys to the Console metrics. Will pair with Data to add a 14-day forecast card.

2026-03-03 - Design review (Priya Rao): Suggested making Slate Shepherd examples explicit — include three canonical trims: (A) UX scope trim, (B) non-critical eval gating, (C) infra feature deferral. Priya added wire examples to Drive: drive.redwood.ai/designs/slate-shepherd-examples-v1

2026-03-05 - Patrick Gomez (Engineering lead, Initiative Alpha): Asked for an exception path for security-sensitive tasks that cannot be deferred. We added 'security-immovable' flag and will route those to Private deployment planning rather than de-scope.

2026-03-07 - Liam O'Sullivan: Updated the runbook with SLA for staff allocation (48h) and shadow roster contact list. Created a short rotation plan with People Ops for micro-credit pool. See People Ops play: docs.people.redwood.ai/micro-credit-rotations

2026-03-09 - Data team (Sana Irani): Built a confidence score for the 14-day capacity forecast. Initial calibration shows 68% accuracy on historical cycles — target is 75% post pilot. We'll backfill missing telemetry from older jobs to improve performance.

2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Pilot kickoff scheduled for 2026-03-16 with Initiative Alpha (customer-facing latency work) and Initiative Beta (console UX). Prep checklist completed. Noting blocker: one SRE on leave week of 2026-03-16 — contingency assigned to Marisol's backup (Daniel Kim).

source.title: Developer Onboarding Funnel & SDK Playbook — sample apps, guided flows, and measurement
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
This initiative creates a repeatable onboarding funnel and operational playbook to improve first-time SDK activation, reduce time-to-first-success, and establish measurement for SDK + docs + sample apps. Outcome: a documented playbook, 3 production-ready sample apps (Python, JS, Go), a guided 'first-call' interactive quickstart in Console, and an analytics schema to track funnel conversion and retention.

Goals / OKRs: 
- OKR 1: Increase SDK activation rate (trial accounts completing first successful API call with SDK) from 22% -> 45% in Q2.
- OKR 2: Reduce median time-to-first-success from 22 minutes -> <10 minutes.
- OKR 3: Create an evaluation signal for SDK regressions: deploy a weekly smoke test suite covering top 10 endpoints.

Scope & Deliverables (top-level):
1) SDK onboarding playbook (PM + Eng + Design): step-by-step flows for sign-up -> token -> local quickstart -> sample app deployment. Includes decision trees for private/dedicated customers.
2) Three opinionated sample apps: minimal web demo (JS), batch embed pipeline (Python), lightweight inference microservice (Go). Each with CI, Dockerfiles, and GitHub Actions publishing to packages registry.
3) Console-guided first-call flow: embedded terminal/Code Runner + copy-into-editor flow that wires API keys securely with ephemeral tokens.
4) Metrics & instrumentation: analytics schema (events + properties), dashboards for activation funnel (signup -> key creation -> sdk install -> first-call success), and retention cohorts.
5) Playbook docs: Confluence playbook + publishable docs site pages and a 'Runbook for SDK regression' for on-call.

Key decisions & trade-offs: 
- Prioritize Python and JS sample apps for GA; Go sample app scoped as alpha (fewer clicks) due to engineering bandwidth.
- Console-guided flow will use ephemeral token service (short TTL) to avoid long-lived secrets in user browsers. This requires a small infra change (ENG-3928).
- We will not attempt full interactive REPL for private/on-prem in this cycle; provide an equivalent CLI-template instead and mark as TODO in the playbook.

Progress updates (high level):
- 2026-02-20: Kickoff completed; cross-functional roster confirmed (PM, Eng SDK, Infra, Design, Docs, Analytics).
- 2026-02-28: Design delivered three initial UX mockups for the Console 'first-call' flow; reviewed with security for token design (DES-1284). Feedback: add explicit consent copy and a rotate-key CTA.
- 2026-03-04: Engineering spike completed on ephemeral token service: prototype shows acceptable latency; dependency created (ENG-3928).
- 2026-03-08: Docs team pushed initial drafts for sample app READMEs and playbook outline to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.example/display/DEVX/SDK+Onboarding+Playbook (internal).
- 2026-03-10: Began assembling analytics schema; draft event list and property definitions committed: sdk_install, sdk_import_run, first_call_success, sample_app_deploy, console_first_call_started.

Open blockers / risks: 
- Infra capacity for ephemeral token service during peak onboarding demos (risk: Console first-call latency). Mitigation: ephemeral tokens will be cached per-region for 30s and autoscaled; infra owner ENG-3928.
- Sample app packaging: single-source-of-truth for templates required to avoid divergent examples; solution: create a template repo and a thin generator (PM to review).

Success criteria & measurement: 
- Instrumentation in GA: funnels visible in LookerStudio and live dashboard within 2 weeks of sample app GA.
- Activation conversion > 40% within 6 weeks post release for newly created trial orgs.
- Time-to-first-success median < 10 minutes from sign-up for interactive guided flow.

Artifacts & links: 
- Confluence playbook (draft): https://confluence.redwood.example/display/DEVX/SDK+Onboarding+Playbook
- PR: SDK python quickstart: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/421 (internal)
- PR: Console first-call UI: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/998 (internal)
- Analytics schema draft: https://docs.redwood.example/analytics/sdk-onboarding-schema (internal)

Next milestones / timeline: 
- 2026-03-18: Complete Python sample app GA candidate + CI publishing.
- 2026-03-25: Console guided-flow beta deployed to staging + smoke-tests running.
- 2026-03-31: Publish playbook and docs site pages + begin limited beta with 50 new trial orgs.
- 2026-04-15: Evaluate metrics and iterate for broader rollout.
comments: 2026-02-21 - Diego M: Followed up with Infra on ENG-3928; they expect a small service and estimate 2 sprint work. Marking dependency.
2026-02-28 - Priya K (Design): Reviewed mockups; updated copy to call out data residency for private deployments. See DES-1284.
2026-03-04 - Samir P (SDK Eng): Prototype for ephemeral tokens looks good; latency ~40ms median on staging. Need review for threat model.
2026-03-09 - Lina H (Docs): Draft READMEs pushed. Will add quick troubleshooting section and common gotchas by 3/15.
2026-03-10 - Metrics: Draft event list uploaded. Planning a short 1-hour sync with analytics team to finalize naming conventions.

source.title: Dynamic batch-slot experiment and QSA gap analysis for per-vector pricing
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: validate a dynamic batch-slot pricing model that reduces per-vector unit cost for high-throughput customers while preserving latency SLOs and rerank quality. This ticket covers the product spec, experiment design, QSA pre-check for enterprise contract language, and the rollout gating criteria. We want to capture trade-offs (latency vs unit cost), required runtime changes, and the contractual ask for batch-discount optics in QSA/QSA addenda.
source.background: Current per-vector proposals assume static slab discounts or fixed batch-credit bundles. Sales feedback indicates customers want discounts that reflect real batch behavior (bursty vs steady) and a clear conversion between 'batch slots' and per-vector units. Engineering flagged kernel-level constraints for very large batch sizes and potential KV-cache hit regressions. Security asked for contract language clarity around retained request metadata for billing reconciliation.
source.proposal: Introduce a dynamic batch-slot meter: customers purchase 'batch slots' and receive a variable per-vector rate that scales with observed average batch occupancy over a 30-day rolling window. Experiment with two variants: (A) aggressive discount where slot occupancy > 70% yields 25% per-vector discount, (B) conservative discount with occupancy > 85% yields 15% discount. Provide an opt-in pilot with clear guardrails: latency capped to 99th percentile SLO of 600ms on CPU-backed models and automatic fallback to standard per-vector pricing if SLOs are violated for more than 6 hours in a day.
source.experiment_design: Cohorts: 50 pilot customers split across control (no change), Variant A (aggressive), Variant B (conservative). Duration: 6 weeks with 2-week ramp, 4-week measurement. Metrics windows: daily token counts, batch occupancy distribution, per-embedding cost, P50/P95/P99 latency, rerank NDCG@10 / mean reciprocal rank on canonical evaluation set, error rate. Sample size target: customers with >= 50k vectors/day to ensure statistical power. Data collection: ingest billing signals + runtime telemetry (batch occupancy histograms) into pricing-experiments dataset. Canary behavior: if latency regressions correlate with occupancy > 70% and increase error rate, immediately pause discounts for affected customers and open incident for ENG routing tuning.
source.rollout_plan: Phase 0: internal sandbox test with synthetic load and billing emulation (2 weeks). Phase 1: closed pilot with 50 customers (6 weeks experiment). Phase 2: evaluate results and prepare pricing catalog entry + contract language. Phase 3: 8-week ramp to self-serve if KPIs meet pass criteria. Rollout gates: no more than 0.5% customers experience >10% latency degradation; billing reconciliation mismatches <0.1% over 30 days; security sign-off on QSA addendum language.
source.qsa_notes: Initial QSA review surfaced three gaps: (1) audit trail for batch-slot usage needs explicit log retention and export format for customer audits; (2) data residency implications when batch aggregation occurs across regions; (3) billing reconciliation process and sample reports required for SOC2/enterprise audits. Action items: draft QSA addendum language, add fields to billing export CSV and to audit logs, propose tenant-level config to pin region for batch accounting.
comments: 2026-02-19 - Ava Chen: Kickoff: scoped to embeddings + rerank only. Eng estimate requested for telemetry changes and billing export. Scheduled QSA pre-check with Security for 2026-02-23.
2026-02-23 - Priya Narang (Security): QSA pre-check completed. See qsa_notes. Need concrete retention period and export schema before sign-off.

2026-02-25 - Ethan Morales (Eng): Runtime note: continuous batching changes required to emit per-request batch occupancy tag. Estimated 5 dev days + 2 days infra rollout. Possible performance regression at occupancy > 90% on A100 80GB under specific KV cache sizes.

2026-03-02 - Rina Patel (Finance): Modeling shows Variant A reduces ARR for a 2M token/month customer by ~14% but increases win probability for churn-risk customers. Need to create a credits cap to protect gross margin.
2026-03-05 - Design (Noor Ahmed): Added UX for console billing preview including per-day effective price, occupancy distribution graph, and 'pause discounts' toggle for customers to opt-out.
2026-03-08 - Sales (Liam O'Connor): Prelim list of 18 enterprise customers interested. Request: contractual language for 'burst credits' and invoice line-items clarity.
2026-03-10 - Mateo Singh: Updated experiment cohort selection and finalized KPI thresholds. Pushed PR for billing CSV schema. Next: onboard first 10 pilot customers and run sandbox test.

source.title: Enterprise SDK adapter matrix: streaming ergonomics & auth convergence
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
Consolidate incoming enterprise requests for language-specific SDK adapters and streaming authentication ergonomics into a prioritized, pragmatic roadmap and a repeatable triage/playbook. Customers are asking for better first‑class experience across: Java/.NET/Go/Rust bindings, resumable streaming with token rotation, client-side reconnect semantics, and enterprise auth patterns (SAML/OIDC token exchange, mTLS, client cert rotation).

Goals:
- Produce a prioritization rubric that balances enterprise security needs, integration complexity, and business impact.
- Define an adapter matrix (language x feature surface) that yields a minimal viable set of SDKs + streaming primitives for high-risk customers.
- Provide engineering-friendly acceptance criteria (API contract, test harness, telemetry) so engineering can scope and plan.
- Surface security and rollout constraints early (KMS integration, audit logs, SSO flows).

Scope & constraints:
- Non-bug feature triage only (new SDK adapters, streaming auth ergonomics, client-side reconnection/rotation).
- Focus on enterprise onramp use-cases (high-privacy customers, strict audit/KMS requirements).
- Avoid full language parity initially; emphasize adapters that unblock largest customers and platform teams.

Proposed prioritization heuristic (triage rubric highlights):
1) Security sensitivity (does customer require confidential computing, KMS/HSM integration, or strict token rotation?) – high weight
2) Expected concurrent streaming sessions and latency SLOs – medium weight
3) Customer ARR / strategic value & referenceability – medium weight
4) Implementation complexity (bindings, runtime differences, native deps) – negative weight
5) Maintenance cost (surface area, backward-compatibility, CI footprint) – negative weight

Initial recommended priorities (MVP set):
- P0 (Q2): Java SDK adapter + streaming resumable token protocol (enterprise TLS + token rotation flows) — high enterprise demand, many platform teams run on JVM stacks
- P1 (Q3): .NET SDK with explicit SSO/OIDC exchange examples and token refresh helpers
- P2 (Q3): Go SDK with lightweight streaming client and connection-retry primitives (low-latency services)
- P3 (Future): Rust binding (customer-driven) and language community contributions via adapter template

Risk & mitigations:
- Security gating may delay initial rollout -> Mitigate by designing an isolated feature flag/fallback and conducting an early security review.
- Eng cost for native dependencies -> Provide a C-core + thin language wrappers to reduce duplication.
- Breaking changes to streaming protocol -> Maintain stable handshake v1 and design v2 with opt-in semantics.

Deliverables (MVP):
- Adapter matrix doc mapping: language -> supported features (streaming resume, token rotation, mTLS, example integration)
- Triage rubric spreadsheet used to score pending requests
- API contract doc for streaming handshake & resumable sessions (example gRPC/websocket and REST fallbacks)
- Minimal Java SDK prototype with streaming resume and token refresh helpers + integration test harness
- Security checklist and audit-playbook for enterprise enablement

Success metrics (first 90 days post-launch):
- Two enterprise customers onboarded using the Java adapter with streaming resume enabled
- 50% reduction in engineering discovery time for enterprise onboarding requests (measured via intake cycle time)
- Telemetry: token rotation errors < 0.5% of streams; reconnect success rate > 98%
acceptance_criteria: Triage rubric published and used to score >20 pending requests
Adapter matrix document available in Confluence and copied to shared drive
Streaming handshake API contract (v1) documented with examples for websocket and gRPC
Java SDK prototype: streaming resume + token rotation helpers with CI tests passing
Security review completed and checklist items tracked in ticket
Runbook on migration/fallback to hosted API when private/dedicated capacity unavailable
comments: 2026-01-20 — Initial intake synthesis: compiled 42 unique customer asks (internal sheet: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ExampleIntake). Observed clusters: JVM + streaming auth, .NET SSO examples, Go microservices needing lightweight clients.

2026-02-02 — PM/Eng/Design sync: agreed to build an 'adapter matrix' artifact first to avoid ad hoc work. Action: Marcus to draft API contract and prototype scope; Leah (design) to propose developer ergonomics for reconnect flows.
2026-02-15 — Decision: prioritize Java streaming resume as MVP because (a) largest enterprise demand and (b) we can reuse existing SDK infra. Logged dependency ENG-1842 (runtime reconnect hooks).

2026-02-22 — Linked artifacts: Confluence spec (https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SDK/Adapter+Matrix+Spec), intake spreadsheet (https://drive.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Example), and Figma prototype for SDK error surface (https://www.figma.com/file/Example).

2026-03-01 — Design feedback: simplify streaming API to two primitives (openStream, resumeStream). Design raised accessibility concerns for resumable tokens — added token rotation helper to acceptance criteria.
2026-03-08 — Engineering note from Marcus: prototype will target our internal C-core + thin JVM wrapper. Requesting security kickoff to confirm KMS flows; marking security_review_required = true.

2026-03-10 — Updated prioritization and requested estimates from eng: Java (5 points), .NET (8 points), Go (3 points). Created sub-ticket plan to break prototype into SDK scaffolding + streaming core + security gating.
Follow-ups: schedule customer advisory calls with 3 representative enterprise customers to validate API ergonomics and migration concerns (assigned to Ava).

source.title: Capacity reservoir + tenant isolation controls for Dedicated clusters (PRD)
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: This PRD defines the capacity reservoir concept and tenant isolation controls for Redwood Dedicated clusters. The goal is to provide predictable reserved throughput for paying customers while enabling bounded overflow (bursts) and strong isolation guarantees to prevent noisy-neighbor and overload scenarios. The design balances utilization efficiency with SLA fidelity and operational simplicity.
source.background: Customers running Dedicated pools require predictable minimum throughput and tight isolation for compliance-sensitive workloads. Current reserved-pool behavior guarantees capacity but either wastes headroom or forces conservative sizing. We need a mechanism to reserve steady-state throughput while permitting controlled, auditable overflow into shared burst capacity; and a set of tenant isolation controls (affinity, soft/hard caps, eviction priorities) to keep tail latency SLOs intact.
goals: Provide a capacity reservoir primitive: guaranteed baseline plus configurable overflow allowance
Expose controls for tenant isolation: soft cap, hard cap, eviction priority, tenant affinity
Define deterministic overflow policies with cost attribution and telemetry
Offer safe defaults with an opt-in advanced policy surface for sophisticated customers
Minimize changes required to customer API surface and Dedicated provisioning workflows
non_goals: Implement entirely new scheduling/placement engine (we will extend existing pool scheduler)
Replace existing billing model—billing adjustments will be additive and require Revenue sign-off
Support arbitrary cross-region bursting in initial launch
user_stories: As a platform owner, I want each reserved customer to have a baseline reservoir so that critical requests have guaranteed tokens during peak usage
As a customer admin, I want an optional burst allowance to handle traffic spikes without manual capacity purchases
As an SRE on-call, I want eviction priorities and clear telemetry so I can quickly triage noisy tenant incidents
As a product manager, I want billing signals to reflect overflow usage for chargeback and fairness
requirements: REQ-1: Capacity reservoir must guarantee a customer-configurable baseline throughput (tokens/sec equivalent) on Dedicated pools
REQ-2: Each reservoir exposes a configurable burst allowance expressed in token-seconds or equivalent and a time-window smoothing (e.g., 5m/1h windows)
REQ-3: Overflow attempts beyond a customer's burst allowance must follow a deterministic policy: allow-with-cost, throttle, or reject, configurable per customer
REQ-4: Tenant isolation controls include soft cap (priority degrades), hard cap (reject), and eviction priority level (0..100)
REQ-5: The platform must surface telemetry: per-tenant reservoir fill state, burst consumption, throttle/reject counts, eviction events, and latency percentiles
REQ-6: Admins can pin affinity to specific GPU pools or permit cross-pool spillover subject to policy
REQ-7: Implementation must support live policy updates without draining the entire pool
REQ-8: All overflow/burst decisions must be logged with reason codes for auditing and billing reconciliation
api_changes: Add 'reservoir' block to Dedicated pool configuration: {baseline_tokens, burst_allowance, window_seconds, overflow_policy}
Add tenant-level controls: {soft_cap_ratio, hard_cap_tokens, eviction_priority, affinity_tags}
Add admin API endpoints: GET /pools/:id/reservoir, PATCH /pools/:id/reservoir, POST /tenants/:id/isolation-policy
Add metrics endpoint: GET /pools/:id/reservoir/metrics that returns time-series of fill and consumption
ui_changes: Console: Dedicated pool detail page shows reservoir utilization graph and per-tenant burst heatmap
Console: Tenant isolation policy editor with sliders for soft/hard caps and eviction priority
Console: Alerting UI quick-links to create alerts on reservoir fill > 80% or sustained throttle rate
data_and_metrics: SLA: P99 tail latency for baseline traffic must not increase by >10% after feature enabled
acceptance_criteria: AC-1: Create a Dedicated pool with reservoir baseline=1000 tokens/sec and burst_allowance=300k token-seconds; baseline throughput for tenant A remains available under 5x baseline simulated load
AC-2: When tenant exceeds burst allowance in simulation, behavior follows configured overflow_policy: 'allow-with-cost' charges are recorded and request succeeds
AC-3: For overflow_policy 'reject', requests above hard cap receive 429 with policy reason and are logged
AC-4: Soft cap behavior degrades priority but preserves minimal baseline; eviction events occur according to eviction_priority and are visible in logs
AC-5: Live update: changing a tenant's soft_cap_ratio takes effect within 30s without draining the pool and metrics reflect new behavior
AC-6: Telemetry surfaces per-tenant reservoir_fill_ratio and burst_consumed_tokens with <5s delay in console graphs
AC-7: End-to-end test demonstrates cost attribution: overflow_token_seconds for tenant B appear on billing reconciliation pipeline
AC-8: Security review completed and KMS-encrypted audit logs for overflow events are persisted per compliance requirements
success_metrics: Customer SLA compliance for Dedicated: reduce incidents caused by noisy neighbors by 60% in quarter following launch
Increase reserved utilization by 15% (less wasted idle capacity) across test customer fleet
<2% false-positive evictions during randomized chaos tests
Positive customer feedback in early access cohort (CSAT >= 4.0/5)
rollout_plan: Phase 0: Internal canary on staging cluster with synthetic tenants and chaos tests
Phase 1: Invite-only early access (5 customers) with opt-in flag and dedicated SRE support
Phase 2: Gradual ramp to all Dedicated customers with telemetry gating and rollback plan
Phase 3: General availability with billing integration and docs
migration_plan: Default behavior: existing pools have reservoir baseline equal to current reserved capacity and burst_allowance=0 to preserve semantics
Provide a migration tool to suggest reservoir/burst parameters based on historical token usage over 30/90 day windows
Customers can opt into suggested reservoir settings via console or API
Automated calibration (experimental) offered after 2 weeks of telemetry collection
open_issues: I-1: Kernel-level scheduling integration needed to surface per-request token accounting (eng ticket ENG-9150 proposed)
I-2: Definition of token <-> real unit mapping for multi-model workloads (latency-sensitive vs throughput-sensitive) requires alignment with ML Research
I-3: Billing product needs a schema for overflow_token_seconds and pricing guidance
I-4: Cross-pool affinity semantics for heterogeneous hardware need constraints (GPU type mismatch)
risks_and_mitigation: Risk: Overly permissive burst policies may cause tail latency spikes. Mitigation: default conservative burst_allowance and active circuit-breaker thresholds
Risk: Complexity of policy surface confuses customers. Mitigation: provide simple presets (balanced, conservative, aggressive) and one-click suggestions
Risk: Incorrect billing attribution. Mitigation: end-to-end smoke tests and separate billing pilot before GA
Risk: Live policy updates causing transient imbalances. Mitigation: staged rollout with canaries and rollback hooks
stakeholders: Eng leads: Marcus Rivera (runtime), Sofia Patel (scheduler), Daniel Wong (billing)
source.notes: This PRD intentionally separates reservoir semantics (guaranteed baseline + smoothing window) from burst policy choices to allow conservative GA defaults. We will run a 4-week early access cohort and refine pricing before general availability. Implementation should surface clear human-readable reason codes for overflow decisions to aid customer support.

source.title: Initiative Cartography: Prioritization Grid & Scoring for Roadmap Allocation
source.creator: Priya Patel
source.description: Create an explicit, multidimensional prioritization framework — called Initiative Cartography — to help PMs and GTM align roadmap bets across latency sensitivity, per-token cost impact, user value, and deployment risk. This ticket captures the scoring design, measurement plan, visualization grid, and an initial audit of 12 candidate initiatives to validate the model before broader rollout.
source.rationale: Existing single-axis or RICE-like approaches obscure important tradeoffs for inference products where latency and token economics materially change value. Initiative Cartography treats each initiative as a point in a 4-axis space and produces an actionable ordering by combining weighted distances and risk-adjusted multipliers. This yields clearer decisions between small high-value low-cost tweaks and large infrastructure investments.
scoring_method: Axes: User Value (UV: 0-10), Latency Sensitivity (LS: 0-10), Token Cost Impact (TCI: -10 to +10), Deployment Risk (DR: 0-10)
Normalize UV, LS, DR to 0-1. Normalize absolute TCI to 0-1 using max observed delta across candidates; keep sign separately.
Default weights: w_uv=0.40, w_ls=0.25, w_tci=0.20, w_dr=0.15 (configurable per cycle)
Risk adjustment: if DR>7 then multiply BaseScore by 0.7, if DR<3 multiply by 1.05
Throughput/scale multiplier: initiatives with TCI negative (cost-reducing) get +10% per unit cost bucket when expected Q1 tokens > 1B
FinalScore rounded to 2 decimals and bucketed into: Green(>=0.7), Yellow(0.4-0.7), Red(<0.4) for roadmap lanes
sample_scores: Search-relevance rerank: UV=8, LS=6, TCI=-3, DR=2 => BaseScore 0.40*0.89+0.25*0.67-0.20*0.30-0.15*0.20=0.356 => risk adj *1.05=0.374 => throughput multiplier +10% => FinalScore=0.41 (Yellow)
Dedicated autoscaler policy: UV=6, LS=9, TCI=+4, DR=5 => BaseScore 0.40*0.67+0.25*1.0-0.20*0.67-0.15*0.50=0.314 => risk adj 1 => FinalScore=0.31 (Red)
KV-prefix cache for chat sessions: UV=7, LS=8, TCI=-6, DR=3 => BaseScore 0.40*0.78+0.25*0.89-0.20*0.60-0.15*0.30=0.435 => risk adj 1.05=0.457 => throughput +20% => FinalScore=0.55 (Yellow)
decision_log: 2026-02-24: Kickoff review with Eng & Design — agreed axes and initial weights (see scoring_method). Action: PMs to map 12 candidate initiatives.
2026-03-02: Weight proposal reviewed by leadership — change request to make w_uv configurable per cycle. Action: expose weight sliders in the PRD.
2026-03-08: Data quality check — token cost deltas recalculated using last 90 days from billing dashboard (dashboard/ref: https://redwood.internal/dashboards/token-econ)
2026-03-10: In Review — design produced draft grid viz and UX for Console import. Decision: run a 2-week pilot with three squads.
comments: Ava Chen (03-01): Need clearer signal sources for Latency Sensitivity — propose using feature-level p95 increase if >5% when latency regressions occur.
Diego Morales (03-03): ENG-21456 must deliver a per-route cost estimator before we can score backlog reliably. Blocking until 03-12.
Mira Shah - Design (03-05): Draft grid looks good; request to include hover state showing contribution breakdown for each axis.
Priya Patel (03-09): Suggest adding customer-criticality tag to mark initiatives tied to enterprise SLAs.
acceptance_criteria: PRD with scoring algorithm documented and examples included (this ticket)
Prototype dashboard that plots current candidate initiatives on the 2D projection and allows weight adjustments
Pilot with 3 squads mapping 12 initiatives and producing prioritized backlog for Cycle 2026-W12
Automated data pulls for UV, TCI, and LS from agreed sources (billing, telemetry, NPS) wired to scoring script
tasks: PM: finalize axis definitions and canonical data sources (owner: Priya Patel) - doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/INIT-CARTOGRAPHY
ENG: implement per-route token cost estimator endpoint (owner: Diego Morales) - dep ENG-21456 - PR github.com/redwood/infer/PR/1234
Design: produce grid+tooltip mocks and export tokens for Console integration (owner: Mira Shah) - figma: https://www.figma.com/file/xyz/init-cartography
Metrics: create dashboard cards for UV and LS signals (owner: Omar Li) - dashboard: https://redwood.internal/dashboards/uv-ls
PM: run pilot, collect feedback, and finalize weight defaults (owner: Priya Patel)
metrics: Adoption: percent of PM squads using Initiative Cartography in cycle planning >= 60% after pilot
Signal fidelity: correlation between predicted UV and observed uplift in A/B tests >= 0.45
Economic win-rate: % of prioritized initiatives that improve cost-per-request by pipeline target when expected
Time-to-decision: median time to move initiative from discovery to committed lane <= 10 days
source.rollout_plan: Pilot with 3 product squads for two cycles, collect results and tune weights, then ship a Console import button and weight presets for Growth, Reliability, and Enterprise lanes. Post-launch: monitor metrics for 4 cycles and collect qualitative feedback via retro.
next_steps: Complete ENG-21456 and validate cost estimates (owner: Diego Morales)
Design QA: finalize tooltip breakdown and accessibility checks (owner: Mira Shah)
Pilot kickoff meeting with squads and Engineering (target: 2026-03-15)
Automate scoring script and add to PM tooling repo for iteration (owner: Priya Patel)

source.title: Model Terrain Pulse — procurement trigger experiments & cohort signal playbook
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: produce an operational playbook and prototype dashboards that translate continuous market signals (model capability, latency & pricing shifts, regional availability) into deterministic procurement triggers and prioritized follow-up work.

Why now: sales + customer success reported three recent prospect losses due to sudden provider availability and pricing swings. We need a repeatable, low-friction system to detect when a supplier/model requires procurement action (e.g., reserved capacity, switch, or contract negotiation).

Scope / Deliverables:
- Signal taxonomy & scoring rubric: define 8 signals (availability, price delta, latency delta, quantization support, license change, region outage, API contract mismatch, empirical quality regression) and an algorithmic score that maps to actions (monitor, engage partner, escalate to sales).
- Cohort mapping: categorize customers/workloads into 4 procurement cohorts (latency-sensitive, cost-sensitive, regulated, edge/RAG).
- Prototype dashboard spec: time-series & event overlay, alert thresholds, suggested remediation steps, and a sample procurement-execution checklist. Link to spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/ModelTerrainPulseSpec
- Synthetic workload tests: generate three workload profiles and run model substitution experiments to estimate expected customer impact and unit-cost delta. Results to be published as CSV and a short report.
- Operational runbook: PR trigger flows, Slack alert format, escalation owners, and handoff to sales/solutions.

Acceptance criteria:
- Scoring rubric documented and reviewed by PM, Eng, and Sales (sign-off in comments).
- Prototype dashboard mocked and validated with Design.
- Signal ingestion pipeline MVP ingesting at least two telemetry sources (model catalog updates + cloud price feeds).
- Three synthetic workload substitution experiments completed with baseline delta table.

Success metrics (first 90 days):
- Time-to-detect for supply shocks less than 6 hours (from feed -> alert) for signals with high confidence.
- At least one procurement action that avoids a customer-facing degradation (tracked in CRM).
- False positive alert rate < 15% for production customers in the pilot cohort.

Plan & milestones:
- Week 1 (2026-02-25 to 2026-03-03): finalize signals and cohorts; align with Sales and Infra (completed).
- Week 2: dashboard spec + design mockups; telemetry mapping (in progress).
- Week 3: MVP ingestion + scoring engine prototype; run 3 synthetic experiments (blocked on ENG-22344 GPU quota request).
- Week 4: pilot run with 5 customers in the regulated cohort; collect feedback and runbook iteration.

Risks & trade-offs:
- Signal noise: model capability changes can be noisy; to reduce churn we will require multi-source confirmation before triggering high-severity actions.
- Telemetry gaps: some providers don't publish timely price/region feeds — fallback to empirical probes (synthetic workloads) increases cost.
- Procurement latency: operational buy-in is required; the playbook must be simple and prescriptive to be effective.

Data sources and artifacts:
- Model catalog changefeed (internal Kafka topic: models.catalog.changes)
- Public cloud price feeds (GCP/AWS/Azure snapshots)
- Synthetic workload harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-terrain-harness (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-terrain-harness/pull/42)
- Dashboard mock: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/ModelTerrainPulseMocks

Stakeholders:
- Product: Ava Chen (owner), Liam O'Rourke (PM lead)
- Eng: Priya Kapoor (runtime), Marcos Silva (telemetry)
- Design: Zoe Martinez (dashboards)
- Sales/Ops: Nora Patel (enterprise sales), Diego Ramos (solutions)

Next steps for assignee:
1) Finalize telemetry schema with Marcos by 2026-03-12.
2) Book design sync with Zoe and provide sample data.
3) Coordinate GPU quota request resolution for synthetic tests with Infra (ENG-22344).

Notes: this ticket focuses on productizing the detection + recommendation layer; actual procurement execution is out of scope and will be handed to Sales/Ops via the runbook.
comments: 2026-02-26 — Nora Patel (Sales): We lost a POC last week because a fallback model had a 3x latency increase in apac-west. Happy to help provide customer examples for the pilot.

2026-03-01 — Priya Kapoor (Eng): Telemetry ingestion can start with model-catalog changefeeds and public price snapshots. Empirical probes for availability will need scheduled workers — adding ENG-22344 to dependencies.

2026-03-03 — Zoe Martinez (Design): Dashboard mock posted; recommendation: compress the action panel to three prioritized remedies to avoid analyst fatigue. Refer to mock: Drive/ModelTerrainPulseMocks/overview.png
2026-03-05 — Liam O'Rourke (PM): Sync with Sales scheduled 2026-03-07. Goal of that sync: agree pilot customer list and SLA for alerts.

2026-03-08 — Marcos Silva (Eng): Quick note — public cloud price feeds vary in cadence; we'll normalize to hourly snapshots for the MVP. This may increase detection latency for short-lived price shocks.
2026-03-09 — Ava Chen (PM): Updated acceptance criteria to require multi-source confirmation for high-severity triggers (catalog change + empirical probe or price delta).

source.title: Field Alpha Probe Program: model liveness & sourcing cost tracker
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Objective: run a lightweight, repeatable "alpha probe" program to validate model liveness, true-to-production sourcing cost, and procurement friction signals for 6 target open and partner models across regulated verticals (finserv, healthcare, legal).

Why: existing market scans surface candidate models but do not capture operational liveness (how often binaries/weights are pulled/updated or deprecated), procurement latency (time to license/confirm usage terms), nor real per-customer cost to serve under production-like token distributions. These signals materially affect fallback strategy and Dedicated capacity commitments.

Scope and deliverables: compact playbook and dataset: 1) A runbook for a 6-week alpha probe executed by PM + Solutions Eng + Procurement; 2) A reproducible telemetry harness that measures: liveness (availability checks, patch cadence), provisioning latency (time-to-get-keys/contracts), cost-to-serve estimates (token-based simulation using our latency/cost profiles), licensing risk notes, and model-quality quick-checks on a 200-case prompt corpus per vertical; 3) A scoring rubric (LivenessScore, ProcurementLagDays, ServeCostUSD_per_month_estimate, QualityDelta) to feed the model-supply dashboard; 4) A short go/no-go recommendation template for whether to onboard or keep as fallback.

Method: use a mix of synthetic traffic (to emulate 95/99p seq lengths), partner pilot calls (where allowed), and public artifact monitoring (GitHub, model hub changelogs). Coordinate procurement to attempt minimal license agreements in parallel to capture real legal/ops timings.

Key success metrics: automated liveness checks run weekly with >= 5 data points per model; procurement latency measured end-to-end for at least 4 models; cost simulation validated against one Dedicated pilot; rubric yields actionable binary go/no-go for 75% of probed models.

Acceptance criteria: (1) Playbook published to Confluence with run steps + ownership; (2) Telemetry repo with scripts and synthetic workload definition merged to GitHub; (3) Dashboard tile in supply-monitor shows the four rubric signals for all 6 models; (4) One pilot procurement completes during the alpha window and documents negotiation blockers.

Notes on constraints and trade-offs: we will not attempt full-scale quality evaluations (LLM fine-grained metrics) in this alpha — that is a separate eval effort. We must balance synthetic workload realism vs. cost: prefer sampled real customer prompts when partner pilots are not available. Procurement experiments may be blocked in some regions — log as data point rather than blocker.
updates: 2026-03-02 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes posted. Agreed list of candidate models (3 open-source, 3 partner). Action: Solutions Eng to produce synthetic workload generator by 2026-03-06.

2026-03-04 - Diego Martinez: Telemetry repo created (github.com/redwood-inference/supply-alpha-probe). Initial liveness probe script (ping + checksum) implemented. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/supply-alpha-probe/pull/4

2026-03-05 - Priya Reddy (Procurement): Procurement will attempt MSA-lite for two partner models; expect first response window 7-10 business days. Noted potential legal redlines for export control in model A.

2026-03-06 - Solutions Eng (Liam Park): Synthetic workload generator ready; includes seq-length distribution profiles derived from console-2025.02 token histograms. Running baseline cost simulation for all models; initial results show one open model has 2-3x higher token cost in tail scenarios.
2026-03-07 - Diego Martinez: Dashboard proto tile added to staging (grafana link). Need mapping from our ServeCost USD estimate to Console billing meter names — flagged to Billing team (ENG-1842).

2026-03-08 - Ava Chen: Consolidated alpha playbook draft pushed to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SUPPLY/Alpha+Probe+Playbook. Next: capture procurement latency in a dedicated sheet and run first weekly liveness sweep.
comments: Priya Reddy (Procurement) - 2026-03-03: We should treat partner model procurement as a separate experiment; legal needs to record any interface limitations to ensure we don't promise long-term support.
Liam Park (Solutions Eng) - 2026-03-04: Trade-off called out: deeper quality evals would require curated human labels and add ~2 weeks; for alpha we surface a lightweight 200-case pass/fail corpus.
Marta Gomes (Research) - 2026-03-05: Recommend adding a 'deprecation cadence' signal by scraping release tags and commit frequency — will add a small scraper to the telemetry harness.
Elliot Shaw (Sales) - 2026-03-06: Capture buyer sentiment by tagging any procurement friction notes with 'deal risk' so AE can preempt escalations.

Telemetry repo merged with scripts for liveness, procurement latency capture, and cost simulation
Dashboard tile showing LivenessScore, ProcurementLagDays, ServeCostUSD_estimate, QualityDelta for all 6 models
At least one procurement completed with documented blocker summary

source.title: Quarter orchestration: hosted API + model-fleet sprint conductor
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description: Coordinate quarterly roadmap commitments for Hosted API and the Model Catalog to align OKRs, reduce cross-team dependency risk, and create a lightweight scheduling conductor that automates canary windows and rollout lanes. The goal is a single runnable sprint plan that maps feature deliverables, model onboarding milestones, canary schedules, and capacity hedges so engineering and infra teams can lock resource commits earlier.
acceptance_criteria: Consolidated quarterly roadmap document linking each hosted-api feature to the model(s) it depends on
A dependency heatmap with owners and mitigation plans for top 10 risks
Automated canary schedule template that SlotOps can consume for Dedicated and Hosted deployments
Cross-team sign-off from Eng, Research, Design, and Infra on rollout windows and capacity hedges
Updated OKR mapping in the central tracker with measurable KPIs and owners
outcomes: Reduced last-minute model-compatibility rollbacks by target 40%
Faster sign-off cycle for canary windows (target 4 days average)
Clear unit-cost / capacity trade-off documented per initiative
updates: 2026-01-12 - Maya Chen created ticket. Draft scope and initial stakeholders list added. Noted high-risk dependencies on ENG-5231 (KV cache refactor) and DES-882 (canary UX changes).

2026-01-20 - Kickoff w/ Eng, Research, Infra. Decision: treat model-onboarding tasks as first-class schedule items. Action: ENG to produce compatibility matrix by 2026-02-05. Link: https://confluence.redwood/roadmaps/quarter-conductor (internal).

2026-02-02 - Received preliminary compatibility matrix from ENG-5231 owner. Found 3 models with potential latency regressions under our default batching profile. Trade-off discussion: delay two hosted features vs ship with reduced batch sizes. Recorded pros/cons in doc and flagged to PM triage.
2026-02-08 - Design review: DES proposed a minimal canary dashboard pattern to show per-model latency/cost delta. DES-882 will prototype. Decision: prioritize visibility over feature polish for Q1.

2026-02-14 - Risk heatmap draft completed. Top risks: model-quantization regressions (high), infra quota conflicts during peak (medium), cross-region rollout sequencing (medium). Mitigations assigned with owners. Link to heatmap: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/quarter-heatmap
2026-02-21 - Sprint conductor spec v0.3 created. It defines canary windows, rollback criteria, and autoscaling reserve tiers. Shared with Ops for feedback.

2026-02-25 - Ops feedback: reserve tier 2 (5% capacity) unacceptable for Dedicated customers without contract change. Action: add tier toggle in conductor and notify sales for communication. Added to dependencies: PM-100346 (billing notice).

2026-03-01 - Evals team reported a regression on semantic ranking for model-X when run with quantized kernels. Decision: introduce a gated canary that runs ranking evals alongside traffic for 48h before ramp. Sub-issue: PM-100347 created to own eval gating automation.

2026-03-03 - Cross-functional sync: Accepted 3-week canary cadence for weighty model swaps. Agreed on metrics to track: 95th pct latency, tail error rate, embedding-distance drift for selected prompts. Dashboard prototype: https://grafana.redwood/hostedapi/canary-dash

2026-03-05 - Draft roadmap published to stakeholders for review (link in Confluence). Next steps: finalize timelines and secure capacity commitments from Infra by 2026-03-12. Assigned to assignee Ethan Park.

Lock canary window templates and produce SlotOps runbook
Coordinate messaging with Sales for Dedicated customers affected by reserve tiers
Complete automation for eval-gated canaries (PM-100347)
Run tabletop exercise for a model rollback scenario with Eng and Ops by 2026-03-15

source.title: Onboarding playbook for experimental open models with compatibility matrix
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Clarify acceptance criteria and execution steps for onboarding new open-source model variants into the Redwood model catalog. This ticket defines scope, required compatibility metadata, UI and API surface decisions, and acceptance tests for experimental models with nonstandard kernels or quantization. The goal is a repeatable playbook that reduces release friction and avoids runtime regressions when we expose community models to hosted and dedicated customers.
source.background: We regularly receive requests to add community/open variants (quantized forks, LoRA-tuned checkpoints, trimmed tokenizers). Existing onboarding guidance is fragmented and often misses runtime compatibility signals that cause inference failures or silent quality regressions. Previous PM tickets addressed pinning and fallback lists, but we need an operational playbook that ties catalog metadata, runtime tests, and catalog UX together so that engineers and model-scoping reviewers can make consistent decisions.
source.scope: In scope: intake checklist, required metadata fields (compatibility tags), deterministic acceptance tests, UI labels for 'experimental' vs 'production-ready', API flags for pinning behavior, and rollout gating steps. Out of scope: automated model conversion tooling and on-prem installation packaging (handled by infra teams).
source.non_goals: We will not attempt to auto-quantize or automatically convert community checkpoints as part of this playbook. We will not change pricing or SLAs for models marked experimental in this ticket.
requirements: Create a standard compatibility vocabulary (list of tags) that all new entries must include: runtime_compatibility, supported_quantizations, max_context_len, kv_cache_semantics, deterministic_kv_hash, device_support (e.g., cuda/rocm/cpu), tokenizer_compatibility

Define pinning semantics: allow pin by major, major.minor, or full-checksum. Default catalog behavior for open variants is major.minor pin when provided; allow explicit full pin via API/UI for Dedicated and Private customers.
Add 'experimental' and 'community-verified' UI badges with copy and tooltip text for the Console and model catalog API responses.

Require an automated runtime compatibility test suite (skeleton) to run during onboarding: smoke generation, tokenization roundtrip, kv-cache fetch/store test, numeric stability fuzz test at two sequence lengths.

Define acceptance metadata: at least one model artifact URL, declared license, owner contact, test artifacts (seed prompts and expected tokens for smoke test), and a 'compatibility note' free-text field for edge cases.
Document rollback/fallback policy: catalog must specify fallback model keys (one or more) used by the routing system when the onboarding variant fails request validation or runtime checks.
Expose API param allow_variant_fallback and behavior: if true, routing will select a compatible variant automatically; if false, requests error for incompatible variants.
Add a lightweight lint step during intake that flags missing compatibility tags and potential conflicts with existing pinned customers.
Provide migration guidance for existing catalog entries that lack explicit compatibility metadata (backfill plan).
Acceptance tests must run against hosted runtime and a dedicated-simulated environment to surface differences in kernel selection or quantization failures.
Prepare short PRD snippets for partner/customer-facing docs describing what 'pinned' means for open models and the implications for upgrades/patches.
acceptance_criteria: A canonical compatibility tag list is published in the model-catalog docs and referenced by the Console onboarding flow
The Console shows a distinct experimental badge and tooltip for any model marked experimental via metadata; QA validates tooltip wording
Onboarding flow requires all required metadata fields; intake linter rejects submissions missing runtime_compatibility or license
Automated runtime checks run as part of the onboarding job and produce a pass/fail report stored in the catalog entry
Pinning options (major, major.minor, full-checksum) are accepted by the catalog API and respected by routing in a test harness
Fallbacks are used automatically in the dedicated-simulated environment when a model fails request validation, consistent with the documented policy
A sample of three community models are onboarded using the playbook and pass both hosted and dedicated-simulated acceptance tests
Backfill plan has been created with a prioritized list of existing entries missing compatibility metadata and a schedule for tagging
Engineering review and signoff (comments + approval) are captured on the PR that implements the metadata schema and lint
Product & Security have reviewed and acknowledged the experimental badge copy and the API opt-in behavior for allow_variant_fallback
source.implementation_notes: Work will be coordinated with Runtime (ENG) for the test harness and with Console (ENG/Design) for the badge and tooltip. Keep tagging schema intentionally minimal to reduce friction; prefer enumerated tags with 'other' free text. The lint should be a pre-merge GitHub Action against model metadata YAML/JSON. For pinning semantics, prefer semantic-major.minor behavior for default unless full-checksum is explicitly provided. Ensure telemetry events are emitted for 'onboard_attempt', 'onboard_pass', 'onboard_fail', and 'pin_change' with model_key and runtime_fingerprint.
rollout_plan: Phase 0: Publish docs and compatibility tag list; implement lint and metadata schema (week 1-2)
Phase 1: Console badge + API support for experimental flag + basic acceptance tests wired to onboarding job (week 3)
Phase 2: Integrate pinning semantics in routing and add fallback test harness for dedicated simulation (week 4)
Phase 3: Backfill top 25 catalog entries lacking metadata and onboard 3 community models as pilots (week 5)
Phase 4: Open intake to internal teams and observe telemetry for 2 weeks before broad public acceptance (week 6)
Post-rollout: Retrospective and follow-up tickets for automation gaps discovered during pilots
risks_mitigation: Risk: Incomplete metadata leads to silent runtime failures. Mitigation: Lint and mandatory runtime checks before promotion.
Risk: Customers misuse 'experimental' models in prod. Mitigation: prominent badges, clear Console copy, and opt-in fallback API behavior for Dedicated.
Risk: Pinning semantics confuse customers. Mitigation: explicit API docs, examples, and UI chooser with explanation and confirmation step.
open_questions: Should we expose 'runtime_fingerprint' to end customers or keep it internal?
What is the minimum passing threshold for numeric stability fuzz tests? (ENG input required)
Do we allow community models to opt into automatic quantization conversions in future (separate project)?
source.notes: This ticket consolidates prior PM discussions about pinning and open-model onboarding into a single operational playbook. Follow-up tickets will break out the runtime test CI work and Console UX implementation. Engage a runtime SME (e.g., Marcus Rivera) early for numeric-stability thresholds.

source.title: Volume-tier onboarding and special pricing request policy
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description: Define and operationalize an end-to-end policy for onboarding new volume tiers into the public pricebook and handling Special Pricing Requests (SPRs) from Sales. This ticket covers approval gates, required artifacts (SPR form, deal economics worksheet), pricebook versioning, audit trail requirements, and integration points with Security Questionnaire reviews when non-standard deployment or data controls are requested.
acceptance_criteria: A single-step SPR form template exists and is stored in Confluence + a link is present in the Sales playbook
Approval matrix implemented: discounts up to 20% can be approved by Sales Director, 20-40% requires RevOps + VP Commercial approval, >40% requires CRO sign-off and legal review
Pricebook update process documented: changes go through draft -> review -> publish with semantic versioning (e.g., pricebook-v2.3)
Every pricebook change is accompanied by a GitHub PR linking to the SPR and a migration script where necessary
Security Questionnaire checks are triggered for any deal that requests Private/VPC deployments, customer-managed keys, or cross-border data exceptions
Rollback and emergency discount revocation runbook exists and is tested once in staging
implementation_steps: Create SPR form in Confluence and add to Sales toolkit
Publish approval matrix and map approvers to Linear/Slack groups
Add a pricebook versioning guideline and a standard PR template in repo infra/pricebook
Add automated checks in CI to ensure PR references the SPR id and Confluence link
Add Security Questionnaire trigger rule into RevOps intake flow for specific deployment flags
Run one shadow pricebook update in staging and record timing/rollbacks
rollback_plan: Revert pricebook PR and publish previous semantic version
Notify all routes in Redwood Console about rollback via rollout dashboard
Issue customer communications template (Sales Ops + CS) for affected customers
Trigger postmortem and log audit entries for the reversal
decisions: Use a threshold-based approval matrix (percent discount + TCV bands) instead of deal-by-deal manager discretion
Require SPR and an economic worksheet for any deviation from published pricebook
Integrate security questionnaire gating for specific technical terms (on-prem, VPC, KMS) to RevOps review step

source.title: Retention & Revalidation Policy for Redwood-Verified Performance Profiles
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: As Redwood’s catalog of verified performance profiles grows, we need a clear lifecycle policy that balances signal freshness, operational cost, and compatibility with model pinning. This ticket defines retention windows, automated revalidation cadence, archival behavior, and the UX/notification flow for profile staleness and republishing. The goal is to ensure customers relying on Redwood-verified latency/throughput/cost numbers have up-to-date guidance while limiting unnecessary rebenching costs.
acceptance_criteria: Define retention windows and staleness thresholds for verified profiles (active, warning, archived)
Implement automated revalidation job that runs lightweight smoke benchmarks against profile samples on a configurable cadence
Console shows profile age, staleness state, last-validated timestamp, and a suggested action (revalidate, archive, mark-for-review)
Notification pipeline that alerts model owners and affected customers when a profile goes from active -> warning or when revalidation fails
Migration guidance for pinned model versions: if a profile is archived or significantly regresses, provide automatic pin-migration suggestions and a rollback path
Measure revalidation cost delta and ensure revalidation budget is tracked in Optimize dashboard
notes_or_decisions: Retention policy proposal: active=90 days since last full validation, warning=90-180 days (show 'stale' badge), archived>180 days (hidden from primary catalog)
Revalidation cadence: lightweight daily sampling for high-traffic models, 30-day full revalidation for catalog models, configurable per-project in Dedicated/Private
Regression thresholds: latency >5% or cost per token >10% relative to published profile triggers manual review; throughput drop >10% triggers immediate investigation
Revalidation implementation will reuse ENG's verified-benchmark-pipeline (PM-6342) and add a smaller smoke harness for cadence runs
Profiles remain immutable once published; republish creates a new profile version and the console links old -> new. Auto-migration suggested but requires owner approval for pinned models
comments: 2026-02-16 - Kickoff: Sync with ENG (Diego, Priya), SRE (Marco). Action: ENG to prototype revalidation job; PM to draft retention thresholds.
2026-02-22 - ENG update: Prototype smoke harness pushed to branch feature/profile-reval; cost estimate ~12 GPU-hours/week for catalog of 120 models. See PR #432.
2026-02-24 - UX draft: Ava uploaded two mockups for profile staleness states and migration modal (Drive link). Asked for accessibility review.
2026-03-01 - Security note: KMS access required for private benchmarking in VPCs; security sign-off path added (ENG-3312).
2026-03-03 - Decision: Use existing profile schema with extra metadata fields last_validated, validation_method, validation_cost_usd. ENG to add field migration script.

2026-03-05 - PM note: Updated acceptance criteria to include notification and pin-migration suggestions. Blockers: scheduling window for full revalidations during low-traffic hours. ENG investigating.
source.implementation_plan: Phase 1: Define thresholds and add profile metadata fields (ENG). Phase 2: Build smoke revalidation job and pipeline integration with verified-benchmarks. Phase 3: Console UI changes + notification integration. Phase 4: Gradual rollout, monitor revalidation cost and customer feedback.
source.risks_and_mitigations: Risk: repeated revalidations increase infra costs. Mitigation: tiered cadence (sample vs full) and cost cap per project. Risk: false positives from noisy samples. Mitigation: require regression to be observed in two consecutive runs before triggering owner notification.
source.test_plan: Unit tests for metadata migration, end-to-end revalidation run in staging that compares baseline profile values, canary release for console UI to 10% of catalog owners, smoke tests to verify notification delivery.
source.estimated_cost_notes: Initial weekly smoke runs estimated at ~12 GPU-hours/week for current catalog; full revalidation of 120 models estimated ~180 GPU-hours/month. Optimize to propose batching/quantization to reduce costs.

source.title: Trajectory & Autoscale Experiments Cadence — Quarterly Roadmap and Runbook
source.creator: Ethan Park
source.description: This ticket defines the Q2 cadence and prioritized slate of experiments for improving Dedicated capacity economics via adaptive autoscale and predictive trajectory windows. Goal is to produce a 10-week experimentation plan plus an operations runbook that can be handed to Capacity Ops and SRE for execution and to link clear OKRs and success metrics back to billing and SLOs. This initiative should align with Console signals (cost per token, prewarm hit-rate) and Runtime telemetry (cold-start latency, queue depth).
goals: Define three priority experiment families: predictive prewarm windows, reservoir smoothing, and demand-driven burst lanes
Deliver an operations runbook for canarying autoscale changes with rollback criteria
Ship telemetry dashboards that map experiment variants to unit-economics (cost/token) and tail-latency SLOs
Tie experiments to two concrete OKRs: reduce median cost/token by 6% and maintain 99.9% 95th-percentile latency
milestones: M1 (2026-03-10): Finalize experiment hypotheses & instrumentation plan
M2 (2026-03-24): Implement feature flags + metric hooks in runtime and console
M3 (2026-04-07): Start staggered canary runs for family A (predictive prewarm)
M4 (2026-04-28): Analyze first 2-week results & decide go/no-go for scaling to additional pools
M5 (2026-05-12): Publish runbook + retrospective and hand off to Capacity Ops
acceptance_criteria: Experiment hypotheses documented and approved by PM + Eng
Instrumentation captures cost/token, prewarm hit-rate, cold-start latency, queue depth and per-route SLOs
Canary gating rules implemented (automatic rollback on >1.5x SLO violation or >12% cost uplift)
Dashboards show experiment variant breakdowns and cost uplift estimates
Runbook contains step-by-step rollout, rollback, owner list, and post-mortem template
decisions: Use feature-flag-based rollout per dedicated-pool to avoid broad blast radius
Prioritize prewarm window experiments because historical telemetry shows 60% of cold-start cost concentrated in bursty 1% of time
Limit concurrent experiment families to two live variants per pool to reduce noise in analysis
risks: Telemetry noise due to simultaneous customer traffic pattern shifts (mitigation: staggered canaries and control groups)
Cost regressions during experiments (mitigation: hard cost gates + automatic rollback)
Operational load on Capacity Ops during rollouts (mitigation: staffing rota + runbook checklists)
Model-level latency regressions if prewarm heuristics misfire (mitigation: route-level SLO throttles)
comments: 2026-02-20 Ethan Park: Drafted initial plan after sync with Runtime and Capacity teams. Focusing scope to dedicated pools with highest burst variance.
2026-02-26 Marina Castillo: Added OKR mapping and suggested canary gating thresholds. Requesting Eng estimate for instrumentation changes.
2026-03-02 Nadia Gupta (Engineering): Eng spike completed — instrumentation will add two counters and one histogram. Estimate increased by 2 points due to per-pool feature flagging complexity.
2026-03-04 Ops rotation: Noted potential staffing conflict week of 2026-04-07; recommend shifting M3 by one week if unresolved.
notes: Analysis plan: use lift / control comparison and bootstrap confidence intervals for cost/token reductions
Fallback policy: for any variant exceeding cost or latency gates, automatic fallback to current baseline within 10 minutes
Experimentation play: each family has a predefined sample size and minimum observation window (48 hours for initial signals, 2 weeks for rollout decision)

source.title: Release-train comms, ops runbook, and release-notes digest for API/Console/Runtime
source.creator: Ethan Alvarez
source.description:
Summary: Coordinate a single, consolidated release-train communications and operational runbook that covers milestones, scope-lock signals, cut criteria, release-notes generation, and GTM tasks across API, Console, and Runtime teams. Goal is to reduce ambiguity about last-minute scope decisions, automate a digest for release notes, and codify a lightweight hypercare plan for the first 72 hours post-rollout.

Background: Recent rollouts had fragmented comms between runtime and console teams resulting in mismatched docs, late bugfix rollouts, and duplicated rollback efforts. This initiative centralizes cutline decisions and creates an automated release-notes digest that compiles: PRs merged after freeze, critical known issues, upgrade notes, and per-team owner blurbs.

Scope:
- Milestone calendar and signoff checklist for MAJOR, MINOR, and PATCH release lanes.
- Cut criteria matrix (functional, perf, security, docs) and who has veto authority.
- Release-notes digest template and automation spec (GitHub action + Confluence integration).
- Communication matrix with channel templates (Slack, email, release board) and stakeholders.
- 72-hour hypercare plan including on-call rotation, SLA escalations, and rollback playbook.
- Postmortem and evaluation plan for quality/regression metrics tied to the release.

Out of scope: Full localization of release notes (will be addressed as a follow-up), long-form marketing collateral, and marketplace listings updates.
acceptance_criteria: Published runbook in Confluence at /release-train/q1-2026/runbook with signoff table for PM/Eng/SRE/Design/Legal
Automated release-notes digest deployed as GitHub Action that produces a Markdown draft and Confluence page on merge-to-release-branch
Cut criteria matrix agreed and recorded with named approvers for each criteria (functional, perf, security, docs)
Communication matrix templates (pre-announce, go/no-go, rollback, postmortem) available and rehearsed in a dry-run
72-hour hypercare rota created and paged to SRE/Runtime/Console on-call with escalation thresholds documented
tasks: Draft milestone calendar and signoff gates (owner: Priya) — due 2026-02-25
Create cut criteria matrix and map veto owners (owner: Ethan) — due 2026-02-27
Design release-notes digest schema and GitHub Action spec (owner: Eng infra) — due 2026-02-28
Confluence runbook skeleton with links and templates (owner: DocOps) — due 2026-03-01
Legal/security signoff checklist added to runbook (owner: Benji Rao, Security) — due 2026-03-03
Dry-run communication rehearsal with on-call and PMs (owner: Priya) — scheduled 2026-03-05
Finalize hypercare rota and escalation paths (owner: SRE lead) — due 2026-03-07
Enable GitHub Action on release branch and verify draft output (owner: ENG-release) — due 2026-03-09
comments: 2026-02-21 — Ethan Alvarez: Initial draft posted. Need ENG signoff on automation feasibility and a rough estimate from infra for the GH Action work.
2026-02-22 — Maya Chen (Eng): Confirmed GH Action is feasible; infra estimate 3d to implement and 1d to test. Raised a minor risk on permissioning to post to Confluence.
2026-02-23 — Benji Rao (Security): Reviewed cut criteria; security veto will trigger immediate rollback unless mitigations are in production. Requesting list of security owners for each component.
2026-02-24 — Priya Natarajan (PM): Scheduled communication dry-run on 2026-03-05 and added Design to the announcement approvals loop. Added note: docs-freeze at T-48 hours for localized strings.
2026-02-26 — Fiona Park (Design): Provided release banner copy and localization constraints. Asked for a clear fallback if localized strings miss the docs-freeze cutoff.
2026-02-28 — ENG-release (Lead): GH Action prototype created in branch release/train-2026-03; log shows merged PR aggregation working, needs Confluence write token from Platform team.
2026-03-01 — Priya Natarajan: Runbook draft published to Confluence. Requesting final approvals from SRE and Legal by 2026-03-03. Marking security_review_required = true.
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Late merged PRs after docs-freeze can create inconsistent release notes and user confusion. Mitigation: GH Action flags post-freeze merges and posts a highlighted section in the digest and triggers a Slack alert to owners.

Risk: Confluence write permissions may block automation. Mitigation: Platform will provide a service account token scoped to a single release namespace; fall back to generating a draft Markdown in PR if token is unavailable.
Risk: Divergent rollback criteria between runtime and console. Mitigation: Use the cut criteria matrix; security/perf vetoes are universal, feature toggles preferred to last-minute rollbacks.
Risk: Insufficient on-call coverage during first 72 hours. Mitigation: Pre-book rotation and identify two cross-functional backups for each primary on-call slot.
decisions_made: Adopt a single release-notes digest template that includes: summary, merged PRs grouped by team, upgrade notes, known issues, owner contact list, and quick rollback instructions.
Set docs-freeze at T-48 hours for all public strings; localization teams will mark non-blocking items and a highlighted 'late-l10n' section will appear in the digest.

Veto authority mapping: Security lead + Runtime lead for perf/security; PMs have scope veto for UX/Console regressions; Engineering leads for feature stability. All vetoes require a documented mitigation plan to be actionable.
open_questions: Who is the canonical owner for marketplace/partner notice updates? (expected answer: GTM lead, follow-up needed)
What SLA window should trigger automatic rollback vs. manual intervention for tail-latency regressions? (proposal: 2-hour sustained SLA breach triggers manual rollback review)
Do we need localized release notes for enterprise customers in APAC for this train? (defer to localization roadmap)
next_steps: Obtain legal and SRE approvals in Confluence by 2026-03-03
Platform to provision Confluence write token and onboarding docs for GH Action (owner: Platform) — due 2026-03-04
Run the scheduled dry-run and record timing/communications gaps (owner: Priya) — 2026-03-05
Finalize hypercare rota and publish to the on-call roster (owner: SRE lead) — 2026-03-07
