source.title: Acceptance criteria for structured response protocols and fallback modes
source.creator: Maya Rodriguez
source.description: Purpose: Define deterministic, testable acceptance criteria for structured output (JSON/typed responses) and tool/function-calling behavior across three operational modes: synchronous full-response, streaming partial-response, and graceful fallback/error envelopes. This ticket captures requirements clarifications, scope decisions, and explicit testable outcomes the product and engineering teams must satisfy before we mark Structured Output v2 as shippable. The doc focuses on schema negotiation, validation tolerances, error/fallback semantics, streaming checkpoints, and telemetry needed for rollouts.
acceptance_criteria: Schema negotiation: Client can request a structured schema via the 'response_schema' parameter (JSON Schema draft-07). The server accepts or returns a compatible schema warning. Accepted if: server returns 200 + 'schema_accepted': true and includes the canonical schema used.

Strict output mode: When client sets 'mode': 'strict', any deviation from the provided schema (missing required prop, type mismatch) must cause the response to be wrapped in an error envelope with top-level 'error_type': 'schema_violation' and include 'violations' array. Acceptance requires an automated test that injects a type mismatch and asserts envelope presence and HTTP 200 with 'error' object.

Best-effort mode: When client sets 'mode': 'best_effort', model may emit heuristic corrections. Acceptance requires the response contain 'schema_confidence' (0-1) and an explanatory 'schema_adaptations' list if any fields were coerced or inferred.

Function/Tool call contract: Server must provide deterministic tool-invocation messages in the canonical form {"tool_name": "<id>", "args": {...}, "call_id": "uuid"}. Acceptance requires an end-to-end integration test invoking a single tool and asserting call_id is returned, tool args match schema, and a follow-up result event references call_id.

Streaming with incremental schema validation: For streaming responses, each partial chunk must include an incremental validation status header 'validation_state' with values {"ok", "partial", "invalid"}. Acceptance: tests assert that the final chunk transitions to 'ok' for valid outputs or finalizes with 'invalid' and an error envelope if violation persists.

Idempotent tool calls & dedup keys: Tool calls triggered by model output must include an idempotency key (call_id). If the same call_id is received twice, the platform must deduplicate and return the original tool result. Acceptance: replay test sends duplicate event and expects single execution and identical result token.

Graceful fallback policy: When primary model fails schema validation or exceeds latency SLO, platform must automatically fallback to alternate model variant (e.g., quantized or smaller) and attach 'fallback_reason' and 'fallback_model' metadata. Acceptance: chaos test simulates kernel OOM and verifies fallback occurred and no user-facing 5xx is returned.

Error envelope standard: All non-schema errors (rate limit, auth, internal) must be returned in a consistent envelope: {"error_type": "<type>", "message": "...", "code": "E_XXX"}. Acceptance: API contract test covering rate-limit and auth failures checks envelope fields and RFC-compliant HTTP codes.

Backward compatibility: Existing clients that do not supply 'response_schema' must continue to receive legacy freeform text/JSON responses. Acceptance: regression tests from legacy test-suite pass unchanged for 99% of fixtures.

Telemetry & observability: Each structured response must emit telemetry keys: 'schema_id' (if supplied), 'mode', 'validation_result', 'streaming_duration_ms', 'tool_calls' (count). Acceptance: Console displays these for sampled requests and unit tests validate emitted metrics format.

Security: If 'response_schema' contains references ($ref) to external URIs, the platform must block remote fetches unless tenant has allowed 'external_schema_fetch' in account settings. Acceptance: security test attempts external $ref and expects block + audit log entry.

Performance SLOs: For non-streaming strict-mode requests, the 95th percentile tail latency must be <= 400ms extra processing overhead for schema validation on top of base generation latency. Acceptance: perf benchmark demonstrates compliance under representative load profile.

Developer ergonomics: SDKs (Python/JS) must provide helpers to construct response_schema and validate SDK-side before send. Acceptance: SDK integration tests validate round-trip schema echo and client-side validation error when schema is malformed.

Documentation: Public API docs updated with examples for strict, best_effort, and streaming modes including example error envelopes and tool call flows. Acceptance: Docs PR merged and latest docs page renders examples without missing assets.

Rollout gating: Feature flagged per-account. GA roll permitted only after completion of automated test suite, two customer pilot signoffs, and no critical regressions in 48h canary window. Acceptance: canary window runs and checklist signed off by PM and Eng leads.
source.test_plan: Automated: unit tests for schema validation logic, integration tests that spin up model runtime + mock tool endpoint, streaming harness that asserts per-chunk validation_state. Manual: run pilot with two customers using real prompt sets, exercise error modes (auth, rate-limit, kernel OOM), validate console telemetry and tracing links. Include chaos-tests for fallback during availability disruption.
source.rollout_plan: Stage 1: internal dogfood + SDK alpha (2 weeks). Stage 2: opt-in tenant pilot with feature-flag (4 weeks) + weekly telemetry review. Stage 3: gated beta release to paid customers with clear opt-out. Stage 4: GA after 2-week canary with no P0 incidents. Rollback path: disable feature flag and route traffic to legacy handler; ensure migration script for stored prompts/schemas is reversible.
decisions: Adopt JSON Schema draft-07 as canonical negotiation format (not OpenAPI) for initial v2 launch.
Expose 'mode' flag with three modes: strict, best_effort, legacy (default).
Require idempotency call_id on tool invocations for deduplication and auditing.
Block external $ref fetches by default; opt-in per-account via security setting.
Stream validation_state in-band to avoid breaking streaming parsers that expect newline-delimited JSON chunks.
open_questions: Should we standardize on a fixed set of 'fallback_model' categories (e.g., fallback-small, fallback-quantized) for telemetry consistency?
How should we price schema-validation as it increases per-request CPU? Billable feature or included?
What is acceptable 'schema_confidence' threshold to auto-apply coercions in best_effort mode?
Should the platform synthesize minimal correction hints when strict mode fails (e.g., suggest missing fields) or leave that to client tooling?
comments: 2026-02-11 Maya Rodriguez: Initial draft and acceptance criteria baked from PM/Eng kickoff. Shared with Eng leads for sizing.
2026-02-18 Ethan Park: Added streaming validation_state requirement after feedback from runtime team re: partial chunk handling.

2026-02-27 Daniel Ortiz (Eng): Perf SLO amended to include validation overhead. ENG owners added dependencies to ENG-2841 and ENG-2999.

source.title: LandingPad sprint allocator and release-train governance
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem statement: As multiple feature teams target overlapping launch windows across runtime, console, and API surfaces, we lack a single, enforceable runway allocator that ties launch dates to capacity reservations, OKR deadlines, and rollback-safe guardrails. This creates last-minute contention for Dedicated capacity, elevated latency risk, and misaligned communications for enterprise customers.

Goal: Build the "LandingPad" sprint allocator — a lightweight governance pattern and supporting coordination checklist that: (1) maps launches to capacity lanes (baseline, hedge, emergency), (2) enforces gating criteria per surface (runtime/console/api), (3) publishes a one-page cross-team runway with rollout owners and rollback triggers, and (4) integrates with quarterly OKRs and the release calendar so product planning and platform capacity are aligned.

Scope and deliverables:
- Define LandingPad governance doc (roles, lanes, gating matrix).
- Create a templated runway artifact in Confluence that PMs can claim per feature and which auto-summarizes capacity needs.
- Lightweight signals & thresholds for autoscaling/capacity reservations (input to ENG tickets ENG-9841/ENG-12012).
- A Slack release-train channel template and weekly cadence (owner rotation) for cross-surface go/no-go.
- Run a pilot with two teams (runtime model-quantization rollout and console reporting migration) during the 2026.04 train.

Non-goals: We will not build a full scheduling automation engine in this ticket — this is governance + templates + two pilots. Full automation will be a follow-up if pilots show >30% reduction in contention events.
acceptance_criteria: LandingPad governance doc published in Confluence with roles (launch owner, capacity sponsor, SRE reviewer) and lane definitions (baseline, hedge, emergency)
Confluence runway template created with required fields (risk level, capacity estimate, regional impact, rollback trigger, OKR tie) and two pilot entries completed
Weekly release-train cadence and Slack channel template documented; first two weekly syncs scheduled and owners assigned
Capacity thresholds and integration points outlined and linked to ENG-9841 and ENG-12012 with clear handoff steps
Pilot retros completed with quantitative metrics: reduced last-minute capacity escalations for pilots by >= 30% or documented root causes if not met
comments: 2026-02-12 Ava Chen: Created ticket. Drafted problem statement and high-level deliverables. Noted dependency on ENG capacity work (ENG-9841) and autoscale signals (ENG-12012).
2026-02-18 Marcus Rivera: Kickoff with SRE and Runtime PMs. Agreed to pilot with runtime-quantization and console-metrics teams. Action: draft LandingPad roles and lane definitions by 2026-02-25.
2026-02-24 Design review (Leah Patel): Recommended a compact one-page runway UI pattern for Confluence export; flagged accessibility requirements. Added as acceptance criteria item.

2026-02-26 SRE feedback (Ravi Kapoor): Proposed capacity hedge lane: 20% reserved GPU for week-of-launch; suggested emergency lane requires security signoff if >100k tokens/sec spike expected. Linked to incident runbook PR (https://github.com/redwood-inference/incident-runbooks/pull/58).

2026-03-01 Marcus Rivera: Governance doc v0.1 uploaded: https://confluence.internal.redwood/LaunchPad-SprintAllocator. Pilot onboarding checklist created: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1PilotLandingPadChecklist. Scheduled pilot kickoff: 2026-03-08.

2026-03-01 PM review (Isabella Moore): Requested explicit OKR mapping table to show cadence alignment and success metrics (lead time to launch, % of launches needing emergency capacity). Added to next iteration.
To do: capture run-rate cost delta for baseline vs hedge lanes for finance review (Finance ticket FIN-3307).

source.title: Procurement Trust Sandbox: SOC2-Minimalist Package + Insurance Offset Pricing Pilot
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Run a 6-week pilot that packages a lightweight, machine-readable SOC2 evidence bundle and an insurance-offset credit product for low-to-medium risk vendors. The goal is to validate whether (a) offering a 'Trust Sandbox' — a self-serve minimal SOC2 artifact package + optional short-term insurance offset — reduces procurement lead time and increases conversion for mid-market customers, and (b) which pricing shape maximizes revenue while keeping legal and risk exposure bounded.
source.background_and_rationale: Enterprise procurement teams spend weeks validating vendor controls; many SMB vendors cannot afford full TA (third-party audit) cycles. We aim to prototyping a minimal evidence bundle (collection of key policies, scoped attestations, standardized service descriptions) that the legal/procurement buyer can consume quickly, plus an insurance-offset credit option that reduces perceived risk and allows procurement to proceed faster. This is complementary to the full SOC2 + custom contract flow and intended as an acceleration path for customers who require lower assurance but faster time-to-contract.
acceptance_criteria: Define a minimum evidence artifact set (<= 12 artifacts) that legal triage agrees reduces manual follow-up by >= 50% in pilot trials
Implement a vendor portal flow to request and deliver the Trust Sandbox bundle within 48 hours for participating vendors
Run pricing A/B: (A) one-time flat fee vs (B) subscription + insurance-credit option; measure conversion and ARR impact across 50 pilot vendors
Legal and Security sign-off documented as Confluence page with explicit allowed use-cases and exclusions
2 pilot customers complete procurement using the sandbox package end-to-end without escalations to enterprise legal
tasks: Product: finalize artifact checklist and buyer-facing FAQ
Design: wireframes for portal request flow, evidence download, and buyer dashboard
Engineering: backend endpoints to attach evidence bundles to vendor records and portal UI
Legal/FinOps: draft insurance-offset term sheet and pricing matrix
Security: threat model and acceptance criteria for allowed evidence sharing
Growth: recruit 50 pilot vendors across SMB and mid-market segments
decisions: Scope: Trust Sandbox will explicitly exclude any raw logs or PII; artifacts limited to policies, control mappings, screenshots of configurations, and scoped attestation statements
Pricing: run simultaneous experiments rather than single canonical price — flat one-time fee vs 12-month subscription with insurance credit; measure payback within 6 months
Portal UX: use lightweight self-serve flow instead of managed collection for pilot to reduce ops overhead
Retention: evidence bundles will be time-stamped and expire after 90 days unless refreshed by vendor
comments: 2026-02-12 - Ava Chen: Kickoff aligned with Legal and Security. Legal asked for explicit exclusions list; Security requested the threat model before any sharing. Action: Product to draft exclusions and initial threat model.

2026-02-18 - Priya Nair (Legal): Draft insurance-offset term sheet attached in Drive. Requested definition of 'accepted evidence' and indemnity caps. Noted we need Finance sign-off on crediting mechanics.

2026-02-22 - Dmitri Volkov (Security): Quick threat model done. Main risks: social-engineered evidence requests, stale artifacts, and vendor impersonation. Proposed mitigations: signed attestations, downloadable audit trail, short TTL on bundles.
2026-02-25 - Marcus Lee (Eng): POC backend schema ready. Two API endpoints: /vendor/{id}/trust-sandbox/request and /vendor/{id}/trust-sandbox/download. Shippable to staging by 2026-03-08.
2026-02-28 - Design review (Priya + Marcus + Ava): Wireframes OK with minor copy changes. Action: Design to add contextual help and sample artifacts so buyers know what they're getting.

2026-03-01 - Ava Chen: Added 50 pilot vendors target; Growth to own outreach. Confirmed security_review_required = true and legal to provide required contract language before pilot starts. Updated due_date to 2026-04-15.
risks_and_mitigation: Risk: Legal/regulatory rejection for non-audited evidence bundles. Mitigation: explicit disclaimers, TTL, and scope limits; require buyer acknowledgement.
Risk: Vendors upload falsified artifacts. Mitigation: require signed attestations and random sampling of vendor follow-up checks; escalate to full audit for high-value deals.
Risk: Insurance partner rejects offset mechanics. Mitigation: engage two insurance partners in parallel and design fallback credit ledger handled by Finance.
Risk: Increased support load. Mitigation: self-serve flows first; reserve a small Solutions bucket for escalations (max 10% of pilot volume).
metrics_and_success: Procurement lead time reduction (median) for pilot deals >= 40%
Pilot conversion rate (interested -> signed) >= 20% higher than baseline
Average revenue per converted pilot customer >= $7k ARR additional in first year
Number of legal escalations per pilot <= 2
False-positive artifact failure rate < 5% following random sampling checks
next_steps: Product: finalize artifact checklist and the buyer FAQ by 2026-03-05
Legal/Finance: finalize insurance-offset term sheet and caps by 2026-03-10
Security: complete and publish threat model to Confluence by 2026-03-04
Engineering: PR to add endpoints and docs for the pilot branch by 2026-03-08
Design: ship portal flows to staging and handoff to Engineering by 2026-03-06
Growth: shortlist and confirm 50 pilot vendors by 2026-03-12
notes: We should measure downstream effect on sales cycle time separately for deals that use the Trust Sandbox vs those that bypass it (control group)
Consider a future automation path where evidence artifacts are continuously refreshed via scheduled security attestations from vendor-managed signals
If insurance-offset credits are successful, consider bundling as an upsell on annual contracts

source.title: Calibration run priority queue, artifact staging, and hot-swap workflow for quant profiles
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: As calibration runs (FP8/INT8 sweeps) scale across tenants and model variants, we need a robust orchestration layer in the Console to prioritize runs, stage resulting quant-profiles as promoted or ephemeral artifacts, and support hot-swap promotion without service disruption. Current behavior queues runs FIFO and writes artifacts directly to long-term storage which causes delays for critical runs and complicates rollbacks and permissioning.

Goal: Implement a priority-aware queue with tenant and SLA awareness, an intermediate staging area for candidate quant profiles (ephemeral with TTL), and a hot-swap promotion path that updates serving routing without requiring tenant downtime. The feature must include RBAC controls for who can promote artifacts, audit logging for promotions, and retention/GC policies for staged artifacts.
acceptance_criteria: Priority queue exists and honors: customer SLA tier, explicit run priority (low/medium/high/mission-critical), and run age (preemption for stuck low-priority runs)
Staging area implemented: new 'candidate' artifact state with configurable TTL (default 48h) and ability to mark artifact as 'promoteable' after accuracy validation passes
Hot-swap promotion updates routing atomically: serving routes can reference promoted artifact without service recycle and have immediate fallback to previous artifact on failure
RBAC: roles defined and enforced for 'run-submit', 'promote-quant-profile', 'approve-promotion', and 'revoke-promotion' with audit entries recorded in console logs
UI: Console shows queue position, estimated start time, candidate artifacts per run, and a 'promote' action guarded by approval flow when required
E2E tests and a canary rollout: smoke test for hot-swap, rollback, and multi-tenant fairness metrics measured on staging cluster
Documentation: Run behavior, TTL defaults, permissions matrix, and run cost/latency expectations published in Console docs
tasks: Design queue policy model and config schema (PM + Eng + Design)
Implement prioritized run scheduler (ENG) with tenant weight factors
Create candidate artifact lifecycle in storage: create, validate, TTL GC (ENG)
Implement atomic hot-swap API in runtime to point model routing at promoted artifact (ENG-runtime)
Add RBAC checks and audit log hooks (ENG-security)
Console UI: queue view, candidate artifact panel, promote flow with approvals (DES + ENG-frontend)
Create migration plan for existing artifacts and small data backfill script
Write e2e tests and run canary on dedicated cluster
Author docs and runbook for incident scenarios and promotion rollbacks
comments: 2025-11-10 - Ava Chen: Scoping kickoff. Initial problem statement and rough acceptance criteria added. Need prioritization matrix from sales for SLA tiers. Assigned to Marco for scoping and ENG proposal.

2025-11-12 - Marco Reyes: Eng kickoff meeting notes: suggested queue model = weighted priority + starvation prevention; prefer ephemeral staging vs immediate LTS writes to accelerate validation; runtime needs an atomic pointer update mechanism. Raised question about cross-region replication latency for promoted artifacts.

2025-11-13 - Priya Nair (Eng): Feasibility note: hot-swap via routing pointer is feasible; requires small runtime change plus consistent KV for pointer publish. Candidate TTL implemented as a lifecycle on S3 + metadata in catalog. We'll prototype with 2-week spike.

2025-11-14 - Jonas Li (Design): Wireframes uploaded to Figma: queue list with filters, artifact detail modal, 'promote' modal with approval gate. UX suggests explicit 'canary percent' on promotion in Dedicated deployments.

2025-11-16 - Marco Reyes: Updated engineering plan posted: scheduler, staging API, promotion API, RBAC extension. Dependencies: ENG-34012 (storage GC infra) and ENG-34789 (runtime pointer publish). Requested security review for audit semantics.

2025-11-18 - Ava Chen: PM decision: proceed with prioritized queue + staging + hot-swap. Set default TTL = 48h for candidates and approval required for cross-tenant promotions. Target release: runtime-1.26 / console-2025.12 canary. Added to Cycle 2025-W46.
decisions: Use weighted priority queue that accounts for customer tier, explicit run priority, and run age to avoid starvation
Staged artifacts are ephemeral 'candidate' objects separate from long-term quant-profile artifacts until promoted
Hot-swap implemented as an atomic routing pointer update with optional canary percent for Dedicated deployments
Approval required when promotion affects another tenant's serving path or when accuracy regression > configured threshold
Default candidate TTL = 48 hours; configurable per project with min 1 hour max 7 days

source.title: Policy Canvas: region priority & tier-fallback visual configurator
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a Console-first visual editor ("Policy Canvas") that lets customers author routing policies combining region selection, customer tier routing, and fallback model variants. The editor should: 1) surface region priority lists with weighted preference and automatic compatibility checks, 2) allow tier mapping (e.g., free/prod/enterprise) to model variants and region tiers, 3) configure fallback variants (quantized/cheaper models or lower-tier regions) with stacked priority, 4) run live validation and a preview simulation that shows example request routing across a sample traffic profile, and 5) generate a machine-readable policy JSON for the runtime while preserving a human-friendly visual layout.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Visual canvas supports adding/removing regions, assigning numeric priority and weights, and marking regions as 'warm' or 'cold'.
2) Tier nodes can be mapped to one or more models; UI prevents mapping to models not available in a region (validation).
3) Fallback chain UI allows multiple fallback steps and shows expected failover order.
4) Real-time validator highlights configuration problems (missing models in all regions, circular fallbacks, incompatible quantization).
5) Preview simulator runs a 10k-sample traffic run and shows % routed to each model/region per tier and expected median latency estimate.
6) Generated policy JSON matches the runtime schema and passes ENG-5214 integration tests.
7) Console telemetry surfaces per-policy dispatch breakdown and validation error counts in the policy inspector panel.
source.engineering_notes:
- Backend: reuse routing engine v2 API from ENG-5214; add preview endpoint that accepts a traffic profile and returns aggregate routing decisions.
- Validation: implement a synchronous linter in the Console that mirrors runtime validation to avoid drift.
- Telemetry: emit policy_id, validation_errors, preview_snapshot, and per-region routing counters to the existing metrics pipeline.
- Performance: preview simulation must run < 3s for 10k synthetic samples in the console job.
- Data model: policy JSON must include explicit compatibility matrix to short-circuit runtime checks.
- Risks: complex UIs can encourage brittle policies; add recommended presets and guardrails.
- Dependencies: coordination with ENG-5389 (runtime fallback ordering) to standardize tie-break rules.
source.qa_plan:
- Unit tests for the linter with negative and positive policy cases.
- E2E tests: create policy through UI, run preview, verify routing outcomes align with simulated expectations.
- Fuzz test: feed randomized traffic profiles to preview endpoint to ensure no crashes.
- Load test: preview endpoint under concurrent requests (50 concurrent previews).
- Accessibility audit for keyboard/ARIA on the canvas nodes.
source.rollout_plan:
1) Feature-flag behind console.beta.policy_canvas.
2) Internal dogfood with 3 enterprise customers and platform team for two weeks.
3) Collect usability metrics and validation error rates; iterate on presets.
4) Gradual ramp to 25% orgs, then 100% after stabilization.
5) Post-launch: monitor errors and per-policy rollback rate for 7 days.
comments: 2025-11-06 - Ava Chen: Kickoff — scoped initial UX flows and core acceptance criteria. Asked ENG to confirm preview latency SLO. Added ENG-5214 and ENG-5389 as dependencies.

2025-11-08 - Miguel Santos: Design review flagged the need for a compact 'preset' panel for common routing patterns (region failover, cost-first, latency-first). Updating Figma.
Feedback: show estimated cost delta per policy in the canvas to help customers choose fallback variants.

2025-11-10 - Priya Nair (Eng): Confirmed ENG-5214 can return per-sample decisions; will add preview aggregation endpoint in the next sprint. Raised a potential schema mismatch: current runtime lacks explicit 'weight' semantics for region tiers — ENG-5389 will address tie-break rules.
2025-11-12 - Design: Uploaded updated canvas flow with microcopy for each node type and a compact policy JSON preview panel. Added keyboard shortcuts for quick mapping between tiers and models.

2025-11-14 - Miguel Santos: Implemented initial linter prototype in Console branch (PR: github.com/redwood/repo/pull/4217). Linter detects missing model availability across selected regions and flags circular fallbacks. Needs integration test coverage.

2025-11-16 - Security: Quick review — no sensitive data stored in policy JSON, but recommend adding an allowlist for model variants exposed in Private deployments. Marked security_review_required=false for now; will re-open if private onboarding requires additional controls.

2025-11-18 - Ava Chen: Updated acceptance criteria to require generated policy JSON to pass ENG integration tests. Scheduling cross-team demo for 2025-11-25 to walk through preview and validation UX with platform and runtime teams.
Links: PR: https://github.com/redwood/repo/pull/4217 | Figma: https://figma.redwood.internal/file/PolicyCanvas/v2 | Confluence PRD: https://docs.redwood.com/confluence/display/RD/Policy+Canvas+PRD

source.title: Post-pilot readout + next-steps plan — Vertical ABM (Regulated Workloads: FinServ + Healthcare)
source.creator: Ben Carter
source.description:
Goal
Create a durable post-pilot readout covering (1) pipeline outcomes, (2) lessons learned / what changed in messaging + process, (3) what assets converted, and (4) a proposal to scale ABM to additional regulated segments without pulling in major product scope.

Audience
Exec staff (Marketing + Sales + Product), Security/GRC, SE leadership. This is intended to become the “v1 playbook” artifact for repeatable regulated outbound.

Pilot snapshot
- Timebox: ~8 weeks (prep + execution + close) May–June 2025
- Verticals: Financial services (primary) + Healthcare/PHI-adjacent (secondary)
- Primary products: Redwood Dedicated + Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Core buyer set: CISO/Security, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement

Success criteria (from brief)
- Validate repeatable positioning + outbound motion for compliance-driven buyers
- Produce a baseline set of vertical assets (landing page, one-pagers, security FAQ/evidence pack index, talk tracks, sequences)
- Generate qualified pipeline (meetings, SQLs, pilots/POCs, conversion to procurement)

Outcomes (high-level)
Pipeline / meetings
- Target accounts: 38 total (FinServ: 24, Healthcare: 14)
- Coverage: 32/38 had a named owner + first touch within first 10 business days of execution
- Positive replies: 21 accounts (55% of targeted accounts) had at least one positive signal (reply, referral, security questionnaire request, or “send materials”) 
- Meetings booked: 14 (FinServ: 9, Healthcare: 5)
- SQLs created: 6 (FinServ: 4, Healthcare: 2)
- POCs started: 2 (Northbridge Banking, Helix Health System)
- Procurement/legal engaged: 1 (Trident Financial Services)

Qualitative signals
- “Regulated-ready” posture consistently drove the first conversion event (security call / evidence request), not performance claims alone.
- The best-performing hook was: predictable throughput + compliance controls + deployment flexibility (VPC/on-prem) framed as “avoid building and operating inference infrastructure under audit constraints.”
- Most late-stage risk was not product gaps; it was response-time + consistency on security questionnaires and commercial packaging clarity (Dedicated vs Private vs hosted, plus retention/residency caveats).

Account outcomes (not exhaustive; focus on stage movement)
- Northbridge Banking (FinServ): POC complete; success criteria met on latency + logging requirements; next step is a rollout plan + procurement discovery (Dedicated vs Private decision leaning Private/VPC due to internal network policy).
- Helix Health System (Healthcare): POC scoped + executed; outcome was “possible” but additional PHI guardrails and clearer retention/residency controls needed for full go-live; next step is security follow-up + procurement timing alignment.
- Brightline Insurance (FinServ): security review completed; evidence pack requested; next step is a short technical deep dive (audit log export format + SAML/SCIM) before committing to POC.
- Regal Wealth Management (FinServ): technical deep dive completed on audit logs + KMS/HSM; strong fit signal; waiting on internal platform team bandwidth to scope pilot.
- Trident Financial Services (FinServ): progressed to procurement/legal; redlines in flight (DPA/SLA + residency language); outcome TBD.

What we shipped (assets + enablement)
Assets produced/updated during the pilot (v1)
- Regulated workloads landing page (with cross-links to Security FAQ + evidence pack index)
- FinServ messaging framework v1
- Healthcare messaging framework v1
- Regulated workloads security FAQ (sales-ready)
- Evidence pack index page (request/sharing guidance)
- Regulated Workloads ABM deck v1
- Regulated inference competitive battlecard
- Outbound email sequence (FinServ) + LinkedIn ads creative brief
- Webinar outline + follow-up templates (webinar not executed within pilot window; outline used for 1:1 talk track)

Asset conversion (directional; pull exact counts from dashboard spec)
- Highest-converting assets in “first conversion event” (meeting / security call):
  1) Security FAQ (link sent in outbound follow-up) 
  2) Evidence pack index (created confidence + made the request process concrete)
  3) ABM deck (used in live calls; reduced time to “next step”)
- Landing page performance was “good enough” for ABM but not yet a scalable demand gen engine; the CTA that worked best was “Book a security + architecture review” over “Download benchmark.”

Messaging learnings (what worked / what we changed)
What worked
- Lead with auditability + control: audit logs, encryption/KMS, SSO/SAML, data handling boundaries.
- Pair compliance with economics: “predictable throughput and cost controls under real traffic,” not generic “fast GPUs.”
- Emphasize deployment flexibility as a risk reducer: Hosted → Dedicated → Private/VPC/on-prem as they mature.

What did not work (or triggered friction)
- “HIPAA-ready” language in healthcare; too loaded without strict qualification and legal review. Replaced with “PHI-adjacent workflows” + “supports common security controls; details depend on deployment and customer configuration.”
- Overly model-centric positioning; buyers cared more about operational posture (logs, access controls, change management, retention).

Top objections and effective responses
- “We need on-prem / no public internet access” → Redwood Private deployment pattern + customer-managed networking; SE-led architecture review.
- “We need audit logs exported to our SIEM” → show log fields + export options; clarify timeline for any format specifics; point to examples.
- “We need strict data retention controls” → clarify current retention policy per deployment and what is customer-configurable; align on requirements early.
- “We already have GPUs” → frame Redwood as the inference runtime + control plane + operational tooling (rollouts, fallback, observability) and faster path to SLA compliance.

Process learnings (internal)
What went well
- Weekly governance with Sales/PMM/Security/SE prevented major claim drift.
- Evidence pack “index + request process” reduced ad-hoc doc hunting and lowered security review cycle time.

- Security questionnaire turnaround was inconsistent when asks landed via multiple channels (email + Slack + calls). We need a single intake and SLA.

- Verticals: Financial services (primary) + Healthcare/PHI-adjacent (secondary)
- Primary products: Redwood Dedicated + Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Core buyer set: CISO/Security, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement

Success criteria (from brief)
- Validate repeatable positioning + outbound motion for compliance-driven buyers
- Produce a baseline set of vertical assets (landing page, one-pagers, security FAQ/evidence pack index, talk tracks, sequences)
- Generate qualified pipeline (meetings, SQLs, pilots/POCs, conversion to procurement)

Pipeline / meetings
- Target accounts: 38 total (FinServ: 24, Healthcare: 14)
- Coverage: 32/38 had a named owner + first touch within first 10 business days of execution

- Meetings booked: 14 (FinServ: 9, Healthcare: 5)
- SQLs created: 6 (FinServ: 4, Healthcare: 2)
- POCs started: 2 (Northbridge Banking, Helix Health System)
- Procurement/legal engaged: 1 (Trident Financial Services)

source.description:
Goal
Run a 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot focused on regulated workloads (initial verticals: Financial Services + Healthcare) to validate repeatable positioning/outbound motion for security/compliance-driven buyers and generate qualified pipeline for Redwood Dedicated + Redwood Private.

In scope
- Target account selection + assignment (NA + EMEA)
- Persona map + messaging alignment for: CISO/Security, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement
- Vertical assets (landing page, one-pagers, security FAQ + evidence pack index, talk tracks, sequences)
- Outreach + meeting booking + discovery/demo/security reviews
- Optional short pilots (benchmark + architecture validation) with SE support
- Measurement: coverage, engagement, meetings, SQLs, POC starts, stage velocity, closed-won signals

Out of scope
- Major new product features (OK: minor docs updates/clarifications + enablement)

Primary deliverables (this ticket tracks plan + KPI definitions + weekly execution status)
1) Targeting
- Account list for FinServ + Healthcare, with score + intent signals + owner
- Persona mapping per account (where known)

2) Assets
- Regulated workloads landing page
- Vertical one-pagers (FinServ Private/Dedicated + Healthcare PHI-adjacent)
- Security FAQ for regulated buyers + evidence pack index + sharing guidance
- Outbound email sequence(s) + LinkedIn ad brief
- Sales enablement deck + competitive battlecard alignment

3) Measurement / ops
- Weekly operating cadence + risk log
- KPI dashboard spec + data sources (HubSpot, Gmail, LinkedIn)
- UTM conventions + attribution approach

Working links
- Week-by-week plan (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/abm-pilot-week-by-week-plan.json
- Target accounts (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/target-account-list-finserv-health-sheet.json
- Intent scoring notes (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/intent-signal-sources-and-scoring.json
- Persona/pain matrix (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/persona-pain-points-messaging-matrix.json
- Landing page copy draft (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/landing-page-copy-draft-regulated.json
- Metrics dashboard spec (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/abm-pilot-metrics-dashboard-spec.json

Owners / RACI
- Pilot execution + reporting: Ben Carter
- Executive sponsor / approvals: Camila Reyes
- Messaging + positioning / claim approvals: Soojin Lee (with Dr. Aisha Rahman + Naomi Feldman for security/compliance review)
- Marketing ops + attribution / tracking: Tyler Benson
- CRM hygiene + pipeline analytics: Isabelle Laurent
- Outbound program: Grace O'Connor + Rohan Mehta
- Sales coverage: Stephanie Nguyen (NA), Lukas Meyer (EMEA); AEs: Avery Johnson, Priyom Das, Hannah Schmitt, Diego Fernandez
- SE coverage for technical deep dives/pilots: Markus Klein (lead), Fatima Noor (regulated lead)

Timeline (baseline)
- Weeks 1–2 (Prep): finalize vertical choice + ICP, build account list, finalize messaging, produce/QA assets, finalize measurement
- Weeks 3–6 (Execute): outreach, meetings, security reviews, 1–2 pilots (as qualified)
- Weeks 7–8 (Close): convert to next stage, procurement support, post-pilot readout + plan

KPI definitions (pilot success criteria)
Top-level targets (pilot-level)
- Target account coverage: 60–80 named accounts with assigned owner + next step recorded
- Meetings booked: 12–18 total (mix of security/discovery/technical deep dives)
- SQLs created: 6–10 (explicit project, timeline, deployment path, champion)
- POCs/pilots launched: 2–4 (benchmark + architecture validation); at least 1 in FinServ OR healthcare reaching closeout
- Pipeline created: tracked as $ qualified pipeline for Dedicated/Private (report range weekly; do not commit publicly)
- Stage velocity: time from first meeting → security review → pilot scoping (trend over time)

Leading indicators (weekly)
- Outreach volume and reply rate (by persona)
- Landing page engagement (CTR, form fills, meeting-request conversion)
- Evidence pack requests and security review progression (questionnaires opened, evidence shared, follow-up scheduled)
- Partner/co-sell sourced intros (AWS Marketplace motion) and conversion

Quality gates (compliance-safe marketing)
- Claims must match approved language from Security/PMM threads (avoid “HIPAA-certified/ready” style over-claims; use precise phrasing and footnotes)
- Evidence pack sharing per confidentiality guidance; log who has access

Risks / mitigations
- Security review bandwidth bottleneck → mitigate with evidence pack index + pre-baked FAQ answers + weekly 30-min Security office hours
- Ambiguous packaging for Dedicated vs Private → mitigate with internal guidance + consistent talk track + escalation path (Lauren Bishop / Vivek Kulkarni)
- Low-volume LinkedIn audiences in EMEA → mitigate with account-based outreach + partner co-sell + tighter job-title filters

Acceptance criteria
- KPI dashboard spec finalized and implemented in tracking sheet (owner: Tyler/Isabelle)
- Target account list v1 shipped + owners assigned + status hygiene process documented
- Landing page + one-pagers published with Security-approved language
- Email sequence in-market + A/B variant noted
- Weekly standup cadence established with updates captured below
- Post-pilot readout ticket (PM-1440) kicked off with inputs ready

title: Vertical ABM pilot plan + KPIs (regulated workloads)
preview:
Goal
Run a 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot focused on regulated workloads (initial verticals: Financial Services + Healthcare) to validate repeatable positioning/outbound motion for security/compliance-driven buyers and generate qualified pipeline for Redwood Dedicated + Redwood Private.

In scope
- Target account selection + assignment (NA + EMEA)
- Persona map + messaging alignment for: CISO/Security, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement
- Vertical assets (landing page, one-pagers, security FAQ + evidence pack index, talk tracks, sequences)
- Outreach + meeting booking + discovery/demo/security reviews
- Optional short pilots (benchmark + architecture validation) with SE support
- Measurement: coverage, engagement, meetings, SQLs, POC starts, stage velocity, closed-won signals

Out of scope
- Major new product features (OK: minor docs updates/clarifications + enablement)

Primary deliverables (this ticket tracks plan + KPI definitions + weekly execution status)
1) Targeting
- Account list for FinServ + Healthcare, with score + intent signals + owner
- Persona mapping per account (where known)

2) Assets
- Regulated workloads landing page
- Vertical one-pagers (FinServ Private/Dedicated + Healthcare PHI-adjacent)
- Security FAQ for regulated buyers + evidence pack index + sharing guidance
- Outbound email sequence(s) + LinkedIn ad brief
- Sales enablement deck + competitive battlecard alignment

3) Measurement / ops
- Weekly operating cadence + risk log
- KPI dashboard spec + data sources (HubSpot, Gmail, LinkedIn)
- UTM conventions + attribution approach

Working links
- Week-by-week plan (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/abm-pilot-week-by-week-plan.json
- Target accounts (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/target-account-list-finserv-health-sheet.json
- Intent scoring notes (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/intent-signal-sources-and-scoring.json
- Persona/pain matrix (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/persona-pain-points-messaging-matrix.json
- Landing page copy draft (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/landing-page-copy-draft-regulated.json
- Metrics dashboard spec (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/abm-pilot-metrics-dashboard-spec.json

Owners / RACI
- Pilot execution + reporting: Ben Carter
- Executive sponsor / approvals: Camila Reyes
- Messaging + positioning / claim approvals: Soojin Lee (with Dr. Aisha Rahman + Naomi Feldman for security/compliance review)
- Marketing ops + attribution / tracking: Tyler Benson
- CRM hygiene + pipeline analytics: Isabelle Laurent
- Outbound program: Grace O'Connor + Rohan Mehta
- Sales coverage: Stephanie Nguyen (NA), Lukas Meyer (EMEA); AEs: Avery Johnson, Priyom Das, Hannah Schmitt, Diego Fernandez
- SE coverage for technical deep dives/pilots: Markus Klein (lead), Fatima Noor (regulated lead)

Timeline (baseline)
- Weeks 1–2 (Prep): finalize vertical choice + ICP, build account list, finalize messaging, produce/QA assets, finalize measurement
- Weeks 3–6 (Execute): outreach, meetings, security reviews, 1–2 pilots (as qualified)
- Weeks 7–8 (Close): convert to next stage, procurement support, post-pilot readout + plan

KPI definitions (pilot success criteria)
Top-level targets (pilot-level)
- Target account coverage: 60–80 named accounts with assigned owner + next step recorded
- Meetings booked: 12–18 total (mix of security/discovery/technical deep dives)
- SQLs created: 6–10 (explicit project, timeline, deployment path, champion)
- POCs/pilots launched: 2–4 (benchmark + architecture validation); at least 1 in FinServ OR healthcare reaching closeout

Leading indicators (weekly)
- Outreach volume and reply rate (by persona)

- Landing page + one-pagers published with Security-approved language
- Email sequence in-market + A/B variant noted
- Weekly standup cadence established with updates captured below
- Post-pilot readout ticket (PM-1440) kicked off with inputs ready

source.title: Create regulated workloads landing page (ABM pilot)
source.creator: Ben Carter
source.description:
Goal
Launch a customer-facing landing page tailored to security/compliance-driven buyers (finserv + healthcare/PHI-adjacent) as part of the 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot. Page should route to the correct next step (security review call / architecture consult) and link to Sales-ready security materials without making over-broad compliance claims.

Primary CTA
“Request a security + architecture review” (form) with secondary CTA: “See security FAQ” (public doc) and gated CTA: “Request evidence pack” (sales-assisted / NDA).

Scope / Requirements
1) Page structure (target: single page, lightweight, skimmable)
- Hero: regulated positioning (performance + compliance posture) with 1 sentence on deployment options.
- Sections:
  - “Deploy the way regulated teams need”: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (VPC/on-prem) with clear guidance on when to choose each.
  - “Security & compliance controls”: encryption, key management, audit logs, SSO/SAML, retention/residency controls (wording must be Security-approved).
  - “Operational reliability”: SLAs (where applicable), predictable throughput, observability, rollouts/fallback.
  - “How a 2–3 week pilot works”: benchmark + architecture validation + security review timeline.
  - “FAQ”: short, link out to full security FAQ.
- Footnotes/clarifiers:
  - No “HIPAA-certified/HIPAA-compliant” or “guarantees” language.
  - Clarify shared responsibility (customer config + Redwood controls).

2) Copy guidelines (must align with PMM + Security)
- Must use the approved phrasing from PMM/Security review thread (see: sources/gmail/soojin_lee/20250504-messaging-review-regulated-workloads.json).
- Avoid absolute statements unless backed by evidence pack artifacts.
- Keep claims to capabilities (e.g., “supports audit logging” vs “meets all audit requirements”).

3) Design + UX
- Reuse existing docs site layout patterns where possible.
- Include “Talk to Security” routing for regulated buyers (minimize dead-ends).
- Ensure accessibility (contrast, headings, keyboard nav). (Design ticket: DES-882)

4) Measurement / Ops
- Add UTMs and conversion tracking (form submission + calendar booking).
- Capture persona + deployment interest on form:
  - Persona: Security/GRC, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement/IT
  - Deployment interest: Dedicated, Private (VPC), On-prem, Not sure
  - Region/residency requirement (optional)
- Owner for attribution spec: Tyler Benson.

5) Review checklist (hard gate before publish)
Security (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Naomi Feldman)
- Approved language for encryption, key management, audit logging, retention/residency, incident response.
- Confirm links to Security FAQ + evidence pack index are correct and access-controlled.
- Confirm no new commitments on timelines/SLAs.

Legal (Michael Grant / Sofia Mendes)
- Check disclaimers/footnotes and CTA wording.
- Ensure evidence pack request flow implies NDA where required.

Docs (Elliot Price / Kira Thompson)
- Style, IA, link hygiene, and maintenance owner for future edits.

Deliverables
- Published landing page on docs/marketing site (final URL TBD; align with existing docs routing).
- Cross-links:
  - Security FAQ: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/security-faq/regulated-workloads-security-faq.json
  - Evidence pack index (internal + process): sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence/abm-pilot-security-evidence-pack-index.json
- Brief internal enablement blurb in Slack (#sales, #marketing) with approved snippet + link.

Implementation notes / Links
- Draft copy source: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/landing-page-copy-draft-regulated.json
- Docs PR (implementation): sources/github/redwood-docs/pr-421-add-regulated-workloads-vertical-landing-page.json
- Related positioning brief: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/positioning-and-messaging/vertical-abm-pilot-regulated-workloads-brief.json

Acceptance criteria
- Page is live and indexed where intended (or intentionally noindex if requested by Marketing during pilot).
- CTA form is functional and routes to correct owner queue.
- All security/compliance claims are explicitly approved by Security + Legal.
- Links to Security FAQ and evidence pack request instructions work.
- Tracking confirms at minimum: page views, CTA clicks, form submits.

Launch target
Publish by 2025-05-30 (end of prep window) to support Week 3 outreach; if delayed, publish no later than 2025-06-03 with comms to ABM owners.
comments: 2025-05-02 — Ben Carter: Creating ticket to unblock outbound. Please keep scope tight (no new product promises). Target is a conversion-oriented page that passes Security/Legal on first review.
2025-05-05 — Elliot Price: Acknowledged. We’ll implement on docs site first for speed + version control. Looping in Kira for edits and review flow.
2025-05-07 — Soojin Lee: Added copy notes: avoid “HIPAA-ready” headline; use “PHI-adjacent workflows” language. CTA should bias toward security review vs generic demo.

2025-05-09 — Naomi Feldman: Security notes: do not claim specific compliance certifications on-page unless we link to evidence pack process. Also ensure audit logging wording is capability-focused (fields/export formats vary by deployment).
2025-05-13 — DES-882 update: Design wireframe ready for implementation; reuse existing section components; keep hero minimal and trust-building (no logo wall yet).
2025-05-20 — Tyler Benson: Tracking plan confirmed. UTMs standardized to utm_campaign=regulated_abm_pilot, utm_source=linkedin|email|partner. Form should capture persona + deployment interest.
2025-05-23 — Michael Grant: Legal review quick pass: add disclaimer that security features/configurations vary by deployment mode and customer policy; evidence pack is under NDA on request.
2025-05-28 — Elliot Price: PR up for review (PR-421). Added cross-links to Security FAQ and evidence pack index. Requesting Security final sign-off on copy blocks + footnotes.
2025-05-30 — Naomi Feldman: Approved with edits: removed one sentence that implied retention guarantees; added ‘configurable retention policies’ phrasing. OK to publish after those are in.
2025-06-03 — Elliot Price: Merged + published. Shared final link in #marketing and #sales with approved excerpt. Tracking verified on form submissions.
2025-06-06 — Ben Carter: Marking done. This is now the canonical link for Week 4–6 outreach; any changes must go through Security review.

source.title: pricing-boundaries-sso-rbac-contract-clauses-experiment
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design and run a pricing + contract experiment that clarifies when SSO/SAML and RBAC are treated as included features vs paid add-ons, and produce a contract language pack and security questionnaire guidance for sales/legal. Outcome: a repeatable packaging decision framework and a short-run pricing experiment to validate willingness-to-pay and sales friction.
source.background: Multiple enterprise prospects request SSO/SAML + fine-grained RBAC as explicit contractual commitments. Legal and Sales need consistent language and guardrails (implementation timelines, support commitments, pricing). Current ambiguity causes long procurement loops and ad-hoc concessions. Prior PM tickets outlined the security commitments matrix but did not tie to pricing experiments or standardized contract language.
source.goal: Ship a contract clause library + pricing experiment that: (1) establishes whether SSO/SAML is part of 'Enterprise' baseline or an add-on in >50% of negotiations; (2) quantifies price uplift and sales close impact for RBAC as a committed SLA; (3) produces approved security questionnaire canned answers and a one-page implementation SLA to include in term sheets.
acceptance_criteria: Legal-approved contract clause templates for: SSO/SAML included, SSO as add-on, RBAC commitment clause
A 6-week pricing experiment plan with 3 cohorts (baseline, include-SSO, add-on-SSO+RBAC) and measurement plan
At least 5 sales cycles run in experiment with documented customer feedback
Security questionnaire mapped to standard answers for top-10 enterprise controls and cross-referenced to our Private/Dedicated offering
Go/no-go decision documented and next steps for rollout (packaging change + billing SKU updates)
decisions: Experiment splits agreed: Cohort A (current packaging), Cohort B (SSO included in Enterprise list price), Cohort C (SSO included + RBAC committed with 6-month SLA, price uplift)
RBAC guarantee limited to configuration-level RBAC for Redwood Console and API keys; database-level row-level permissions deferred to customer implementation
Implementation SLA windows: 90 days for Hosted/Dedicated, 180 days for Private (on-prem) — to be reflected in contract clause
Security questionnaire canned answers will reference existing SOC2 and provide additional items specific to VPC/Private deployments rather than promise new controls
comments: 2025-03-11 - Ava Chen: Kickoff with Legal and Sales. Legal requested examples of prior concessions and estimated implementation effort from Eng (see ENG-2048 backlog). Assigned working doc in Confluence.
2025-03-12 - Liam O'Connor: Sales provided 7 anonymized RFPs where SSO/RBAC were gating items. Extracted common asks and added to experiment criteria.

2025-03-13 - Priya Patel (Legal): Reviewed draft clauses; flagged indemnity language around RBAC commitments. Requested explicit limit of liability language and implementation remedy (technical support + credits).

2025-03-14 - Rafael Gomez (Engineering): Feasibility note: current RBAC milestone to support workspace-level roles is on roadmap for runtime-1.22; dedicated/private install timing varies. Suggested SLA windows above.

2025-03-15 - Liam O'Connor: Updated experiment cohort definitions and revenue modeling. Added projected uplift scenarios: +8% price for SSO included, +18-25% for SSO+RBAC commitment (varies by deal size).
2025-03-17 - Legal review meeting: agreed to narrow RBAC commitment to 'feature availability' and not uptime SLA for RBAC-specific APIs. Legal approves clause template pending risk register addition.

2025-03-18 - Ava Chen: Prepared go/no-go checklist for end of experiment. Requested instrumentation from Analytics: close-rate delta, time-to-close, average ACV, and sales friction notes. Ticket updated.
risks: Large enterprise customers expect immediate RBAC parity with existing on-prem systems; overpromising will lead to delivery delays
Price uplift could lengthen sales cycles and reduce conversion in mid-market segment

source.title: Tiered Security Addendum & Pricing Experiment for SSO/SAML + RBAC
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem statement: Enterprise customers frequently require contractual commitments for access control (SSO/SAML) and role-based access control (RBAC). Currently these appear as ad-hoc addenda negotiated during sales cycles which increases legal time and sales friction. We need a product-led packaging experiment that standardizes a small set of contractable security addenda tied to explicit pricing/commitment tiers so sales and legal can offer predictable options without lengthy negotiation.

Goals: 1) Define 2-3 standardized 'Security Addendum' tiers that can be attached to Hosted and Dedicated offerings: Basic (SSO/SAML support + standard RBAC), Enhanced (SLA-backed RBAC enforcement + audit log retention), and Isolated (private tenant + KMS integration + custom RBAC mappings). 2) Determine attachment pricing or commitment thresholds for each tier and run an experiment with a subset of pipeline customers to validate willingness-to-pay and contract velocity impact. 3) Produce proposed contract clause language, checklist for Security Questionnaire responses, and an approved sales playbook for offering the tiers.

Scope: Includes hosted API and Redwood Dedicated. Excludes full on-prem appliance packaging and custom engineering for bespoke RBAC models; those remain enterprise professional services work.

Assumptions: - Most customers prefer a small menu of options vs bespoke legal addenda. - Pricing signal should be conservative for initial experiment to reduce friction. - Security questionnaire answers must map directly to the tier capabilities to reduce legal back-and-forth.

Planned experiment: Draft tier definitions and pricing -> legal review for clause language -> pilot offer to 8 inbound enterprise trials (sales will include a simple A/B: standard negotiation vs pick-a-tier) -> measure contract cycle time, acceptance rate, average ARR uplift, and number of redlines.
acceptance_criteria: Three security addendum tiers defined with clear capabilities and limits
Contract-ready clause language for each tier reviewed & approved by Legal
Price or commitment thresholds proposed for each tier and validated with finance
Security questionnaire (standardized answers) mapped to each tier and stored in Confluence
Pilot with at least 8 customers executed and initial metrics recorded
Sales playbook and templated order form created for pilot offers
metrics: Change in average contract negotiation time (target: -25%)
ARR uplift for customers choosing Enhanced/Isolated tiers
Number of legal redlines per contract with tier vs bespoke addenda
Conversion delta between A/B groups after 90 days
steps: Draft tier definitions and capability matrix (PM + Eng + Security)
Collaborate with Legal to produce clause language and addendum templates
Define pricing/commitment thresholds with Finance and RevOps
Prepare Security Questionnaire canonical answers for each tier
Build sales playbook, templated order forms, and negotiation guidance
Identify pilot customers and run A/B offering with sales reps
Collect metrics for 60-90 days and synthesize learnings
Decide: roll to GA, iterate on tiers/pricing, or retire experiment
risks: Legal pushback on canned commitments could require time-consuming revisions
Pricing set too high will suppress adoption; too low will cannibalize revenue
Engineering/infra changes needed for Enhanced/Isolated tiers may be underestimated
Customers with custom compliance needs may still require bespoke work, limiting experiment reach

2025-03-15: Finance recommended starting price multipliers: Basic = no charge, Enhanced = +15% committed monthly, Isolated = +40% with minimum 12-month commitment
2025-03-17: Security requested explicit data residency wording for Isolated tier; added to clause draft

2025-03-15 - Priya Nair (Finance): Confirmed experiment budget and suggested price elasticity tests across pilot buckets
2025-03-16 - Maria Gomez (Security): Provided canonical answers for SAML metadata lifecycle, audit log retention, and RBAC mapping constraints

source.title: Consolidate SIG/CAIQ responses and standardized customer attestations for pricing tiers
source.creator: Anita Lopez
source.description: Goal: Create a single authoritative SIG/CAIQ response bundle and a set of tiered customer attestation templates that map to Redwood pricing/packaging tiers. This reduces sales cycles, ensures consistent legal posture across deals, and enables automated artifact generation for self-serve and sales-assisted flows. Scope: inventory existing responses, reconcile conflicts between engineering artifacts and legal guidance, produce a canonical CAIQ answer pack (v1) plus three attestation templates (Self-Serve, Dedicated, Private) with agreed scoping language. Deliverables: canonical CAIQ JSON+PDF, three attestation templates, a lightweight sales playbook page, a generator script to inject customer-specific values, and a legal sign-off checklist.

A single-source-of-truth doc and a short runbook for Sales to attach to proposals

source.title: Enterprise dedicated request gating and prioritization framework
source.creator: Marisol Ortega
source.description:
Problem: We receive ad-hoc enterprise requests for Dedicated (reserved) capacity that vary widely in SLA expectations, throughput requirements, and hardware compatibility. Currently intake lacks consistent gating criteria, leading to mismatched capacity commits, last-minute engineering scope increases, and elevated operational risk.

Goal: Define a lightweight but rigorous intake and prioritization framework that product and solutions teams can use to triage enterprise Dedicated requests. The framework should: 1) capture minimal required signal to assess feasibility (SLA targets, steady vs burst throughput, GPU class preference, region/VPC constraints, data residency), 2) produce a prioritization score combining customer strategic value and operational risk, and 3) recommend concrete next steps (pilot, reserved commit, or decline) and engineering scope.

Scope: This is a non-bug product initiative focused on intake/process (not new runtime features). It will produce a decision rubric, updated intake form, and a short runbook for Solutions/SEs and PMs. Engineering will be consulted on feasibility/effort estimates but large infra deliverables are out of scope for this ticket.
acceptance_criteria: Decision rubric documented (PDF/Confluence) with mandatory fields and gating thresholds for SLA targets, throughput, GPU class, and burst policy.
Prioritization matrix (scorecard) implemented as a Google Sheet template that outputs recommendation: pilot / reserved-commit / follow-up / decline.
Intake form updated in Salesforce/HubSpot and a short Solutions checklist created for field teams.
Two pilot cases triaged with the rubric and outcomes recorded (one high-priority commit, one declined/redirected).
Sufficient engineering review/comments captured; any non-trivial infra work is converted to ENG tickets with clear owners.
notes: Design constraints: keep the intake to <10 fields to avoid friction with sales; require only minimal technical details for initial gating.

Scoring factors proposal: customer strategic value (0-5), SLA strictness/latency SLO (0-5), throughput size (tokens/s or rps -> 0-5), GPU fit risk (model needs specialized GPUs or quantization -> 0-5), operational complexity (VPC + compliance -> 0-5). Weigh strategic value x2 when computing final priority.
We will surface recommended GPU classes (A100, H100, A10x, T4) and an initial throughput band mapping to reserved GPU count estimates — engineering to validate mappings in follow-up ticket.
Consider adding an optional field for 'acceptable fallback model variant' to enable automatic policy-level fallbacks during rollout.
comments: 2025-03-01 - Marisol Ortega: Created ticket. Drafted goal and scope. Linking initial intake prototype: https://docs.redwood.com/confluence/dedicated-intake-prototype

2025-03-03 - Ethan Park: Reviewed draft. Suggest adding a required 'latency SLO' field (p50/p95) and clear definition for 'burst' vs 'steady' throughput. I will prototype the scoring spreadsheet by 2025-03-07.

2025-03-05 - Liam Chen (Eng): Engineering input: GPU mapping table needs validation against current kernel/quantization support. Recommend ENG-2987 to own validation across model families. Also flagged potential constraint: cross-region VPC peering can increase lead time by 3-6 weeks.
2025-03-07 - Design (Aisha Kaur): Quick mock for intake form posted: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/designs/dedicated-intake-v1.pdf — kept compact, added inline tooltips for technical terms.

2025-03-09 - Ethan Park: Uploaded prioritization sheet (view-only): https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1-example. Scorecard uses weighted sum; initial thresholds: >=12 -> recommend reserved commit; 8-11 -> pilot; <8 -> follow-up/decline.

2025-03-10 - Sales engineering (Priya Nair): Field feedback: customers often ask for 99.9% uptime + p95 < 120ms. Suggest adding an 'SLA flexibility' checkbox (can accept slightly higher latency for cost savings).

2025-03-11 - Marisol Ortega: Decision: Accept framework as alpha. Next steps: run two pilots week of 2025-03-17. Assign ENG-2834 to create GPU-fit validation doc and estimate any infra work; assign Solutions to pilot two existing leads.

2025-03-12 - Ethan Park: Updated intake template in GH (internal): https://github.com/redwood-inference/internal-proposals/pull/42 — PR includes template JSON, onboarding checklist, and sample scored cases. Engineering review requested: @LiamChen @ZaraLim.

source.title: Enterprise staging pin reservation & risk-buffer playbook for model catalog onboarding
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Enterprise customers frequently request model pins and new model additions that have high business impact but varying risk profiles (latency, PII exfiltration surface, quantization quality). Current intake is binary (approve/reject) which causes either expensive emergency rollbacks or lengthy hold times. Proposal: introduce an 'Enterprise Staging Pin Reservation' playbook that creates a temporary, time-boxed pin with an attached risk-buffer and mandatory eval gates. This playbook standardizes decision criteria, operational controls, and provides a fast but safe path to grant enterprises short-term stability while the model completes full onboarding.

Key goals:
- Reduce mean time to pin approval for high-value enterprise requests from days to <48 hours for staged pins.
- Prevent long-lived, unreviewed pins by enforcing automatic expiry and deprecation triggers.
- Tie pin issuance to quantifiable eval gates (quality regression, latency, estimated cost delta, security scan).
- Provide clear rollback and fallback policies to protect customer SLAs and platform SLOs.

Proposal summary:
1) Staged Pin Reservation: when a customer with Enterprise tier requests a pin, PM can approve a staged pin valid for N days (default 14). This reservation is visible in the catalog and in the Console.
2) Risk Buffer: each staged pin includes a risk rating (Low/Medium/High) and an operational buffer—e.g., traffic throttles, dedicated fallback model, and a KV-cache warmup period.
3) Eval Gate Bundle: a mandatory checklist (automated and manual) that must be completed during the staged period to convert the staged pin into a permanent pin. Failure to pass gates triggers automatic rollback and a post-mortem.
4) Pin Expiry and Escalation: unconverted staged pins auto-expire; PM triggers escalation and customer communication.
5) Pilot: roll out to 3 enterprise customers with diverse workload profiles (chat/embeddings/rerank) before wider launch.

Why different from existing intake flows: this creates a middle lane between fast-hosted pinning and full catalog onboarding, balancing time-to-stability with controls required by compliance and SRE.
acceptance_criteria: Console UI shows 'Staged Pin' with expiry and risk rating visible to PM and customer
Default staged pin TTL set to 14 days configurable per customer or PM override
Eval gate automated checks run on submit (quality regression, latency delta, cost estimate, security scan) and populate a results dashboard
If gate fails, the system automatically reroutes traffic to the configured fallback model and creates an incident ticket template
Pilot completed with 3 customers and telemetry shows no customer-facing errors attributable to staged pins during the pilot window
rollout_steps: Week 0: Align stakeholders (PM, Eng, SRE, Security, Design). Finalize playbook and acceptance criteria.
Week 1: Build Console flags and catalog metadata for staged pins (ENG-61402)
Week 2: Implement eval gate automation integrations (quality runner, perf CI, security scan) (ENG-61990)
Week 3: Design and review customer-facing copy and in-console notices (DES-2561)
Week 4: Internal dogfood with Redwood apps; iterate on TTL/risk defaults
Week 5-6: Pilot with 3 enterprise customers, collect telemetry and SLO impact
Week 7: Post-pilot review, security sign-off, and production rollout schedule
eval_gates: Quality regression: run customer-specific prompts + golden-set; require pass rate >= 95% vs baseline
Latency: 95th percentile latency change <= 15% of baseline or within agreed SLA
Cost delta: projected token cost increase <= customer allowance or pre-approved threshold
Security/PII scan: static model intake scan + threat review; any high findings must be mitigated
Observability hooks: tracing, per-token cost attribution, and error-rate alerting enabled for the staged pin
scoring_rubric: Composite risk score = weighted sum: Security(40%), Quality regression(30%), Latency(20%), Cost(10%)
Operational controls per band: Low - standard staging; Medium - throttled traffic + dedicated fallback; High - require SRE oncall approval and canary 5% traffic cap
Conversion threshold: to convert staged->permanent, risk score must be <=35 and all eval gates must be green
Emergency rollback threshold: any single security critical finding or quality regression that causes >5% unseen hallucination rate triggers auto-rollback
comments: 2025-02-10 Ava Chen: Created ticket after PM & SRE sync. Initial draft playbook attached. Asked Eng for rough estimate.
2025-02-12 Marcus Patel: Prioritized as P1 for enterprise runway; assigned to Marcus. Suggested 14-day default TTL and pilot with 3 customers.
2025-02-18 Engineering triage (ENG-61402): Confirmed console flags required; estimate 3 dev weeks + 1 week for integration testing.
2025-02-22 Design feedback (DES-2561): Recommend in-console copy 'Staged Pin — temporary stability window' with clear expiry and customer CTA to request extension.
2025-02-27 Security review kickoff: Security to run model intake scanner and provide gating thresholds. Action: Security to provide 'red-line' criteria by 2025-03-07.
2025-03-03 Marcus Patel: Updated acceptance criteria to include automated gate results dashboard and incident template on failures. Added ENG-61990 as dependency for automation.
2025-03-07 Security note: Preliminary scan rules agreed. High-risk plugins and external data access are automatic fail conditions. Security to join pilot retros.
2025-03-09 PM sync: Decided pilot customers: Acme Health (regulatory-heavy), FinNova (low-latency trading), and EchoScribe (transcription). Schedule invites sent.
2025-03-12 Marcus Patel: Engineering started work on console flags; created GitHub PR #10987 for staged pin metadata schema. Link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/10987
2025-03-12 Ava Chen: Noted communication plan: PM to send customer onboarding docs + expectations template. Draft: https://drive.redwood.com/docs/staged-pin-playbook

source.title: Geofence AuditStream: automated control-to-artifact mapping for data residency & retention evidence
source.creator: Aisha Kapoor
source.description:
Goal: build an automated 'AuditStream' that maps residency and retention controls to tamper-evident artifacts so SOC 2 / ISO 27001 auditors and customers can rapidly verify compliance for region-bound datasets.

Scope: produce evidence for three common control families: (1) Geofencing (data never leaves region X), (2) Retention policy enforcement (data deleted after N days, retention exceptions recorded), (3) Evidence collection & signing (artifact generation, hash anchoring, and access claims). This ticket covers the PM-level orchestration, control mapping matrix, acceptance criteria, and rollout plan. Implementation split into collectors (cloud telemetry, object metadata sweeps), an aggregator service that assembles artifacts, and a signer/manifest service that timestamps and stores proofs in a tamper-evident store.

Design notes: artifacts will be compact JSON manifests containing: data scope descriptor, selector queries, evidence snapshot (counts, sample keys), retention state, KMS-wrapped signature, and a digest anchored to an append-only store (e.g., ledger S3 with object lock + digest index). Collector triggers: scheduled (daily) + on-demand (customer request / legal hold). Evidence TTL: artifacts retained for 7 years by default for audit trails but surfaced to customers with configurable retention windows in the console.

Non-goals: this ticket does not include UI polish for the public customer-facing evidence packages nor Dedicated on-prem packaging; those will be follow-ups.
acceptance_criteria: Control mapping matrix created and reviewed by Security and Legal with explicit SOC 2 / ISO 27001 control IDs
PoC collector implemented that produces residency manifests for AWS S3 buckets in two regions (us-east-1, eu-west-1)
Aggregator composes manifests and stores them in the tamper-evident store with KMS-wrapped signatures
Console endpoint that lists generated artifacts and exposes an exportable ZIP for auditor download
Automated end-to-end test that validates manifest digest matches stored anchored digest
Runbook documenting how to produce ad-hoc evidence for a customer, including legal escalation steps
comments: 2025-03-02 - Kickoff: aligned scope with Security (Lina Morales) and Legal (Marcus Li). Decided to anchor digests in immutable S3 + periodic Merkle root snapshot rather than a blockchain to avoid vendor lock-in.

2025-03-04 - Architecture sync: agreed on three collector types: telemetry (CloudTrail/CloudAudit), object-sweep (S3 metadata + tags), and retention-enforcer hooks (lifecycle job logs). ENG to prototype collectors as separate microservices.

2025-03-07 - Tradeoffs: sampling vs full-scan. For large buckets we will capture statistical samples plus a hashed bloom-filter of object keys to keep manifests small. Security requested additional sample sealing to increase trustability.
2025-03-09 - PR feedback: Security review requested proof of KMS key rotation handling in signatures (ENG-2210). Added dependency to rotation task.
2025-03-11 - Demo: PoC collector produced residency manifests for a test dataset. Found issue where lifecycle deletes are soft deletes in test env; added test to surface tombstone records.
decision_log: Chosen evidence anchor: S3 object lock + append-only digest index (v1) to balance immutability and operational simplicity
Artifact format: small JSON manifests with KMS-wrapped signature; no PII included — manifests reference selectors and hashed samples
Collector cadence: daily for most workloads, hourly for 'high-governance' customers behind Dedicated
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Large object sets make full manifests too large. Mitigation: use probabilistic sampling + bloom filter + manifest sharding by prefix.
Risk: KMS rotation invalidates signatures. Mitigation: include key-id/version in manifest and store rotation provenance; add re-signing job for historical artifacts if required.
Risk: Customers expect raw logs. Mitigation: provide an export option and a documented minimal dataset customers can request; escalate to Legal for sensitive requests.

ENG-2142 - Build telemetry collector for CloudTrail and CloudAudit events
ENG-2210 - Implement signer/manifest service with KMS integration and rotation handling
ENG-2245 - Implement object-sweep collector with sampling + bloom filter
DES-412 - Create minimal console view for artifact listing and download
PM-17347 - Draft runbook and auditor export process

title: Geofence AuditStream: automated control-to-artifact mapping for data residency & retention evidence
preview:
Goal: build an automated 'AuditStream' that maps residency and retention controls to tamper-evident artifacts so SOC 2 / ISO 27001 auditors and customers can rapidly verify compliance for region-bound datasets.

Scope: produce evidence for three common control families: (1) Geofencing (data never leaves region X), (2) Retention policy enforcement (data deleted after N days, retention exceptions recorded), (3) Evidence collection & signing (artifact generation, hash anchoring, and access claims). This ticket covers the PM-level orchestration, control mapping matrix, acceptance criteria, and rollout plan. Implementation split into collectors (cloud telemetry, object metadata sweeps), an aggregator service that assembles artifacts, and a signer/manifest service that timestamps and stores proofs in a tamper-evident store.

Design notes: artifacts will be compact JSON manifests containing: data scope descriptor, selector queries, evidence snapshot (counts, sample keys), retention state, KMS-wrapped signature, and a digest anchored to an append-only store (e.g., ledger S3 with object lock + digest index). Collector triggers: scheduled (daily) + on-demand (customer request / legal hold). Evidence TTL: artifacts retained for 7 years by default for audit trails but surfaced to customers with configurable retention windows in the console.

Non-goals: this ticket does not include UI polish for the public customer-facing evidence packages nor Dedicated on-prem packaging; those will be follow-ups.
