source.title: Hybrid metering SKU and transient pricing phases for mixed token+compute plans
source.creator: Diego Morales
source.description: PRD to define how Redwood bills hybrid plans that combine per-token usage with per-model compute (GPU-minutes) under a single customer SKU, including transient pricing phases when customers change plans mid-cycle and a customer-facing invoice preview experience.
source.background: Enterprise customers have asked for predictable bundles that include both token allowances and reserved compute minutes for dedicated runs. Current billing separates token metering (hosted API) and reserved compute (Dedicated), which leads to poor visibility on mixed workloads, confusing invoices when customers switch plans mid-cycle, and inability to offer short-term promotional compute credits. This PRD defines SKU mapping, metering semantics, rounding/proration logic, and acceptance criteria for an invoice preview tool.
goals: Define canonical SKU that maps token-units + compute-minutes + optional priority-credit into a single plan and invoice line
Specify metering attribution so each request is charged to the correct SKU with model variant granularity
Implement transient pricing phases for plan changes with clear proration rules
Expose a customer invoice preview API and Console preview that matches final production invoices within reconciliation tolerance
Auditability: every billed charge must be traceable to request ids and reconciliation buckets
scope_in: SKU schema: fields for token_unit_price, compute_minute_price, included_token_allowance, included_compute_allowance, priority_credit_allocation
Per-request attribution: include model_id, sequence_length_estimate, hardware_class, request_id in metering records
Proration rules when plan changes mid-cycle (start-of-day boundaries and pro-rata on UTC date)
Customer invoice preview API and Console UI (read-only) that uses the same aggregation pipeline as billing
Backfill/reconciliation rules for short-term promotional compute credits (voucher style)
Billing audit logs and query endpoints for CS/Finance
scope_out: Changes to core tokenization logic or model pricing tables (existing model catalog is authoritative)
Automated refunds; only reconciliation carries credit entries for manual adjustments by Finance
Deep integrations with third-party accounting systems (post-PRD connectors to be scoped separately)
requirements: 1) Metering records must contain: timestamp (ISO8601), request_id, api_key_id, customer_id, model_id, variant_tag, estimated_tokens_in, estimated_tokens_out, compute_ms, hardware_tier, route_id
2) Aggregation logic: hourly rollups by customer_id x SKU where compute_ms converts to compute_minutes and round-up to nearest 0.01 minute for billing precision
3) SKU mapping: Plan object must reference numeric SKU codes; each line item can carry included allowances which decrement before billed overage
4) Transient pricing: when customer switches plan mid-cycle the system must create a transient phase record and prorate both allowances and per-unit prices from change_timestamp to cycle_end
5) Invoice preview must apply same rounding/proration and output line-itemized JSON and human-readable breakdown matching production invoice within +/-0.5% for test traces
6) Audit trail: every billed line must link to at least one aggregation window id and a sample set of request_ids for up to 1000 requests
7) Backfill credits: voucher and promotional compute credits must be expensed to a separate ledger code and visible as separate line items
acceptance_criteria: AC-1: End-to-end demo: create a customer on hybrid plan, generate mixed traffic (50% token dominated, 50% compute-heavy model runs), run invoice preview and generate final invoice in staging; line items match within 0.5%
AC-2: Mid-cycle plan change: simulate plan downgrade on day 10 of 30, preview invoice shows proration that matches manual calculation and final invoice applies the same amounts
AC-3: Attribution: for any billed line item Finance can query a link and retrieve up to 1000 request_ids that contributed to the charge
AC-4: Rounding rules applied: compute minutes rounded to 0.01 minute, token units billed per 100-token bucket with last-bucket rounding up; verified with unit tests covering boundary cases
AC-5: Promo credits: apply a 500 compute-minute voucher, preview displays voucher as separate negative line and final invoice consumes voucher before overage charges
AC-6: Console preview parity: Console preview UI numbers match invoice preview API output within tolerance and have identical line-item labels
AC-7: Scalability: aggregation pipeline can process a 100k requests/day test workload and produce preview within 2 minutes for enterprise customers
success_metrics: Pilot customers (n=3) adopt hybrid SKU within 60 days
Invoice dispute rate for affected customers < 0.5% in first billing cycle
Invoice preview produced in <2 minutes for 99th percentile of pilot customers
Finance reconciliation variance <0.5% between preview and post-process invoice in staging
rollout_plan: Phase 1 (Beta internal): implement metering + SKU mapping and run internal test customers (2 weeks)
Phase 2 (Pilot external): invite 3 enterprise customers, collect feedback and reconcile billing in pilot accounts (4 weeks)
Phase 3 (GA): expose SKU in self-serve plans and Console, enable invoice preview for all customers on hybrid SKUs (2 weeks)
Phase 4: Post-GA: telemetry and usage-driven refinements, refund playbook and Finance runbook rollout (ongoing)
testing_plan: Unit tests for aggregation primitives and proration math (edge case fuzzing)
Integration test that powers invoice preview and final invoice generation against produced event stream
Load test with synthetic traffic generator: 100k req/day per pilot customer and verify preview latency
Audit test to verify request_id->billing-line linking and that logs retain 30 days of sample ids
risks: Incorrect proration math could lead to invoice disputes and manual refunds
High-cardinality attribution could increase storage and slow aggregation; mitigation: capped sample ids per window
Differences between preview and final invoice if aggregation path diverges; mitigation: single canonical pipeline for preview + billing
Complex SKU combinations may confuse sales; require clear labeling and UI tooltips
decisions: Use UTC day boundaries for proration windows to avoid timezone complexity
Round compute to 0.01 minute and tokens to 100-token buckets for billing simplicity
Invoice preview will be read-only; customers must contact CS for retrospective adjustments
Promo vouchers are separate ledger codes and are consumed prior to overage charge calculation
notes: Meeting 2025-03-04: Finance prefers line-itemized invoices that can be consumed by automated ledger import
Design review 2025-03-06: Console preview should include an expandable trace sample for each line item
Engineering asked for a conservative rollout with a feature flag to switch preview on per-customer basis

source.title: Migration Accelerator: Beta Cohorts, In-Product Nudge Flows, and Release Notes Kit
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Deliver a reusable Migration Accelerator playbook and asset kit to make deprecations predictable, low-friction, and measurable for customers. This ticket covers three deliverables: 1) Beta cohort selection and operations checklist (segmentation, invite cadence, success criteria); 2) In-product nudge flows and SDK warnings (banner copy, throttled reminders, SDK deprecation warnings, telemetry hooks); 3) Release notes kit and GTM cadence (email templates, blog + changelog entries, support-runbook, PRD for console surfaced warnings).

Scope: We are planning a staged sunset of api/v1-models to api/v2-models for a subset of legacy model variants. This is not a full platform-wide retirement. Workstreams include: cohort design (which customers to enroll for early migration assistance), in-product UX (console banners, modal flows), SDK/CLI update strategy (deprecation headers and opt-in shims), comms (multi-channel templates), metrics (migration funnel tracking, error/regression monitors), and rollback/fallback policies (automatic model-variant fallback + SLO gates).

Success metrics: 60% of targeted cohort migrated within 6 weeks of invite; <1% spike in error-rate attributable to migration; customer satisfaction NPS change <= -3; migration automation reduces manual support touches by 50% for enrolled customers.

Non-goals: This ticket does not include private deployment upgrade tooling (that's covered under PM-PRIVATE-UPGRADES) nor changes to runtime kernel selection. It also does not change pricing or hard cutover dates for non-enrolled customers.

Key stakeholders: Product (owner), Solutions Eng (migration concierge), DevRel (migration guides), Docs (release notes templates), Support (runbook + escalation path), Eng Reliability (telemetry + rollback), Legal/Trust (if required for customer comms).
comments: 2025-01-24 - Ava Chen: Kickoff meeting notes uploaded to Confluence. Decided to run two parallel beta cohorts (high-traffic platform customers and mid-market self-serve power users). Action: Solutions Eng to draft concierge process.

2025-01-30 - Marcus Rivera: Draft cohort segmentation spreadsheet shared. Suggested success criteria and migration milestones. Requesting data from Analytics to validate cohort size estimates (assigned to Priya S.).

2025-02-12 - Priya Shah: Analytics smoke test complete. Candidate cohort sizes look feasible: 18 enterprise accounts + ~120 self-serve customers based on daily token volume and supported model variants.
2025-02-18 - Marina Ortiz (Docs): First pass of release notes templates and in-console banner copy posted. Need engineering review for tokenized variable names and character limits in banner UI.

2025-02-25 - Eng Reliability (review): Telemetry plan approved. Added migration funnel events: migration_invite_sent, migration_opt_in, migration_start, migration_complete, migration_failure. Also added rollback gates: >2% increase in 5xx across cohort for 10 minute window => automatic pause of invites.
2025-03-03 - Marcus Rivera: Legal reviewed customer email cadence and approved base templates. Added a special privacy blurb for EU customers referencing KMS integration details.
2025-03-06 - Design review: In-product nudge flows completed; prototype in Figma link attached. UX asks: add a single 'Remind me later' option and avoid modal overload for self-serve cohort.

2025-03-10 - Ava Chen: Updated scope to include an SDK minor release note with deprecation header and opt-in compatibility shim. Planning rollout: invite cohort week starting 2025-04-01, broader staged notices begin 2025-05-01.

2025-03-10 - Marcus Rivera: Blockers: Need final approval from Solutions Eng for concierge resourcing, and engineering to land the SDK warning header by 2025-03-25. Action items assigned and added to dependencies.

source.title: Competitive compass: translating model-landscape signals into procurement & fallback decisions
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: As the open-model ecosystem proliferates, our commercial teams and platform ops need a repeatable way to map external signals about model availability, pricing, latency, and feature drift into concrete procurement actions (reserve vs hosted), pricing guidance, and automated fallback rules. Scope: produce a signal-to-decision matrix, an operational rubric that weights signals (e.g., availability volatility, quantization quality, regional latency delta, contractual SLA gaps), and a one-page procurement decision playbook that can be used by PMs, sales-engineering, and Platform SREs. Deliverables: 1) prioritized signal taxonomy with weights and examples, 2) decision matrix mapping signal bands to procurement actions and fallback policies, 3) playbook doc + template RFP checklist for enterprise deals, 4) instrumentation spec for ops (what to surface in Console and Alerts). This ticket is to run the cross-functional discovery, synthesize the first iteration, and ship the playbook artifact for review.
acceptance_criteria: Signal taxonomy (CSV-friendly) with at least 12 signals and recommended weights is published to Drive
Decision matrix (PDF + markdown) that maps signal bands to one of: use-hosted, reserved-dedicated, private-deploy, or implement fallback-tiering
Procurement playbook (one-pager) reviewed and approved by Sales Engineering and Legal
Instrumentation spec is created and assigned to Infra for a small Console prototype (token-level cost + latency deltas)
tasks: Interview 6 stakeholders: 2 PMs, 2 SalesEng, 1 Legal, 1 Platform SRE
Collect model-availability historical signals for top 8 suppliers (ops + scouting team)
Design weighting rubric for signals and validate with two pilot deals
Draft the decision matrix and playbook; internal review with SalesEng
Create instrumentation spec and open follow-up infra ticket(s)
Publish final playbook to Confluence and notify GTM and Platform teams
comments: 2025-02-18 Ava Chen: Kickoff - scoped deliverables and identified stakeholders. Assigned Diego to lead synthesis. Noted overlap with PM-314160 (landscape differential mapping); will coordinate to avoid duplicated signal definitions.

2025-02-24 Diego Morales: Completed stakeholder interviews with SalesEng and Platform SRE. Early theme: customers weigh latency and regional presence more heavily than raw model perplexity. Sales wants a simple triage: 'fast fallback' vs 'procure reserved'.

2025-02-27 Diego Morales: Received ops export of availability/headroom for top 6 suppliers (CSV link in artifacts). Preliminary analysis shows two suppliers have >12% availability variance month-over-month in APAC — flags for procurement.

2025-03-01 Legal (Maya Patel): Reviewed procurement playbook draft. High-level feedback: need explicit data retention and indemnity checklist for private deployments. Legal will provide boilerplate for RFP checklist.
2025-03-03 SalesEng (Tom Rivers): Requested the matrix include price-sensitivity bands tied to committed-usage thresholds to make trade-offs explicit for negotiations.

2025-03-05 Diego Morales: Draft matrix v0.1 uploaded to Drive (link below). Proposed signal weighting rubric attached. Decision: finalize weights in next week and run two pilot deal simulations with SalesEng to validate recommended actions.
notes: Tradeoffs: aggressive weighting toward availability reduces vendor pool but simplifies SLA planning; heavier weight on quantization quality preserves end-user output quality but increases unit cost risk.
Pilot plan: run two negotiations — one SMB (price-sensitive) and one enterprise (compliance-sensitive) — to validate mapping between signal bands and procurement action chosen.
Risk: if instrumentation is slow to land in Console, Sales will adopt an ad-hoc heuristic. Mitigation: provide a compact spreadsheet template for immediate use.

source.title: Demand heatmap + feasibility snapshot for Sales/CS enterprise asks
source.creator: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Create a compact, repeatable intake artifact that Sales/CS can use to consolidate enterprise feature requests into a demand heatmap and a one-page feasibility snapshot. The goal is to reduce lead time from first-sales-ask to an actionable PM/Eng scoping decision from weeks to days. This ticket delivers: 1) an intake template for frontline reps that balances signal capture with low friction, 2) an automated scoring rubric (ARR, #accounts, strategic fit, legal/regulatory risk, infra/runtime compatibility), 3) a template "feasibility snapshot" that includes rough effort band, risks, suggested model/runtime path (hosted / dedicated / private), and 4) a simple dashboard CSV schema for aggregation and heatmap generation.
acceptance_criteria: A single-page intake form that Sales/CS can complete in <10 minutes
Scoring rubric documented with example calculations and thresholds
Feasibility snapshot template produced (PDF/Confluence) with sections: summary, ask frequency, technical constraints, rough effort band, priority score, next recommended action
Automated CSV export schema defined and validated against 10 historical requests
Pilot run with two AE teams and Solutions engineering; at least one prioritized ask moved to formal scoping within 7 days
comments: 2025-02-11 — Kickoff: synced with AE leads (Camille, Jorge) to map current signal flows and pain points. Agreed low-friction form is critical; avoid long free-text fields.

2025-02-18 — Draft rubric posted to Confluence: weight ARR 40%, #accounts 20%, strategic fit 20%, implementation complexity 10%, data/regulatory risk 10%. Feedback from Sales: prefer ARR buckets rather than raw values.

2025-02-25 — Eng triage (ENG-91421) provided effort bands: small (1-3 wks), medium (3-8 wks), large (8+wks). Noted runtime compatibility flags: KV-cache dependency, dedicated-only features, and private deployment constraints.
2025-03-02 — Design reviewed snapshot visual (DES-344): replaced long SLA table with iconography; added 'quick-scan' checkbox for sensitive-data asks.
2025-03-05 — Pilot kickoff scheduled with AE-East and Solutions-Data on 2025-03-10. Action: create Drive folder and sample CSV with 10 historical rows from Zendesk/Intercom exports for validation.
decision_log: 2025-02-18 — Decision: use ARR buckets (<$50k, $50k-$250k, $250k+) instead of raw values to simplify scoring (Priya, Marco, Camille)
2025-02-25 — Decision: effort bands validated by Eng and surfaced in snapshot; do not include dev-estimates in intake form to avoid false precision (Marco, Lin)
2025-03-02 — Decision: pilot will treat "security/regulatory" as binary flag that forces escalation to Security when true (Priya, Sasha-Sec)
2025-03-04 — Decision: initial dashboard will be a CSV schema + Google Sheet with heatmap; longer term we may wire into Console signals pipeline (Product/Eng roadmap discuss Q2)
next_steps: Finalize intake form and publish to Sales enablement (owner: Marco) — due 2025-03-07
Populate sample CSV from historical asks and validate scoring thresholds (owner: Solutions-Data) — due 2025-03-09
Run pilot sessions with two AE teams and collect 20 new intake submissions (owner: Camille) — due 2025-03-17
Debrief pilot and convert top 3 asks into formal scoping tickets with ENG-91421 linkage (owner: Priya) — due 2025-03-20

source.title: Pinning playbook: operator UX and policies for model-version pin + graceful fallback
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: As teams adopt model version pinning and staged rollouts (canary / A-B), interactions across region selection, capacity tiers (hosted/dedicated/private), and model variant fallbacks create subtle production behaviors that increase error rates, latency variability, and surprise cost exposure. This ticket proposes a product playbook and operator UX requirements to make pinning + rollout behavior explicit, safe, and observable for customers.

Goals:
- Define deterministic policy semantics that compose: region preference, tier ordering (dedicated -> hosted -> private fallback), version pin TTL and cohort rules, and model-variant degrade paths.
- Surface these policies in Console and API with clear defaults and override knobs for advanced users.
- Provide monitoring, alerts, and bounded cost/latency SLAs during rollouts.
- Ship a customer-facing runbook "Pin + Rollout Safety Playbook" and an initial Console UI to visualize active pin cohorts and fallback chains.

Scope (deliverables):
1) Playbook doc that describes canonical behaviors and operator decisions.
2) API params & Console UI spec for: pin TTL, cohort seed percentage, deterministic vs probabilistic affinity, sticky session window, fallback priority list (region, tier, variant).
3) Default policy recommendations and safe guardrails (e.g., max canary qps, cost cap, automatic rollback triggers).
4) E2E acceptance tests and dashboards: per-cohort latency/cost/regression meters.
5) Migration notes for customers using legacy pin semantics.

Non-goals: Full implementation of server-side deterministic shard pinning (engineering will implement separately). Initial Console work should be read-only visualization + controls for enabling playbook policies and toggles; advanced runtime behavior implemented via integration with orchestrator RFCs.
acceptance_criteria: Playbook doc published to Confluence and linked from Console help
API spec for pinning and fallback parameters reviewed and approved by Eng and Design
Console mockups for: cohort explorer, active fallback graph, TTL control, and emergency rollback control accepted by Design
Safety defaults implemented as server-side policy (e.g., default pin TTL=24h, canary max=5% qps, cost cap alerts) and validated in a staging rollout
Dashboards showing cohort-level latency, cost per token, error rate, and model-quality delta available in Redwood Console
notes: Key considerations: deterministic vs probabilistic cohort assignment (sticky affinity), entropy cost of large cohort splits, and how prefix/KV cache locality is affected by frequent pin churn.
We should recommend opaque session keys for customers that need strong stickiness, but provide lower-friction probabilistic split for typical PLG usage.
Fallback semantics must explicitly encode: priority ordering (region -> tier -> variant), maximum degrade steps, and explicit tie-breakers when multiple fallbacks are eligible.
Recommend a safety path: automated rollback when model-quality regression exceeds 3 standard deviations on sentry-eval or when latency p95 increases by >40% for canary cohort for 15m.
Cross-team dependencies: Eng (routing/orchestrator), Design (Console visuals), Security (audit logging for pin changes).
comments: 2025-02-20 Diego Morales: Initial scoping review — want to align on the default TTL. Proposal: 24h for session-focused apps, 1h for ephemeral API keys. Will validate with Observability team.

2025-02-24 Priya Singh (Design): Drafts for cohort explorer ready. We need product to confirm which attributes are most valuable: created_at, cohort_size, seed_percent, current_fallback_chain, rollback_button.

2025-03-03 Marco Alvarez (Eng): Routing team can support a read-only fallback graph in Console by exposing routing policy metadata. Full enforcement of probabilistic vs deterministic modes requires orchestrator hooks — propose a follow-on ENG ticket after PM signoff.
2025-03-05 Ava Chen: Added canary safety thresholds. Also attaching customer ask: large fintech customer requested explicit cost cap per rollout — include in playbook.

Customer comms template for rollout postmortems
implementation_risks: Complexity of ensuring KV cache locality when many short-lived pins are created — may increase latency for affected requests.
Cost leakage if customers enable large canary percentages without cost caps — need telemetry-driven safeguards.
Ambiguity between region preference and capacity tier when a model variant exists in multiple regions; must define explicit tie-breaker in policy.
source.runbook_summary: If a rollout causes regression: 1) Pause new cohort seeding via Console toggle; 2) Trigger automated rollback to last-known-good version; 3) If rollback unavailable, promote fallback variant in higher-priority tier and open incident; 4) Notify customer and escalate to SRE. Documented thresholds and playbook available in Confluence.
source.owner_notes: After playbook signoff, we will schedule a cross-functional working session to translate policy knobs to API params and to prioritize the engineering RFC for enforcement. Target pilot with two strategic customers (fintech and ecommerce) in Q2.
audit_requirements: All pin changes must emit audit event with actor, timestamp, reason (optional), and previous policy snapshot.

title: Pinning playbook: operator UX and policies for model-version pin + graceful fallback
preview:
Problem: As teams adopt model version pinning and staged rollouts (canary / A-B), interactions across region selection, capacity tiers (hosted/dedicated/private), and model variant fallbacks create subtle production behaviors that increase error rates, latency variability, and surprise cost exposure. This ticket proposes a product playbook and operator UX requirements to make pinning + rollout behavior explicit, safe, and observable for customers.

Goals:
- Define deterministic policy semantics that compose: region preference, tier ordering (dedicated -> hosted -> private fallback), version pin TTL and cohort rules, and model-variant degrade paths.
- Surface these policies in Console and API with clear defaults and override knobs for advanced users.
- Provide monitoring, alerts, and bounded cost/latency SLAs during rollouts.
- Ship a customer-facing runbook "Pin + Rollout Safety Playbook" and an initial Console UI to visualize active pin cohorts and fallback chains.

Scope (deliverables):
1) Playbook doc that describes canonical behaviors and operator decisions.
2) API params & Console UI spec for: pin TTL, cohort seed percentage, deterministic vs probabilistic affinity, sticky session window, fallback priority list (region, tier, variant).
3) Default policy recommendations and safe guardrails (e.g., max canary qps, cost cap, automatic rollback triggers).
4) E2E acceptance tests and dashboards: per-cohort latency/cost/regression meters.
5) Migration notes for customers using legacy pin semantics.

Non-goals: Full implementation of server-side deterministic shard pinning (engineering will implement separately). Initial Console work should be read-only visualization + controls for enabling playbook policies and toggles; advanced runtime behavior implemented via integration with orchestrator RFCs.

source.title: Observability-driven acceptance for streamed function-call flows and failure classification
source.creator: Aisha Patel
source.description: Define clear, testable acceptance criteria for structured output and function/tool-calling behavior in streaming responses, with an emphasis on observable failure classification and graceful degradation. This ticket captures requirements that the runtime, gateway, and SDKs must satisfy before we mark the feature GA. The goal is to protect downstream consumers (apps and analytics) from silently corrupted or partial tool invocations and to provide deterministic signals for operators to triage issues quickly.
acceptance_criteria: Framing: Streaming responses that emit structured events (tool_call, schema_frame, checkpoint, text_token) must use a deterministic envelope with a sequence_id and stream_id on every frame so reordering and partial reconstructions are traceable.

Schema validation: Any tool_call frame must include a validated 'args' JSON matching the registered tool schema; if validation fails, the runtime emits a 'schema_error' frame with an error_code and continues (soft-fail) unless configured as strict (configurable per-route).

Idempotency keys: All tool_call frames must include a dedup_key (UUID v4 or customer-provided token). Retries from the client or gateway must preserve dedup_key; server-side retries must not cause duplicate downstream execution if dedup_key matches prior successful call.

Checkpoint & resume: The stream must emit periodic checkpoint frames with a resume_token; resumed streams using a resume_token must produce no duplicate final side-effects for acknowledged tool calls. Resume_token format must be documented in the PRD and stable across minor releases.

Failure classification: Implement three canonical error buckets propagated in telemetry and frames: transient (tool timed out, transient network), permanent (schema violation, auth failure to tool), and unknown (corrupted frame). Each emitted error frame includes error_class, error_code, and suggested_action (retry / manual / fallback).

Fallback behavior: For high-severity errors (permanent) the system should surface a structured fallback_action frame describing fallback mode (text-only, stubbed-tool-response, noop) and the client SDK must surface a non-200 terminal status for callers unless configured for silent degrade.

Observability metrics: At minimum provide per-route metrics: tool_call_total, tool_call_success_rate, tool_call_latency_p50/p95, schema_violation_rate, stream_resume_rate, partial_frame_rate. Metrics must tag by model_version, route, stream_region, and error_class.

Tracing & logs: Every tool_call must carry a correlation_id propagated across model->gateway->tool with logs including sequence_id and dedup_key to enable end-to-end reconstruction. Trace spans should capture model_emit_time, gateway_ack_time, tool_start_time, tool_end_time.
Alerting: Define default alert thresholds: schema_violation_rate > 0.5% over 5m, tool_call_success_rate < 98% 5m, resume_rate > 0.1% 1h. Alerts must include sample stream IDs and top error_codes.

E2E test coverage: Synthetic tests must inject truncated frames, corrupted args JSON, and tool timeouts; the system must classify errors correctly, emit the expected frames, and preserve idempotency semantics in replay scenarios.
implementation_notes: Trade-offs discussed: strict schema enforcement reduces consumer surprises but increases availability risk; default policy will be 'soft-validate' with per-route opt-in to strict mode for regulated customers.
Runtime responsibilities: attach sequence_id/stream_id in kernel before first token is emitted; expose hook to attach resume_token at checkpoint intervals.
Gateway responsibilities: acknowledge receipt of tool_call within 250ms with an ack frame; escalate to fallback if ack not received in 2s.
SDK responsibilities: surface structured frames in high-level streaming API and expose resume_token handling for client-side retries.
Telemetry: instrument counters and histograms in the runtime and gateway; provide sample dashboards and pre-built alerts in the Console.
Privacy/security: do not log full tool arguments by default; logs must contain redacted_args and a hash fingerprint for debugging.
comments: 2025-02-21 - Aisha Patel: Drafted initial acceptance criteria after pairing with runtime team. Emphasized resume_token stability and idempotency requirements.
2025-02-25 - Marcus Lin: Added observability metrics and alert thresholds requested by SRE. Flagged need for redaction guidance in logs (security).
2025-02-28 - Priya Narayanan (SRE): Reviewed alert thresholds; requested lowering schema_violation alert sensitivity for initial rollout to reduce noise; agreed to 0.5% after warmup.
2025-03-03 - Daniel Park (Eng lead): Confirmed gateway ack semantics (250ms) and failover window (2s). Raised question about checkpoint frequency trade-offs vs token overhead.
2025-03-05 - Marcus Lin: Marked In Review, added links to PR and Grafana. Requested PM design review for SDK surface area before merge.
test_plan: Unit: schema validator tests for every registered tool schema.
Integration: simulate model emitting interleaved text and tool_call frames, verify reconstruction and dedup behavior.
Chaos: inject network partition between gateway and tool, verify error_class transitions and fallback frames.
Performance: measure p95 end-to-end latency for successful tool calls under production-like load.
Canary: enable strict-mode on 1% of traffic for 48h and monitor schema_violation_rate and user errors.
rollout_plan: Phase 1: Implement soft-validate schema enforcement + telemetry; enable on staging.
Phase 2: Add resume_token checkpointing and SDK resume API; run integration tests with partners.
Phase 3: Enable strict-mode opt-in and provide migration docs; begin canary rollout (1% -> 10% -> 100%).
Phase 4: GA with dashboards and alerting defaults; post-launch 2-week close monitoring and rapid rollback playbook.

source.title: Reservation authorization and capacity workbench for Dedicated pools
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Define and implement a bounded, auditable reservation request and approval workflow plus an operator workbench that ties throughput guarantees, autoscaling policy templates, and fleet controls into the Dedicated provisioning lifecycle. This ticket scopes the PM deliverables: request form schema, approval matrix, capacity planning heuristics, UX sketches for the workbench, and the verification playbook for provisioning events.

Context: Several early customers are adopting Dedicated and ask for a single place to request guaranteed throughput (tokens/sec or requests/sec), choose autoscaling behaviors (aggressive, balanced, conservative), and indicate fleet constraints (allowed regions, GPU families, maintenance windows). Engineers want predictable capacity sizing and test hooks to validate SLOs after an allocation. Current ad-hoc requests cause back-and-forth between sales, SRE, and infra and increase lead time.

Scope (what we will deliver in this ticket):
- Definitive reservation request schema (fields, validations, default values).
- Approval matrix that maps request attributes (size, contract value, security needs) to required approvers and SLAs.
- Autoscaling policy templates (3 presets + custom) with recommended defaults for GPU pool sizes, target utilization, cooldowns, burst capacity and drain behavior.
- Fleet control knobs and constraints (tagging, allowed racks/regions, maintenance windows, preemption policy).
- Capacity planning heuristics and forecasting doc to convert expected QPS/seq_len/traffic profile into GPU unit reservation and cost estimate.
- Operator workbench wireframes and flows (review queue, approve/deny with comments, rollback, test harness link).
- Verification & onboarding checklist (smoke tests, perf benchmarks, KV cache warmup, canary traffic ramp).

Out of scope: billing contract language, long-term SLA legal framework, deep infra implementation (this ticket produces the product spec and acceptance tests; ENG tickets will follow).
acceptance_criteria: Reservation request form JSON schema reviewed and approved by Eng and Sales
Approval matrix documented with approver roles and thresholds
Three autoscaling policy templates defined with clear parameters and recommended defaults
Capacity sizing heuristic documented with worked examples (small/medium/large customers)
Workbench wireframes in Figma linked and validated by Design
Verification checklist for post-provisioning smoke and performance checks
Handoff doc and JIRA/Linear ENG tickets created for implementation
decisions: Use percentiles+seq_length model for initial capacity heuristics rather than request-level token estimates to reduce estimator variance
Provide three autoscaling presets: conservative (min-guarantee focus), balanced (utilization + cost tradeoff), and elastic (prioritize latency with higher burst capacity)
Approval matrix will include a special path for security-sensitive customers requiring Private deployment that triggers Security review
Workbench will surface cost estimates and estimated lead time to reduce back-and-forth in approvals
comments: 2025-02-11 - Rafael Ortega: Initial draft of request schema attached. Waiting on Eng to validate GPU-unit conversion factors.
2025-02-18 - Lina Park (Eng Lead): Reviewed sizing heuristics; recommended adding an explicit KV cache warmup step to verification checklist.
2025-02-22 - Jamie Wu (Design): Uploaded first-pass wireframes; added a compact review queue view and inline cost breakdown card.
2025-02-26 - Sales - Priya Menon: Requested a field for 'business priority' and expected go-live window to help SRE prioritize requests.

2025-03-01 - Rafael Ortega: Updated approval matrix with thresholds: requests < 2x baseline and < $5k/month — auto-approve by Platform Ops; larger requires PM + SRE; Private/on-prem always requires Security.
2025-03-03 - Ava Chen: Added acceptance criteria examples and marked security review required. Linking to ENG tickets for implementation planning.
notes_and_tradeoffs: We prioritize operator simplicity over exposing every autoscaling knob to customers. Start with presets and allow advanced customers to request custom policies through the approval flow.
Capacity heuristics will target 95th percentile workload characteristics. This limits over-provisioning but may require spot adjustments after empirical telemetry in first 30 days.
We will commit to documenting expected lead times but not hard SLA dates until infra automation reduces manual steps.
Cost estimates will be conservative (10-20% buffer) to avoid capacity shortfall complaints during initial deployments.
next_steps: Finalize request schema and publish Google Doc for Eng review by 2025-02-14
Design to iterate wireframes and hand off to Frontend by 2025-02-21
Schedule cross-functional review (PM/Eng/Design/Sales/SRE/Security) for approval matrix on 2025-02-25
Create ENG tickets for backend schema, approval engine, and workbench UI by 2025-03-05

source.title: Bucketed commit tier simulator with telemetry gating probe
source.creator: Priya Desai
source.description: Run a simulation-backed probe of a bucketed commit tier model that (1) groups reserved credit commitments into discrete buckets, (2) credits short-term streaming bursts via an on-ramp grace mechanism, and (3) applies telemetry gating to reduce gaming/abuse. This ticket scopes the simulation, acceptance criteria, required artifacts for sales/legal preflight, and pilot plan for 6 pilot customers.
source.objective: Produce a data-driven recommendation for a new commit-tier product that improves revenue predictability for mid-market and enterprise customers while protecting hosted API unit economics and preserving flexibility for streaming-heavy workloads.
source.hypothesis: Introducing bucketed commit tiers plus a telemetry-gated grace credit mechanism will increase committed ARR from mid-market customers by >=3% while limiting churn increase to <1 percentage point. Telemetry gating will reduce burst credit abuse and keep gross margins within 2 percentage points of baseline.
source.experiment_design: 1) Build a customer usage simulator seeded from anonymized production telemetry (3 months). 2) Create three pricing arms: A) baseline symmetric per-token rates, B) asymmetric IO split with streaming floor, C) bucketed commit tiers (small/medium/large) + burst grace credits gated by telemetry. 3) Monte Carlo 10k runs across customer archetypes (chat-first, embedding-heavy, hybrid). 4) Track revenue, churn proxy, margin, and abuse flags. 5) Run a 6-week pilot with 4 opt-in customers under arm C if simulation metrics meet thresholds.
source.analysis_summary: Initial simulation prototypes (synthetic runs) indicate bucketed tiers reduce month-to-month revenue variance by ~12% for mid-market archetypes and increase committed ARR by ~2.8% on median. Telemetry gating reduces flagged abuse by ~65% vs naive grace credits but shifts some ingest-heavy customers into larger buckets (trade-off: potential sticker shock). Embedding-heavy customers are less likely to benefit from bucketing unless we offer embedding-specific buckets or add per-embedding discounts.
next_steps: Complete data science run with real anonymized telemetry by 2025-03-20
Draft PRD with recommended bucket sizes and grace credit rules (link below)
Legal & Sales: run enterprise SQ preflight to surface contract language and audit logging requirements
Engineering: scope billing ingestion changes to support bucket attribution and auto-reprofiling
Identify 6 pilot customers and draft pilot SOWs and opt-in incentives

2025-02-18 - Mateo Alvarez: Engineering review - billing schema needs support for 'bucket_id' and 'grace_credit_used' flags. Flagged migration risk for legacy invoices.
2025-02-25 - Ruth Kim (Legal): Requested enterprise SQ checklist for pilot customers; highlighted encryption-at-rest and audit log retention as negotiation points.
2025-03-01 - DS team (Nathan Li): First-pass Monte Carlo completed; preliminary seeds attached to drive folder. Observed tail sensitivity for 99th percentile streaming bursts.
2025-03-04 - Sales (Liam O'Connor): Identified three mid-market customers likely to opt-in for pilot if we provide 6-month price protection and clear reporting UI.
2025-03-05 - Priya Desai: Updated acceptance criteria and set go/no-go check on 2025-03-20 after full sim run.

source.title: Console policy composer — region priorities, tier routing, and staged model fallbacks
source.creator: Priya Nair
source.description: Build a customer-facing Policy Composer in the Redwood Console that lets customers express routing preferences across regions, capacity tiers (hot/warm/cold), and ordered fallback model variants. The composer should be approachable for PM/Dev users (visual rule blocks + guided defaults) while remaining expressive for advanced users (JSON/DLS export/import). The feature's goals are: 1) enable customers to implement region-first rules with soft or hard preferences, 2) let customers specify tier routing policies with autoscaling/burst hints, and 3) author staged fallback chains of model variants (eg. prefer quantized cheaper model, fallback to full-precision model on quality or capacity failure). Include real-time validation, conflict warnings, and a preview simulator that runs a dry-run against synthetic traces to estimate latency/cost hit and failure modes.
source.context: This ticket extends earlier routing tooling (internal work on route-authoring) to a polished console experience for customers. Many enterprise customers have asked for explicit region prioritization and deterministic fallbacks for regulatory requirements and predictable latency. Engineering has implemented low-level APIs for policy enforcement (ENG-9310, ENG-10122); this ticket covers UX, validation, preview, and product acceptance criteria to ship to hosted customers.
acceptance_criteria: New 'Policy Composer' UI accessible from Routes > Policies for accounts with routing permissions
Users can create rules that list regions in order of preference with 'soft' vs 'hard' enforcement toggles
Users can attach tier routing rules (hot/warm/cold) and specify autoscale hints (min/max concurrency) per tier
Users can add an ordered fallback chain of model variants with optional predicates (capacity, latency SLO miss, quality regression triggers)
UI performs static validation: unreachable regions, incompatible model variants, circular fallbacks, and priority conflicts; errors surfaced inline
Preview simulator runs a dry-run with configurable synthetic traffic profile and returns estimated token cost, 95th/99th latency, and probability of fallback activation
Policy can be exported/imported as JSON DSL; JSON schema validated on import
E2E tests cover UI flow, validation rules, export/import roundtrip, and preview metrics accuracy within +/-10% of server simulation
Feature rollout gated behind account flag and behind telemetry events for adoption metrics
design_notes: Primary composer view will use a left pane for rule steps and a right pane for configuration and validation panel
Provide two authoring modes: Guided (form-driven, presets) and Advanced (JSON editor with live linting)
Validation panel shows severity (error/warning/info), location, and one-click quick fixes for common issues
Preview output includes a waterfall visualization of region routing, tier selection, and which fallback triggered for sampled requests
Accessibility: keyboard-first rule reordering, screen-reader labels for priority toggles
engineering_requirements: Backend API endpoints: POST /policies/preview (dry run), GET/POST /policies (CRUD), POST /policies/validate (schema + semantics)
Server-side validation must reuse enforcement logic from runtime to avoid drift (callable library shared with serving runtime)
Preview simulator accepts synthetic traffic profile and returns token-cost estimate, latency distribution (p50/p95/p99), and fallback activation histogram
Policy storage should be versioned with immutable revisions and support tag-based rollouts (canary/percent)
Client must fetch model compatibility matrix from Model Catalog service to validate variant compatibility across regions/hardware
comments: 2025-02-18 Priya Nair: Created ticket — initial scope and acceptance criteria. POC backend endpoints exist from ENG-9310; need Console UX and preview work.
2025-02-21 Ethan Park: Spiked Guided vs Advanced modes with design. Shared wireframes and asked Design for quick usability round on 2025-02-25.

2025-02-26 Maya Chen (Design): Feedback from usability test (n=6): users liked ordered-region drag/drop but confused by 'soft' terminology. Updated copy to 'preferred vs required'. Added quick-fix suggestions in validation panel.
2025-02-28 Rahul Gupta (Eng): Backend preview endpoint implemented behind feature flag; returns coarse-site simulation. Need contract for model compatibility lookup; pinged Model Catalog owners.
2025-03-02 Priya Nair: PM review — trimmed advanced JSON features for initial launch to reduce risk; keep import/export but delay rule templating until next cycle.

2025-03-04 Ethan Park: Engineering plan ready. Split into two sub-issues for workload: PM-14232A (UI composer + validation) and PM-14232B (Preview simulator enhancements). Created frontend branch and opened PR #512 with initial components.

source.title: Granular lane cost & SLA cutover PRD + deployment checklist for hosted inference endpoints
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Summary: This PRD defines the product requirements, acceptance criteria, and launch checklist for introducing granular lane pricing and SLA profiles across the hosted inference endpoints (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank). The goal is to expose cost-tiered lanes that let customers trade off latency and price while allowing Redwood to perform zero-downtime cutovers and graded degradations between lanes when capacity or incidents occur. The feature surface includes: lane definition and metadata, per-lane SLA and cost band configuration, API surface to request or opt into lanes, backend routing rules and fallback policy, telemetry and billing hooks, canary and cutover playbook, and migration guidance for existing customers.
source.background_and_goals: Background: Customers ask for more predictable unit economics and the ability to choose latencies vs. price when calling hosted endpoints. Internally we need a mechanism to group capacity into lanes with explicit cost profiles (e.g., premium-low-latency, standard-balanced, economy-high-throughput) and a safe way to perform capacity cutovers without dropping in-flight requests. Goals: 1) Provide explicit lane options surfaced via API/Console and SDKs. 2) Map each lane to measurable SLAs (p50/p95 latency, error rate, availability) and cost multipliers. 3) Guarantee zero-downtime cutovers between lane mappings for a tenant (transparent fallback). 4) Make rollouts auditable and reversible with telemetry gates. 5) Minimize changes required by customers — default behavior remains backward compatible.
source.scope_inclusions: - API: request_lanes (optional) query parameter and lane preference header; console UI to view lane costs and expected SLOs; SDK flag. - Server-side: lane metadata registry, routing policy engine, versioned mapping store per-tenant, preflight health checks, graceful KV/prefix cache preservation across cutovers. - Observability: lane-level telemetry, cost attribution tagging, rollout traces, SLA breach alerts. - Launch artifacts: canary and cutover runbook, customer migration doc, billing mapping update, e2e test suite.
source.scope_exclusions: - Billing pricebook UI changes (handled by billing team separately) - Dedicated/Private deployment automation (out of scope for initial launch) - New model flavors or quantization suggestions (handled by Optimize later)
source.requirements: 1) API/SDK: optional lane selector (string) and lane-fallback-policy (enum: prefer, strict, degrade). 2) Console: lane catalog page showing p50/p95 historical and projected cost-per-1k-tokens. 3) Routing: deterministic per-tenant lane mapping with atomic swap support and automatic fallback to compatible lane if capacity insufficient. 4) Safety: preflight canary with synthetic traffic validation and automatic rollback if telemetry gates fail. 5) Telemetry: tags on every request for lane_id, routing_decision, fallback_reason, and billing_tag. 6) Acceptance: performance targets for each lane are validated against traffic mix and load tests. 7) Migration helpers: bulk-assignment tool for enterprise tenants and opt-in CLI for self-serve customers.
source.tradeoffs_and_anti_requirements: Tradeoffs: Introducing lanes increases control plane complexity and risk of misrouting; we prioritize readability of lane semantics over maximal flexibility. We will avoid exposing low-level placement controls to customers in v1 to reduce complexity. Anti-requirements: Do not require customers to re-author prompts or rework prompt engineering for lane adoption. No changes to response semantics or random seed/determinism guarantees except where explicit lane-based quality regressions are documented.
acceptance_criteria: API: lane selector accepted by chat/completions/embeddings/rerank endpoints with valid enums documented.
Console: lane catalog page available with historical p50/p95 and sample cost projection for top 5 models.
Routing: atomic per-tenant lane mapping swap completes with zero dropped requests in synthetic cutover tests under normal load.
Perf: premium lane p95 latency within 10% of baseline SLA target for the same model; economy lane achieves >=25% lower cost per token on average in load tests.
Reliability: no more than 0.1% increased error rate during cutover window in staged canary runs.
Rollback: automated rollback triggers on two telemetry gates (latency spike >30% p95 or error rate >0.5%) during canary and cutover runs.
Billing: every request contains billing_tag and maps to cost band in logs; sample billing export shows correct lane attribution for 7-day window.
Docs: migration guide, API docs, and console help articles published and linked from release notes.
Sign-offs: PM, ENG (runtime + routing), SRE, Billing, and Legal have approved launch checklist items.
Security: security_review_required false but standard telemetry PII review completed; no customer payloads persisted beyond existing retention policies.
launch_checklist: Code merge: routing engine changes merged and green (ENG-84221).
API docs: lane selector fields documented and published in SDK changelog.
Console: lane catalog U/X built and smoke-tested in staging; pricing UI placeholders linked to billing team.
Telemetry: lane tags emitted; dashboards created for lane p50/p95, error rate, and fallback counts.
Canary plan: reserve 5% traffic slice for internal Canary tenants and synthetic traffic during initial rollouts.
Load test: synthetic spike test at 150% normal load with cutover validated and no dropped requests.
Billing mapping: billing export pipeline validated for lane attribution for 7 days in staging.
Runbooks: cutover and rollback runbooks reviewed and dry-run executed by SRE on staging cluster.
Customer comms: email template and in-console banner drafted for enterprise customers outlining lane benefits and migration options.
Compliance: telemetry/data retention check completed; Legal acknowledgement added to release notes.
Launch window: set initial cutover maintenance window with on-call ENG + SRE overlap for 2 hours post-cutover.
Post-launch: 72-hour monitoring window with prioritized alerting and daily postmortem checklist ready.
source.rollout_plan: Phase 0 (internal): Feature flags enabled for internal and dev tenants. Phase 1 (canary): 5% internal traffic plus synthetic validation. Phase 2 (enterprise opt-in): invite enterprise customers to opt into lanes with support assistance and bulk-assignment tooling. Phase 3 (general availability): open lane selector to self-serve with default behavior preserved for existing callers. Cutover approach: per-tenant atomic mapping swap via config store; in-flight requests routed to old lane until completion; new requests use new mapping. Fallback behavior: automatic fallback to nearest-compatible lane if preferred lane is capacity constrained; fallback_reason annotated for telemetry and billing.
source.test_plan: Unit tests for lane selector parsing and header handling; integration tests for routing engine path selection; golden tests for billing tag mapping; e2e staging test: synthetic workload reproducing production percentile distributions across models for chat/completions/embeddings/rerank. Performance tests: p50/p95 measurement under 1x and 1.5x load for each lane. Cutover test: simultaneous atomic swap while generating 10k in-flight requests verifying none are dropped and latency curves remain within acceptance thresholds.
telemetry_metrics: cutover_duration_seconds - time from start of mapping swap to last in-flight request completion
source.risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Misrouted requests during atomic swaps. Mitigation: perform atomic swap via transactional config store and validate with synthetic checks before unflagging. Risk: Unexpected SLA regressions for economy lane on certain models. Mitigation: conservative cost targets during initial launch and model-level exclusions for lanes until validated. Risk: Billing discrepancies. Mitigation: parallel billing export validation in staging for 7 days and reconciliation scripts.
decisions: Expose lane selector as optional parameter rather than required field to preserve backward compatibility.
Do not support custom per-tenant lane creation in v1; lanes are centrally defined and versioned by product.
Fallback policy semantics: default to 'prefer' which attempts preferred lane then falls back when capacity constrained.
Cutover style: atomic config swap per-tenant with in-flight graceful draining rather than live-migration of in-flight requests.
open_questions: Should we surface historical lane cost trends in Console for more than 30 days? (ops/storage tradeoff).
Is there appetite from billing to accept lane-level discounts in month-1 or should we postpone discounts to month-2?
Do we default new customers to economy lane or balanced lane?
Should SDKs expose a prioritized list of acceptable lanes instead of a single selector?

title: Granular lane cost & SLA cutover PRD + deployment checklist for hosted inference endpoints
preview: Summary: This PRD defines the product requirements, acceptance criteria, and launch checklist for introducing granular lane pricing and SLA profiles across the hosted inference endpoints (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank). source.title: Enterprise data controls intake workflow and priority decomposition
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a clear, implementable product-side intake and prioritization workflow for enterprise data-control requests (logging redaction, retention policy alterations, PII handling, and audit export formats). This ticket captures the decision criteria, priority decomposition, routing rules to GTM/SE/Eng, and stubbed responses for the Console and Support intake UI. Aim: reduce time-to-response for security-sensitive requests from days to <48 hours for initial triage and map each request to an owner and SLA band.
acceptance_criteria: A documented intake flow diagram (Confluence) that maps request types to triage owners and SLAs
Priority decomposition table that converts business asks into product/engineering work types and urgency bands
Stubbed Console intake form fields and UI copy for sensitive data controls
Routing rules defined (automatic vs manual) with GH issue templates for ENG and Support
Stakeholder sign-off from Security, Legal, and SRE on redaction/retention guardrails
decisions: Use three-tier SLA bands for enterprise requests: Triage (48h), Scoped Design (10 business days), Implementation (depends on size)
Categorize requests by control type: Logging Redaction / Retention / PII Handling / Audit Exports; each category has a default priority mapping
Default response: for redaction/PII requests we will offer immediate advisor-run guidance and a short-term configurable retention cap until engineering changes are scheduled
Sensitive configuration changes require Security review and sign-off before production rollout; this is non-negotiable for PII/PII-adjacent exports
comments: 2025-02-15 Ava Chen: Created ticket after customer success weekly flag — multiple large customers asked for unified retention override policy and clearer audit export CSV metadata. Goal is to centralize decisioning and reduce back-and-forth.
2025-02-18 Diego Ramirez: Drafted priority decomposition. Proposing mapping: Operational (low) | Config Change (medium) | Runtime/Service (high). Suggest SRE sign-off for any runtime change.
2025-02-22 Priya Nair (Security): Reviewed mapping; requested explicit list of PII fields and example redaction rules. Also requested KMS/HSM gating for export endpoints.
2025-02-25 Marco Li (Support): Suggested intake form must collect 'business justification' and 'data owner contact' to speed approvals. Will add templated questions.
2025-02-27 Diego Ramirez: Updated Confluence diagram (artifact link) with routing rules. Marked ENG-7231 as dependency for intake API and webhook notifications.
2025-03-01 Sophie Martinez (Legal): Approved approach for retention policy exceptions provided there is a documented legal justification and retention expiration baked into policy objects.

2025-03-03 Diego Ramirez: Breaking this ticket into implementation sub-issues in next sprint; primary remaining items: Console form validation, auto-routing engine, and SOC/Security playbook updates. Assigned ENG-7399 for API validation work.
2025-03-03 Ava Chen: Requested PM review meeting with Customer Success for next Tuesday to validate canned responses and SLAs before sign-off.
next_steps: Finalize intake form field list and add validation rules (due 2025-03-07)
Deliver routing rules config to Routing Service (ENG-7231) so automated labels can be attached to requests
Work with Security to define exact PII taxonomy and redaction heuristics to include in the triage guide
Produce Support runbook with canned replies and escalation templates
Create GitHub templates for ENG and SRE that pre-populate risk, scope, and data sensitivity sections
source.timeline: Triage design complete by 2025-03-07; sign-offs by 2025-03-14; handoff to implementation during Cycle 2025-W12
source.risk_assessment: Medium-high: incorrect triage decisions could expose PII or delay required security reviews. Mitigations: conservative default (require Security sign-off for PII), logging of decisions for audit, and automatic escalation to a live SE for high-impact customers.
notes: Trade-offs discussed: heavier intake forms reduce rework but increase initial friction for customers. We will A/B test a short vs extended form for 4 pilot customers.
Consider adding retention profiles (30, 90, 365 days, custom) with engineering guardrails to prevent sub-24h retention which breaks analytics pipelines.
For audit exports, prefer structured JSON schema v1 for machine readability; CSV exports remain for legacy customers but require field-level redaction markers.

source.title: Routing Governor: interactive Console sandbox for region/tier selection and fallback variants
source.creator: Aisha Nair
source.description: Build an interactive 'Routing Governor' sandbox in the Redwood Console that lets customers author, validate, and preview routing policies that express region selection, tier routing, and tiered fallback model variants. This feature surfaces validation hints, live cost/latency estimates, and a simulated traffic preview so product/platform teams can safely iterate on policies before applying them.
acceptance_criteria: Console shows a policy editor canvas that supports region rules, tier priorities, and fallback model variant blocks
Validation engine highlights policy conflicts and suggests fixes inline with links to help docs
Preview mode simulates 1,000 synthetic requests with configurable distribution and returns a per-region/per-tier coverage report
Preview integrates estimated token-cost and 95th-percentile latency numbers using existing cost/benchmarks service
Changes can be saved as drafts and promoted to canaries with a one-click rollout plan (creates related rollout config)

Design API contract for console <-> routing service: validatePolicy, simulatePolicyPreview
Implement frontend editor components and rule validation UI
Hook up validation engine and surface rule-level diagnostic messages
Wire preview simulator to back-end job that returns region/tier breakdown and cost/latency estimates
Add telemetry events for preview runs and policy saves
E2E tests for author/save/preview/publish flows
Docs: add Console walkthrough and sample policy library

source.title: Dedicated: buffer telemetry, grace periods, and preemption visibility PRD
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: This PRD defines a set of telemetry, console UX, and API changes to make tenant "buffer" consumption and grace-period behavior observable and controllable for Dedicated customers. Teams need clear signals when a tenant is borrowing burst credits, when grace periods are active, and why preemptions/evictions occurred. Current gaps cause customer confusion and increase SRE toil during capacity incidents.
goals: Expose per-tenant "headroom" and "buffer credit" metrics with <60s export latency
Surface grace-period state and remaining grace seconds in Console and APIs
Provide deterministic, testable acceptance criteria for preemption decisions and alerts
Enable tenants and operators to correlate client-level latency/cost regressions to buffer consumption events
non_goals: Implement new billing changes tied to buffer usage (out of scope for this cycle)
Make policy changes to eviction algorithms (only observability and controls covered)
Full rewrite of autoscaler — only telemetry hooks and feature flags are required
success_metrics: 95% of buffer-related alerts include tenant_id and grace_state tags
Mean time to diagnose buffer-related customer incidents reduced from 3.5h to <1h
Console adoption: 40% of Dedicated customers view buffer telemetry within 30 days of release
SLO: buffer metrics emitted within 60s for 99% of events under normal load
technical_requirements: New metrics emitted by serving runtime: tenant.buffer_total, tenant.buffer_used, tenant.grace_seconds_remaining, tenant.preempt_count (all with tenant_id tag)
Metrics export via Prometheus/OpenTelemetry with clear cardinality limits and summarization labels
Backend APIs: GET /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/capacity-status returning headroom, buffer_used, grace_state as JSON fields
Event stream: preemption events written to event-bus with reason, timestamp, prior_buffer_used, and request_id
Integration tests simulating borrow-then-preempt sequences with deterministic timing to validate behavior
ui_requirements: Console: Tenant Capacity panel shows current headroom, buffer_used (absolute + %), grace_state (active/expired), and a 1h sparkline
Console: Timeline view of recent preemptions with links to request traces and PRs/Incidents
Alerting: Built-in alert template 'tenant.buffer_high' with recommended thresholds and runbook link
Export: CSV/JSON export of buffer usage for selected time range
acceptance_criteria: Metrics: tenant.buffer_total and tenant.buffer_used appear in Prometheus on staging and prod with tenant_id label and 60s scrape latency
API: /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant}/capacity-status returns correct numeric headroom and grace_seconds_remaining for synthetic tenants in integration environment
Console: Capacity panel matches API values within 5% for a 15-minute rolling window
Eventing: Preemption events emitted contain request_id and prior_buffer_used and are consumable by downstream analysis pipeline
Tests: Automated integration test that simulates borrow->grace->preempt passes in CI with assertion on emitted events
Docs: Runbook and Console help content published and linked from alert template
source.rollout_plan: Phase 1 (staging): Instrument serving runtime and export initial metrics; expose API endpoints behind feature flag. Phase 2 (limited prod): Enable metric scraping for 10 internal tenants and selected beta customers; ship Console widgets behind entitlement flag. Phase 3 (general prod): Flip feature flag for all Dedicated customers, enable default alerting, and publish runbook. Each phase gated by the corresponding acceptance criteria and load test results.
risks_and_mitigation: High-cardinality telemetry: enforce tenant_id as low-cardinality label and add summarized rollups (per-availability-zone) to avoid scrape explosions
Metric lag during scale spikes: batch metric writes in runtime with a bounded buffer and fallback sampling to preserve signal
UI confusion if values differ across systems: implement a reconciliation job and surface last-refresh timestamps and data source links
Event loss in the bus: persist preemption events to durable store before acking; record a fallback audit log
open_questions: Do we need to expose historical per-request token counts with buffer consumption, or are per-minute aggregates sufficient?
Should grace_seconds_remaining be configurable per-tenant or controlled by global policy in this release?
What retention window do we commit to for buffer usage on the Console (30d vs 90d)?
Is billing change required if a tenant consistently exhausts buffer credits? (out of scope but raises product question)
decisions: Expose per-tenant buffer metrics at 1m granularity with 30d retention by default
Grace period will be read-only in this release (visibility only); configurability deferred to next cycle
Emit preemption events to Kafka topic 'dedicated.preemptions.v1' and write an audit record to the internal audit store
updates: 2025-02-14: Kickoff sync with ENG and DES. Agreed to target 1m metric granularity and 30d retention. Action: ENG to prototype metrics emitter.
2025-02-20: ENG PR opened implementing tenant.buffer_* metrics (ENG-19821). Initial unit tests green; integration test scaffolded.
2025-02-26: Design submitted Console mockups (DES-774). Feedback: add last-refresh timestamp and link to raw event stream. DES to iterate.
2025-02-28: Blocker identified: current metrics pipeline cannot attach both tenant_id and zone labels without increased cardinality. Mitigation: add summarized rollups; ENG to add sampling toggle.
2025-03-03: Feature flag wired to staging; API endpoint stubbed. Next: validate metric latency under load and finalize acceptance test harness.

source.title: On-demand Attestation Marketplace + Usage-triggered Crediting Pilot
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: Enterprise deals frequently stall on customized security questionnaires (SIG/CAIQ). Current attestation support is manual and creates uneven sales lift and high engineering/solutions effort. Goal: pilot an on-demand attestation marketplace plus a usage-triggered crediting model so customers can self-request curated, signed attestation bundles while sales gets credits to accelerate legal/security review. The pilot should reduce days-to-contract, lower SE hours per deal, and surface repeatable answer packs for automation.
acceptance_criteria: Pilot MVP: productized marketplace UI in Console where customers request a "feature-level" attestation pack (SIG/CAIQ subset) and select delivery type (self-serve bundle, signed attestation, or SE-assisted).

Credit model: configured pricing triggers that issue credits to customer accounts when requests are above thresholds (e.g., >50k ARR or enterprise tier), and a matched 'sales credit' pool that offsets SE time.
Auditability: all issued attestations are cryptographically signed (customer-friendly PDF + machine-readable metadata) and appear in customer audit export.
Telemetry: track time-to-attestation, avg SE hours saved per request, NPS delta for contract experience, and incremental cost per attestation.
Integration: backend APIs for ENG to wire answer-pack generator and KMS signing endpoint; console routes to display pack status and delivery artifacts.
deliverables: Console mockups: Drive - Attestation Marketplace flows (DES-attachments)
Backend API spec: GitHub gist - /redwood/attestation-service/openapi.yaml (link TBD)
KMS-signed attestation pipeline: ENG PR (WIP) and README
tasks: Design: finalize marketplace entry points and micro-pricing UI (DES-4421)
Eng: implement answer-pack composer and CAIQ subset mapping (ENG-19833)
Eng: KMS signing integration and PDF + JSON attestation output
PM: define credit thresholds, experiment cohorts (self-serve, sales-assisted, control)
Sales Ops: train pilot AE/SEs and add playbook snippets for using attestation credits
Legal: review signed attestation language and liabilities
risks_mitigation: Risk: High operational cost if requests scale uncontrolled. Mitigation: soft cap on free signed attestations per account + tiered charging beyond threshold.
Risk: Incorrect/misleading answers. Mitigation: require owner attestations and include audit trail linking to source evidence; human review queue for high-risk items.
Risk: Security/compliance concerns with signing. Mitigation: use customer-managed KMS or limited-scope signing key per region; involve Security early.
metrics_to_track: Pct deals using marketplace vs baseline
Days reduction in contract close time (median)
comments: 2025-02-18 - Ava Chen: Initial writeup and hypothesis. Proposed cohorts: startup (self-serve), midmarket (credits), enterprise (sales-assisted).
2025-02-24 - Jordan Patel: Synced with Sales Ops. They want an onramp playbook and example credit sizes for 100k/500k ARR tiers. Added to dependencies ENG-19833 for composer work.

2025-02-27 - Priya Rao (Legal): Flagged language for signed attestations; legal prefers a lightweight 'attestation statement' template that avoids warranties. Legal review required before pilot launch.
2025-02-28 - Samir Gupta (Security): Security to consult on KMS approach and signing key rotation. Recommended HSM-backed signing for enterprise tier.
2025-03-01 - Jordan Patel: Updated acceptance criteria to include machine-readable metadata and audit export. Planning kickoff with Design 2025-W10.
next_steps: Schedule cross-functional kickoff (PM/ENG/Design/Sales/Legal/Security) - target 2025-03-05
Lock experiment cohorts and credit thresholds; publish experiment plan to Sales Ops
Design to produce final mockups for console flow and email templates for delivery
ENG to scope API contract and estimate infra cost for pilot
Legal & Security to sign off on attestation template and KMS approach

source.title: Pilot: Enterprise Contract Tiering for SSO/SAML & RBAC Requirements
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background: Large enterprise customers repeatedly list SSO/SAML and fine-grained RBAC in security questionnaires as gating items for contract signature. Sales teams are pushing for a clear product+contract offering that defines minimum commitments (SLA, support, on-boarding) tied to accessible packaging and predictable pricing. This ticket scopes a small pricing/packaging pilot that ties explicit SSO/SAML and RBAC deliverables to contract tiers, and verifies downstream impacts (legal reviews, runtime access patterns, engineering delivery).

Goal: Define a minimal, testable contract tier matrix (three tiers) that includes SSO/SAML configuration commitments, RBAC feature coverage, and corresponding commercial terms (price uplift, committed usage, delivery windows). Run a pilot with 2–3 inbound enterprise prospects to validate willingness-to-pay and operational feasibility. Produce a single-package bundle (feature + contract playbook + questionnaire template) that Sales and Legal can use during negotiation.
acceptance_criteria: A documented tier matrix (Bronze/Silver/Gold) mapping SSO/SAML and RBAC commitments to pricing uplift and onboarding timelines
Playbook PDF for Sales/Legal with pre-filled security questionnaire responses for common enterprise controls
A validated pilot with at least two prospects that sign an MOU or indicate formal intent tied to a tier
Engineering backlog items scoped and estimated for the chosen tier commitments (ENG tickets created)
A small set of console templates and provisioning checklists to enforce RBAC roles and SSO setup during onboarding
success_metrics: Pilot conversion intent >= 40% (2 of 5 engaged enterprise prospects indicate intent)
Average price uplift for signed commitments >= 15% above baseline hosted pricing
Onboarding time for SSO/SAML + RBAC reduced to <= 10 business days for pilot customers
Reduction in security questionnaire turnaround time by >= 50% using pre-filled answers
experiment_plan: Tier definitions: Bronze = baseline with self-serve SSO docs + basic RBAC roles; Silver = assisted SSO setup (1-week SLA) + expanded RBAC (team, project, token scoping) + 6-month support commitment; Gold = dedicated onboarding engineer, guaranteed SSO configuration SLA (3 business days), audited RBAC role mapping, and priority support + 12-month committed spend
Pricing: model uplift as % of baseline usage for Silver (15%) and Gold (30%); include optional Dedicated seat pricing for Gold customers requiring higher isolation

Questionnaire mapping: create canonical answers for 20 common SSO/SAML + RBAC Qs (encryption, session TTL, key management, audit logs, admin separation). Map each answer to the tier where the control is contractually guaranteed
Legal/compliance: coordinate with Legal to determine acceptable language for SLA, support, and liability caps specific to identity integrations
Measurement: instrument sales deals with a tracking tag and require a one-question feedback form post-onboarding to record friction points
rollout_steps: Week 0–1: finalize tier matrix draft and pricing proposal; review with Legal and Sales leadership
Week 2: produce Sales playbook, pre-filled questionnaire templates, and onboarding checklist; create Confluence and Drive artifacts
Week 3: select 2–3 pilot prospects (Sales to nominate); run discovery sessions to capture risk and timeline
Week 4–6: perform assisted onboarding for pilot customers; track time-to-setup and engineering effort
Week 7: retro + decision: iterate on tier definitions or widen pilot; prepare formalized contract language for release
notes_comments: 2025-02-11 — Ava Chen: Initial proposal shared with Legal (link: https://confluence.redwood.ai/enterprise/ssorbac-pilot) — requested firm SLA language for SSO setup windows.
2025-02-12 — Miguel Alvarez: Met with Sales (J. Patel, L. Nguyen) to prioritize inbound prospects; Sales suggested prioritizing regulation-heavy accounts (finance, healthtech).
2025-02-14 — Legal (S. Rao): flagged liability and data processing wording; need to confirm if KMS language can be in standard addendum or needs separate contract.
2025-02-17 — Eng lead (K. Osei): created high-level estimates; stated RBAC role scoping work depends on console RBAC v2 epic (ENG-2711) and audit-logging refinement (ENG-2845).
2025-02-19 — Design (M. Park): sketched onboarding flow and SSO wizard; recommended a one-click SAML test assertion feature to reduce cycle time.

2025-02-20 — Ava Chen: Updated acceptance criteria and scheduled kickoff with pilot prospects for 2025-02-24. GitHub template for contract language: https://github.com/redwood-inference/contracts/pull/342

Run a pilot with 2–3 inbound enterprise prospects to validate willingness-to-pay and operational feasibility.
