source.title: Pricing commitment matrix & security questionnaire alignment for SSO/RBAC contract variability
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Run a pricing/packaging experiment and create canonical security-questionnaire answers tying SSO (SAML/SCIM) and RBAC commitments to contractual packaging. Goal: reduce negotiation friction, standardize commitments across tiers, and measure lift in close rate and deal size when we offer clearly-scoped SSO/RBAC commitments vs bespoke offers.
acceptance_criteria: A completed pricing commitment matrix that maps 4 packaging tiers to SSO/SAML, SCIM provisioning, RBAC granularity, audit-log retention, and associated price/commitment bands
Canonical security-questionnaire answers for top 25 Qs that legal/security teams see in RFPs, stored in Confluence and reusable for sales ops
Draft contract language for SSO & RBAC commitments (lead time, support SLA, rollback/fallback clauses) reviewed by Legal
A 6-week experiment plan with tracking dashboard: conversion, time-to-sign, average ARR, and integration support hours
Executive summary and recommendation with estimated P&L impact for including SSO/RBAC in tier vs selling as add-on professional service
decisions: Adopt 4-tier packaging: Free/Developer, Standard, Enterprise, Dedicated-Private — each with explicit SSO/RBAC deliverables

Standard tier includes SAML SSO (IdP-initiated & SP-initiated) + basic SCIM; Enterprise includes guaranteed SAML timelines, SCIM provisioning, and role-based ACLs; Dedicated-Private includes customizable RBAC and audit retention options
Offer two purchasing paths: self-serve (Standard, limited SSO: docs + community support) and sales-assisted (Enterprise/Dedicated with SLA-backed SSO & RBAC commitments)
Discounting tied to minimum term: 12-month commitments unlock inclusion of RBAC and reduced professional services hours; shorter commitments treated as add-on billable work
risks: Underestimating engineering & support effort for rapid SAML/SCIM onboarding across heterogeneous customer IdPs
Ambiguous RBAC scope leading to scope-creep and unplanned professional services; customers expect fine-grained attribute-based access without paying for integration
Legal exposure from over-promising availability of SSO connectors or timeline guarantees
Complexity in pricing model could slow sales conversation if not surfaced simply in simplified collateral
notes: Observed from prior deals: customers who got SSO + SCIM included closed 18% faster and had 22% higher ARR on average (internal CRM sample, n=42) — sample biased to mid-market
Engineering backlog: ENG-3124 and ENG-3189 cover SAML connector hardening and SCIM provisioning endpoints respectively — need to align dependencies
Legal flagged two contract clauses: (1) change management for connector updates, (2) liability cap specifically tied to authentication failures
Surveyed sales: top 3 enterprise asks are IdP compatibility matrix, SCIM automations, and admin console RBAC controls
next_steps: 1) Finalize pricing commitment matrix draft and share with Legal & Engineering by 2025-02-21
2) Create canonical security-questionnaire document and push to Confluence space (security/enterprise-answers) by 2025-02-24
3) Build experiment cohort (20 inbound enterprise leads, randomized) and prepare tracking dashboard in Metabase; dashboard to include conversion, avg ARR, time-to-sign, and engineering hours logged
4) Scope a 12-hour pilot professional services bundle for customers who need accelerated SSO + RBAC onboarding; price point to be set after consultation with Solutions Eng
5) Book legal review session for drafted contract language on 2025-02-26
comments: 2025-02-10 - Ava Chen: Created ticket. Initial hypothesis: packaging clarity on SSO/RBAC reduces negotiation time and increases ARR. Assigned to Martin.

2025-02-12 - Martin Alvarez: Met with Solutions Eng and Legal. Solutions Eng estimates 10-15 engineering hours per enterprise SAML integration if we standardize connector set; Legal requested explicit SLA wording around 'connector availability' and 'customer misconfiguration' clauses.
2025-02-15 - Priya Nair (Legal): Draft contract language posted to Drive; flagged the need for rollback/fallback clause when we push connector patches that break compatibility.
2025-02-17 - Sophia Kim (Sales): Volunteer list of 22 inbound leads willing to participate in experiment. Suggest segmenting by industry (SaaS, FinServ, Health) to control for procurement variance.

source.title: Tranche Playbook for Enterprise-Isolated Deploys: Quarterly Cadence, Risk Budget, and Acceptance Gates
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary: Create a repeatable, tranche-based quarterly playbook for delivering Private / VPC / on-prem features to enterprise customers. The playbook will define tranche boundaries (canary -> wave -> general availability), a risk budget model (time, rollback windows, compatibility debt), acceptance gates, cross-functional roles/responsibilities, and observability/signals tied to rollout decisions.

Scope:
- Operational playbook for sequencing quarterly deployments across gated tranches (Tier 1 canaries, Tier 2 waves, Tier 3 GA).
- Standardized acceptance criteria and telemetry thresholds for promoting between tranches.
- Rollback and fallback policies mapped to model/runtime versions, upgrade windows, and customer cohorts.
- Communication templates, runbook checklist, and stakeholder cadence (PM/Eng/Support/Customer Success).
- Risk budget spreadsheet and escalation paths for SLA degradations, security findings, and compatibility regressions.

Out of scope: day-to-day incident response procedures (will link to infra runbooks), individual customer onboarding steps (handled by CS playbooks).
acceptance_criteria: Playbook doc published in Confluence with versioning and owner metadata
Tranche definitions (criteria and duration) documented and approved by Eng and Security
Automated metrics dashboard (latency, error rate, model-regression score) wired to tranche gates
Rollback and canary rollback steps documented and validated in a dry-run with one staging customer
Sign-off from Legal/Compliance for GA communication and SLA language
source.notes:
Design decisions and trade-offs: we are prioritizing operational simplicity over maximizing parallelism for Q1 to reduce coordination risk. Trade-offs include longer tranche windows for Tier 1 customers to validate KV cache/quantization profiles in realistic workloads. We will pilot the model-variant fallback mechanism on two internal Dedicated clusters before enabling for customers.

Open questions: optimal tranche size (percent of customer fleet), whether to use static percent or demand-based signals; final owner for tranche gating (SRE vs. Runtime PM).
updates: 2025-01-10: Ticket created by Ava Chen. Initial scope and goals captured; requested high-level review from Eng leadership.
2025-01-18: Kickoff with Eng, SRE, Security, CS. Agreed on three tranche tiers: Canary (5%), Wave (25%), GA (100%). Action: ENG to validate canary rollback steps (ENG-32111).
2025-01-27: Engineering estimate completed (8 story points). Decision: extend Tier1 window to 72 hours for KV-cache dominated workloads. Added risk budget spreadsheet stub in docs.
2025-02-03: Security review requested; initial checklist shared. Security flagged encryption-in-transit verification for private control plane (security_review_required).
2025-02-09: Design/Docs review with Solutions Engineering. Feedback: add customer communication templates and a pre-rollout CS playbook. Assigned PM-156790 for CS template work.
2025-02-15: Implementability review with Runtime team. Agreed signals for tranche gates: 95th latency, CPU/GPU utilization, model quality regression delta. Next: instrument dashboards (ENG-33245).

source.title: Delegated sign-off matrix and cryptographic audit proofs for staged model rollouts
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Define and deliver a flexible approval and change-management system for canary/A-B/shadow rollouts that combines RBAC-driven delegation, time-bound sign-offs, and tamper-evident approval records. The system should support: (1) configurable approver matrices per environment (dev/stage/prod), (2) delegated approvers with expiry windows and secondary approver requirements, (3) cryptographic signing of approvals (short-lived JWTs signed by KMS) to attach to rollout decisions, (4) exportable, queryable audit logs for compliance and postmortems, and (5) integration points for auto-rollback triggers tied to SLOs and quality regressions.
acceptance_criteria: Product UI to define approver matrices (roles, minimum approvers, delegation TTL) saved as named policies
API endpoints to request and attach a signed approval token to a rollout job (token validated by runtime before progressing)
Audit log events for every approval, delegation change, and automatic rollback decision; logs are queryable and exportable as CSV/JSON
RBAC enforcement in Console and API: only members with the configured role/policy may approve or delegate; changes to policies require two approvers when marked 'high-risk'
Auto-rollback flow: canary health gates evaluated against metric thresholds; upon breach, system must (a) stop rollout, (b) trigger rollback, (c) append signed incident annotation to audit log
E2E tests covering: approver delegation, token expiry handling, signed token rejection on tamper, audit export pipeline
notes: 2025-01-23: Kickoff with Eng (Ravi, Priya) and Security (Marta). Agreed to use KMS-backed signing for approval tokens instead of HSM integration in MVP to speed delivery.

2025-01-28: Design pass — DES-5012 provided wireframes for policy builder. Feedback: surface delegation TTL and last-approved timestamp prominently. Action: Design to add 'delegated by' column and approval history modal.
2025-02-01: Engineering spike (ENG-20201) validated token size and verification latency: signed JWT verification adds ~2ms p50 on the runtime path when cached; cache TTL 5s recommended.
2025-02-03: Compliance asked for 7-year retention for enterprise customers and export on-demand. We will support configurable retention per org + immediate export endpoint for legal holds.
2025-02-04: Security review notes: require MFA for approvers in prod; enforce hardware-backed keys for delegated approvers if critical flag set. Tracking: security tickets to be created.

Open questions: admin UX for rotating approvers across tenants; do we allow cross-tenant approver delegation (likely no for MVP); how granular should policy conditions be (model tag, region, capacity tier)?
technical_notes: Signed approval token format: compact JWT including {approver_id, role, policy_id, ttl, issued_at, signature_kid}
Verification: runtime verifies token signature via KMS public key cache; tokens rejected if policy has been revoked since issuance (policy revocation id included)
Audit pipeline: approval events -> redact PII -> append to append-only log (BigQuery/ClickHouse) with immutable sequence id and cryptographic hash chain to detect tampering
Rollback hook: orchestration subscribes to real-time health stream; when gate breached, create rollback job with provenance = linked signed approval(s) + incident annotation
implementation_plan: Phase 1 (MVP, 3 sprints): policy model + console UI, KMS-backed token issuance, runtime token validation, basic audit events
Phase 2: delegation UI, delegation TTL/rotation, export/retention controls, MFA enforcement for prod approvers
Phase 3: enterprise features — HSM attestation, long-term retention SLAs, legal-hold export, cross-tenant approval gating
risks: Approval token leakage could allow unauthorized rollouts if tokens are reused — mitigated by short TTLs and one-time-use markers
Performance impact if runtime verifies tokens on cold path — mitigated with verification caching and batched checks
Complexity for customers when configuring policies — mitigate with sensible defaults and policy templates
next_steps: Ravi to create ENG-xxxxx epics for runtime and API work by 2025-02-07
Design to deliver final mocks for policy builder and approval history by 2025-02-10 (attach DES-5012 update)
Security to schedule formal review on 2025-02-12 and publish required controls checklist

source.title: Market Thread Mapping for Model-Substitute Cascades
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Objective: produce a granular "market-thread" map that predicts how a change in upstream model availability or pricing (e.g., an outage, embargo, or sudden price delta) cascades across customer segments, regions, and feature surfaces. The map should surface operational triggers for procurement, recommended fallback model variants, and comms playbooks for Sales/CS.
deliverables: Market-thread mapping spreadsheet (region x vertical x latency-sensitivity x fallback cost impact)
Scoring rubric for substitute suitability (quality delta, token-cost delta, latency delta)
Prototype Datadog dashboard with trigger alerts and lead indicators
Runbook snippets for Solutions Eng + SRE + Sales to execute fallbacks and customer comms
Experiment plan to simulate cascades using synthetic workload and live A/B probes
acceptance_criteria: Map covers top 40 enterprise customers + 3 representative mid-market cohorts
Scoring rubric validated with 3 Solutions Eng field checks and one SRE latency study
Dashboard demonstrates an alert that would have surfaced the Nov 2024 simulated outage in test replay
Runbook contains step-by-step actions and message templates for customer outreach
tradeoffs_and_risks: Breadth vs depth: deeper instrumentation per-customer slows rollout but yields higher-fidelity signals
Synthetic workloads approximate but do not fully recreate customer prompt distributions
Recommendations may increase short-term cost (reserve burst capacity) to avoid customer impact
False positives in alerts could trigger unnecessary procurement moves; thresholds must be tuned
decisions_to_make: Prioritize which customer cohorts get live probes vs synthetic simulation
Default fallback ordering: quantized local model -> nearby region small model -> hosted alternative
Whether to expose a customer-facing severity indicator in Console vs internal-only ops channel
stakeholder_feedback: 2025-01-20 | SRE (Priya Kapoor) — validate latency thresholds; suggested adding KV cache hit-rate as a lead indicator
2025-01-22 | Solutions Eng (Marcus Liu) — requested per-tenant cost delta calc and example customer scripts
2025-01-28 | Sales Leadership (Nora Patel) — wants template legal-safe notifications and SLA talking points
2025-01-30 | Ethics & Trust (R. Gomez) — flagged any fallbacks that alter response content must be surfaced to customers
comments: 2025-01-12 | Ava Chen — Kickoff: scoped deliverables and candidate customers. Assigned Diego for execution. Asked ENG to reserve 2 dev days for dashboard ingestion schema.
2025-01-18 | Diego Morales — Collected initial telemetry export for 12 pilot tenants (latency, token counts, model variants). Noted significant tail latency in APAC for embeddings-heavy customers.
2025-01-20 | Priya Kapoor (SRE) — Ran latency replay for fallback scenarios; KV cache warmup reduces token cost but increases 95th percentile by ~30ms during first 2 seconds of spike.
2025-01-25 | Diego Morales — Prototype scoring rubric v0.1 submitted to GH: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-resilience/pull/137. Needs review from Product + Quant teams.
2025-01-28 | Marcus Liu (Solutions Eng) — Field validation scheduled with Customer A on 2025-02-03; will capture quality delta with existing prompt corpus.

2025-02-01 | Diego Morales — Dashboard ingestion pipeline PR merged: https://github.com/redwood-inference/telemetry/pull/402. Datadog prototype at https://app.datadoghq.com/dashboard/redwood/resilience-proto
2025-02-03 | Ava Chen — Reviewed runbook draft; requested adding explicit rollback steps and comms templates. Set milestone for runbook review 2025-02-10.

source.title: Interim usage quarantine and automated tenant-credit simulator for contested enterprise invoices
source.creator: Aisha Raman
source.description: Summary: Enterprise customers sometimes raise security questionnaire or contract questions that result in a temporary hold/escrow on invoices while usage is investigated. Current operations require manual ledger edits and provisional credits created by Finance, which is slow and error-prone for high-volume metered accounts. This ticket scopes a short-lived product/ops capability: (1) a usage quarantine flow that isolates suspect usage from final invoicing, and (2) an automated tenant-credit simulator (sandbox) that lets CS/Finance/PMs preview prorations, provisional credits, and final invoice outcomes before accepting/committing ledger changes. Goal: reduce mean time-to-resolution for contested usage, lower manual adjustment errors, and provide reproducible experiments for pricing/packaging A/Bs that touch credits/proration logic.
acceptance_criteria: A usage quarantine flag (quarantine_until, reason, source_ticket) can be set per-tenant and per-usage-window via internal API and console UI
Quarantined usage is excluded from invoice generation pipeline but is retained for audit and traceback

Tenant-credit simulator UI in Console where CS/Finance can load a tenant, toggle quarantine segments, and run simulated invoice to view provisional credits, proration math, tax implications, and final net amount
Simulator produces a signed JSON diff and an audit trail link that can be attached to Linear/GH PRs or finance journals
End-to-end test coverage for quarantine gating in the billing pipeline and for simulator correctness against three canonical dispute scenarios (overage, double-count, retro-usage correction)
Operational runbook created and distributed to CS/Finance with escalation steps and SLA for responses
Security review completed and HSM/KMS interactions (for customer invoice secrets) documented where applicable
notes: 2025-01-15 - Initial scoping sync: PM + Finance + CS. Identified top pain: manual provisional credits take 2-5 business days. Outcome: build quarantine + simulator as MVP to avoid immediate ledger edits.
2025-01-20 - ENG triage (ENG-8123): runway estimated 6 dev sprints for a fully integrated replacement of manual edits; we scoped MVP to API + Console simulator + pipeline exclusion hook (8 points).

2025-01-24 - Design feedback: UX must present clear 'temporary' labeling and require two approvals before applying simulated credits to ledger. Mock links: https://drive.redwood/designs/quarantine-sim-v1.png

2025-01-29 - Security note from Legal/Security: questionnaire-driven holds often require retention of raw request payloads for 90 days. Need legal signoff for storing verbatim request logs — added as dependency to security review.
2025-02-02 - PM update: agreed to put canary on 10 orgs for the simulator; Finance will sample 5 cases/week and compare simulator output vs current manual adjustments during pilot.
test_plan: Unit tests for quarantine flag and billing pipeline exclusion
Integration test: simulate tenant with quarantined segments and validate no invoice lines generated
Regression test: end-to-end for proration math against historical disputes dataset
A/B experiment: compare manual-adjust vs simulator-guided adjustments for accuracy and time-to-resolution over 6-week pilot
rollout_plan: Stage 1 (internal alpha): Console + API available to CS/Finance + PMs behind feature flag
Stage 2 (pilot): enable for 10 enterprise customers by invite; Finance does manual verification
Stage 3 (wider canary): open to 50 customers and integrate with auto-provisional-credit workflow (requires 2 approvals)
Stage 4 (general): enable for all enterprise orgs with audit logging and SLA enforcement
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Simulator mismatch with final ledger leading to customer disputes. Mitigation: require two approvers and keep manual adjustment path until pilot confidence metrics (error rate <1%)
Risk: Security/legal disallow storing raw request payloads. Mitigation: implement redaction pipeline and minimal replay tokens for simulator

Risk: Complexity added to billing pipeline may increase invoice generation latency. Mitigation: quarantine is applied as a pre-processing step and excluded segments are processed asynchronously for reconciliation
